participants.name: 화학공학
participants.professional_persona: 화학공학 박사학위를 받고 현재 부산대학교의 BK21 교육연구단에서 박사후연구원으로 재직 중입니다. 유기합성과 촉매반응을 전공하며 특히 친환경 공정 개발에 집중하고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 유기합성 및 촉매 설계 분야에서 3년간의 연구 경험을 보유하고 있으며, 고압 반응기와 NMR, GC-MS 분석에 능숙합니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 안정적인 연구 환경 속에서 차세대 친환경 촉매를 개발하여 국내 정밀화학 산업 경쟁력에 기여하고, 장기적으로는 대학 교수로 자리잡고 싶습니다.

participants.name: 한양대학교
participants.professional_persona: 한양대학교 기계공학과 교수로 재직 중이며, BK21 4단계 미래인재양성사업 교육연구단장을 맡고 있습니다. 20년 이상의 연구 경력을 바탕으로 차세대 공학 인재 양성에 헌신하고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 기계공학 분야 전문지식과 대형 연구과제 관리 경험, 대학원생 연구지도 및 평가 보고서 작성 능력.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 교육연구단의 중간평가에서 최고 등급을 받아 지원 예산을 확대하고, 연구단 참여 학생들의 취업률과 연구 성과를 획기적으로 높이는 것이 목표입니다.

participants.name: 서울
participants.professional_persona: 서울 소재 명문대학에서 화학공학을 전공한 석사과정생으로, 학부 시절부터 촉매 반응 연구에 관심이 많았습니다. 현재 BK21 교육연구단에 소속되어 차세대 친환경 촉매 개발 연구를 수행하며 월 70만 원의 연구장학금을 받고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 실험 설계 및 촉매 특성 분석에 능숙하며, 논문 작성과 연구 발표 경험이 풍부합니다. 또한 Python을 활용한 데이터 분석에도 관심이 많습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 박사 진학을 염두에 두고 있으며, 우수한 연구 성과를 바탕으로 국내 정유·화학 기업의 연구소에 입사하여 실용적인 촉매 기술을 개발하는 것이 목표입니다.

participants.name: 서울 (uud38w)
participants.entity_name: Education Research Group Participants and Graduate Innovation Practitioners | KG-Gen-uud38wlo
participants.representative_type: Education Research Group Participants and Graduate Innovation Practitioners | KG-Gen-uud38wlo
participants.professional_persona: 서울 소재 명문대학에서 기계공학 박사 과정 중이며, 4단계 BK21 사업의 교육연구단에 참여하여 스마트 제조 관련 연구를 수행하고 있습니다. 매달 130만 원의 연구장학금을 받으며 연구에 집중하고 있으며, 대학원생 인권 보호 온라인 교육을 이수하였습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: CAD/CAM, 3D 프린팅, 공정 시뮬레이션에 능숙하며, 스마트팩토리 관련 프로젝트 경험이 있습니다. 또한 연구 성과를 국제 학술지에 발표한 이력이 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 박사 학위 취득 후 국내 대학의 교수로 임용되어 후속 세대를 양성하고, 산학협력을 통해 실용적인 제조 기술을 개발하는 것이 목표입니다.

participants.name: 한양대학교 (byupv8)
participants.professional_persona: 한양대학교 화학공학과 부교수로, 혁신인재양성사업 교육연구팀을 이끌고 있습니다. 신산업 분야 맞춤형 교육과정 개발에 주력하고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 화학공정 설계 및 최적화, 산학협력 프로젝트 기획, 연구비 집행 및 정산 노하우.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 교육연구팀의 자체평가 보고서를 철저히 준비하여 연차평가에서 우수한 성적을 거두고, 팀원들의 연구 장학금을 안정적으로 지원하고자 합니다.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached a strong consensus that the total BK21 funding for Hanyang University in 2022 is 41.0597 billion KRW, with graduate stipends (master's 700K, doctoral 1.3M, doctoral candidate 1M KRW/month) and postdoc minimum salary (3M KRW/month) fixed by policy. They agreed that operating costs are capped at 10% of the project budget, leaving the remainder for personnel, and that Hanyang has approximately 3-4 engineering education research groups each with a budget of about 826 million KRW. All participants converged on estimates of 180 graduate scholarship slots and 15 postdoc positions, with no disagreement on numeric values or reasoning. The key causal mechanism discussed was that the fixed stipend/salary levels and operating cost cap directly constrain the number of supported slots, and the number of eligible research groups limits the total capacity.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group focused on the operational execution of the BK21 program, emphasizing the disbursement of funds in two installments (70% in March, 30% in September) and the structured monitoring and support mechanisms. There was unanimous consensus on all numeric predictions: total funds disbursed at 41,059,700,000 KRW, evaluation panels convened at 3, consulting sessions held at 280, and compliance checks executed at 205. No disagreements were present, with all participants citing the same policy documents and direct experience. The central tendency and range for each variable were identical across all estimates.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group consistently estimated outputs based on a fixed input of 180 graduate students and 15 postdocs, using per-capita productivity rates of 3 publications per graduate student and 5 per postdoc, yielding 540 total publications. International conference presentations were uniformly estimated at half of publications, or 270. All participants agreed on these numbers, citing personal group experience and high compliance rates (575 of 578 groups reporting) as evidence. No disagreements emerged, and the central tendency was a unanimous 540 publications and 270 international presentations.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus on all outcome estimates, predicting an international research collaboration rate of 270 (50% of 540 publications), a graduate employment rate of 160 (88.9% of 180 graduates), and a full-time faculty lecture ratio of 85%. Key causal mechanisms included program incentives for international co-authorship and conference support, the reputation and training quality boosting employment, and BK21's emphasis on full-time faculty for graduate courses. Evaluation and consulting feedback loops were seen as sustaining these outcomes. No disagreements emerged, with all participants providing identical numeric predictions.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group consistently linked sustained BK21 investment over a decade to building a critical mass of highly skilled researchers, which in turn drives national innovation and improves global S&T talent competitiveness. They emphasized that the program's focus on international collaboration, full-time faculty mentoring, and research integrity fosters high-impact publications, and that alumni disseminating best practices further raises research standards. There was complete consensus on both estimates: all participants predicted a national S&T talent competitiveness rank of 8 and a high-impact publication rate of 12.5 per 1000 researchers. This unanimity was reinforced by independent calculations from group-level data (e.g., 180 publications, 18 top 1% cited, 60 researchers) scaled to approximately 400,000 national researchers.

participants.name: 한양대학교 (jduobt)
participants.professional_persona: 한양대학교 전자공학부 조교수로서 미래인재양성사업 교육연구단의 연구교수로 활동 중입니다. 3년 전 미국에서 박사 후 연구를 마치고 귀국했습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 반도체 소자 및 집적회로 설계, 연구 성과 분석 및 컨설팅 보고서 작성 경험.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 교육연구단의 국제화 역량을 높이고, 참여 대학원생들의 우수 논문 게재를 지원하여 연구단 평가 지표를 개선하는 것이 목표입니다.

participants.name: 부산에서
participants.professional_persona: 부산에서 태어나 자란 전기전자공학 박사과정생으로, 무선통신 시스템 최적화 연구에 매진하고 있습니다. BK21 혁신인재양성사업의 지원을 받아 월 130만 원의 장학금으로 연구에 집중할 수 있게 되었습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: MATLAB과 C++ 기반 시뮬레이션에 강점이 있으며, 딥러닝을 통한 채널 추정 알고리즘 개발 경험이 있습니다. 국제 학술지에 2편의 논문을 게재했습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 통신 분야 글로벌 기업이나 국책연구소에서 연구원으로 근무하며 6G 핵심 기술 개발에 기여하는 것이 꿈입니다. 향후 Post-Doc을 통해 연구 역량을 더 쌓고 싶습니다.

participants.representative_type: Education Research Group Participants and Graduate Innovation Practitioners | KG-Gen-j6xuhc43
participants.professional_persona: 부산 지역 사립대학에서 화학공학 석사 과정에 재학 중이며, BK21 혁신인재양성사업단에 소속되어 에너지 소재 연구를 진행하고 있습니다. 월 70만 원의 장학금으로 생활하며, 대학원 혁신을 위한 온라인 간담회에 적극 참여하고 있습니다.

participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 석사 졸업 후 국책 연구소에 취업하여 차세대 이차전지 기술 개발에 기여하고, 장기적으로는 에너지 분야 전문가로 성장하고자 합니다.

participants.name: 서울대학교
participants.professional_persona: 서울대학교 사회학과에서 박사학위를 받고 현재 동 대학 BK21 교육연구단에서 신진연구자로 활동 중입니다. 디지털 전환과 사회적 불평등에 관한 연구를 수행하고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 대규모 설문조사 설계 및 통계 분석(SPSS, Stata)에 능숙하며, 질적 연구 방법론과 혼합연구법에도 강점이 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: BK21 지원을 통해 데이터 기반 사회정책 연구를 심화하여, 디지털 격차 해소에 기여하는 연구자로 성장하고 싶습니다.

participants.name: 전남대학교
participants.professional_persona: 전남대학교 기계공학과에서 박사학위를 받고 현재 광주과학기술원 BK21 교육연구단에서 박사후연구원으로 근무합니다. 지능형 로봇과 자율주행 시스템을 연구하고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: ROS 기반 로봇 소프트웨어 개발, 센서 퓨전, 딥러닝 기반 영상인식 기술을 보유하고 있으며, 실험실에서 실제 로봇 플랫폼을 운용한 경험이 풍부합니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 자율주행과 서비스 로봇 분야의 실용 기술을 고도화하여, 지역 산업체와 협력해 현장에 바로 적용할 수 있는 솔루션을 만들고 싶습니다.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group focused on how the national BK21 budget of 408,080 million KRW is distributed across 577 education research groups, with Hanyang University hosting about 15-20 groups. There was consensus that a typical engineering group receives around 1.2 billion KRW, supporting roughly 180 graduate scholarship slots (based on an average monthly stipend of 1 million KRW) and about 15 postdoc positions (at 3 million KRW per month). A major disagreement emerged: one participant estimated Hanyang's total at 19,000 slots and 1,200 postdocs, which others argued conflated national totals with Hanyang's share. The central tendency for Hanyang-specific estimates was 12 billion KRW total funding, 180 graduate slots, and 15 postdoc positions.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed the disbursement of funds in two installments (70% in March, 30% in September) and the centralized evaluation process for Hanyang University's BK21 program. There was consensus that total funds disbursed would be 12 billion KRW and that three evaluation panels would be convened annually. Disagreement emerged on consulting sessions and compliance checks: some participants estimated national totals (280 sessions, 205 checks) while others argued for Hanyang-specific estimates (15 sessions, 20 checks). The range of predictions was 12 billion KRW for funds, 3 for panels, 15-280 for consulting sessions, and 20-205 for compliance checks.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed how BK21 outputs—graduate students and postdocs supported, publications, and international conference presentations—are driven by group size (180 graduate slots, 15 postdocs) and per-participant productivity, with engineering groups typically producing 2-3 publications per graduate and 4-5 per postdoc, averaging 3 per participant. There was consensus on the number of supported participants (195 total) and that about half attend at least one international conference annually. Disagreement centered on total publications: most estimated 540 (adjusting for less productive members and reporting compliance), while one participant argued for 585 based on higher engineering output. Predictions ranged from 540 to 585 for publications and 90 to 98 for conference presentations, with a central tendency around 540 and 90 respectively.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group focused on how BK21 program outputs (publications, conference presentations) and support structures (funding, faculty composition, evaluation feedback) drive graduate employment and international collaboration outcomes. There was strong consensus that the graduate employment rate is 88.9% (with a count of 160 out of 180 graduates) and the international collaboration rate is 50%, based on observed industry demand, program incentives like travel funds, and the prevalence of tenure-track faculty teaching 75% of graduate lecture hours. No disagreements emerged, as all participants aligned on these estimates, though some noted slight field-specific variations in employment rates. The numeric predictions were uniform across all participants: employment rate 88.9%, international collaboration rate 50%, full-time faculty lecture ratio 75%, with the count variables consistently at 160 and 90.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group consistently predicted that sustained BK21 investment over a decade would gradually improve South Korea's S&T talent competitiveness rank from around 10-12 to 8, and increase high-impact publications per 1000 researchers modestly to about 12.5. Key causal mechanisms included building a critical mass of skilled alumni, fostering research integrity and interdisciplinary collaboration, and disseminating best practices. There was strong consensus on both the numeric estimates and the reasoning that systemic changes take time, with the 2021-2022 window capturing early-stage impacts only. No disagreements were expressed; all participants aligned on the same predicted values and underlying logic.

participants.name: 경상북도
participants.professional_persona: 경상북도 소재 지방국립대학교에서 식물분자생리학을 연구하는 박사수료생입니다. 농업 생명공학 전공으로, BK21 사업단을 통해 작물 스트레스 내성 유전자 기능 연구를 수행하며 월 100만 원의 장학금을 지원받고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 유전자 클로닝, CRISPR 유전자 편집, 형질전환체 제작 및 표현형 분석에 숙련되어 있습니다. 현장 실험과 실험실 작업을 병행하는 데 강점이 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 기후 변화에 대응하는 고부가가치 작물 개발에 이바지하고자 농촌진흥청 또는 종자 기업 연구소에 취업하는 것을 희망합니다. 지역 농업 발전에 기여하는 것도 중요하게 생각합니다.

participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 박사 후에는 해외 선진 연구실에서 포스트닥 연수를 거쳐, 귀국 후 제약·바이오 기업에서 신약 타겟 발굴 연구를 이끌고 싶습니다.

participants.name: 연세대학교
participants.professional_persona: 연세대학교 생명과학부에서 박사학위를 마치고, 현재 BK21 교육연구단의 신진연구자로 세포 노화 및 재생 의학 연구를 진행하고 있습니다. 줄기세포 분화 조절 기전을 규명하는 데 주력하고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 세포 배양, 유전자 편집(CRISPR), 단백질 분석, 형광 이미징 등 분자생물학 실험 기술에 능숙하며, 생물정보학(Bioinformatics) 분석도 가능합니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: BK21 지원을 바탕으로 노화 관련 질환의 새로운 치료 표적을 발굴하고, 해외 우수 연구실과의 공동 연구를 통해 글로벌 연구자로 성장하는 것이 목표입니다.

participants.name: 한양대학교 (jj3mgw)
participants.professional_persona: 한양대학교 국어국문학과 교수로 인문사회계열 교육연구단장을 맡고 있습니다. 인문학 위기 속에서도 BK21 사업을 통해 우수 인재 양성에 힘쓰고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 한국문학 및 문화콘텐츠 연구, 대학원 교육과정 혁신, 자체평가보고서 작성 및 실적 관리.

participants.name: 서울 (uify15)
participants.professional_persona: 서울 소재 대학에서 경제학을 전공한 석사과정생으로, 노동경제학과 인적자본 형성에 관심이 많습니다. BK21 교육연구단의 인문사회계열 지원을 받아 학비와 생활비 부담을 덜고 연구에 전념하고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: STATA와 R을 활용한 계량경제 분석에 능하며, 대규모 패널 데이터 처리 및 정책 효과 분석 방법론에 정통합니다. 학부 때부터 연구 보조원으로 활동했습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 한국노동연구원이나 KDI 같은 국책연구기관에서 연구원으로 근무하며, 실효성 있는 고용·복지 정책을 설계하는 데 기여하는 것이 목표입니다. 박사 진학도 고려 중입니다.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group discussed how the total funding allocated to Hanyang University's BK21 program (355,151 million KRW) is constrained by operating cost limits (10% of budget) and stipend levels (700K–1.3M KRW/month for graduate students, 3M KRW/month minimum for postdocs). There was consensus that the total funding figure is correct and that graduate scholarship slots and postdoc positions are limited by these costs. Disagreement emerged on the exact numbers: some estimated 200 graduate slots and 30 postdocs, while others argued for 180 slots and 25 postdocs, citing tighter budget constraints from stipend levels and operating costs. The range for graduate scholarship slots was 180–200, and for postdoc positions, 25–30, with central tendencies around 180 and 25, respectively.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached full consensus on all activity-level estimates, with every participant predicting the same values: total funds disbursed at 355,151 million KRW, 3 evaluation panels convened, 280 consulting sessions held, and 205 compliance checks executed. The central causal mechanism was that the policy's fixed budget allocation (70% March, 30% September) and stated counts for consulting (280 groups) and compliance checks (205 innovative talent training groups) directly determine these activity outputs. All participants agreed that the three evaluation streams—annual education research group evaluation, graduate innovation evaluation, and compliance oversight—each require a formal panel meeting, totaling three. No disagreement emerged, as all estimates were identical and grounded in the same policy evidence.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed how inputs (graduate and postdoc slots) and field-specific publication rates drive outputs, with a consensus that budget constraints limit supported participants to around 180 graduate students and 25 postdocs. There was broad agreement on average publication output of about 1.8 per participant (total ~360) and international conference presentations at 30% participation (total ~108), reflecting lower productivity in humanities and longer experimental cycles in STEM. Disagreement centered on the education research group head's higher estimate of 470 publications and 141 presentations, which other participants considered too optimistic for a humanities-heavy group. The central tendency for publications ranged from 360 to 470, with most converging on 360, and for presentations from 108 to 141, with most converging on 108.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus on all outcome estimates, with employment rate at 82.5%, international collaboration rate at 30%, and full-time faculty lecture ratio at 75%, yielding 160 graduate employment and 85 international collaboration counts. Key causal mechanisms included strong industry ties and training reputation driving employment, conference funding and incentives supporting collaboration, and increased full-time faculty improving mentoring quality. Participants consistently noted that evaluation and consulting feedback loops (280 sessions, 205 checks) help groups adjust strategies, but field mix (lower humanities rates) and job placement timelines constrain higher outcomes. No disagreements emerged, with all estimates aligning across STEM and humanities perspectives.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached a strong consensus that the impact of the BK21 program on national-level indicators in 2022 would be modest and incremental, not transformative. All participants estimated South Korea's global S&T talent competitiveness rank at 8 and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers at 12.5, reflecting a shared view that sustained investment builds a critical mass of skilled graduates but that a single year's effect from one university's program is limited. The primary causal mechanism discussed was that the program's focus on graduate training, research integrity, and collaboration—supported by consulting sessions and compliance checks—gradually improves research culture and output quality, though systemic changes take longer to manifest at the national level. Minor differences in reasoning emerged based on disciplinary perspective: participants from STEM fields suggested that higher citation rates in bioscience could lead to slightly faster rank improvement, while those from humanities emphasized slower progress due to lower citation rates in their fields. Overall, the group agreed that the 2022 window captures early-stage impacts, not full decade outcomes, and that the program's dissemination of best practices via the BK21 webzine and forums supports a gradual increase in high-impact publications.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all three input variables, with every participant estimating 410,597 million KRW for total funding, 19,000 graduate scholarship slots, and 1,200 postdoc positions. They consistently cited the policy context's explicit total budget figure and the national student counts of 12,600 (future talent) and 6,400 (innovation talent) as the basis for their proportional allocation to Hanyang University. No disagreements emerged, as all participants treated the policy numbers as ground truth and applied the same proportional reasoning based on Hanyang's share of 577 education research groups. The central tendency and range were identical across all estimates, reflecting unanimous alignment on the inputs.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached full consensus that the activities phase is the operational engine of the BK21 program, with all participants estimating total funds disbursed at 410,597 million KRW, evaluation panels convened at 3, consulting sessions held at 280, and compliance checks executed at 205. Key causal mechanisms included the two-installment payment schedule (70% in March, 30% in September), voluntary consulting for 280 of 578 groups, and targeted compliance checks on 205 innovative talent training groups. No disagreements emerged; all estimates were identical, reflecting strong alignment on policy implementation. The central tendency for all four variables was a single value: 410,597,000,000 KRW for funds, 3 panels, 280 consulting sessions, and 205 compliance checks.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed that outputs directly result from funded graduate and postdoc positions, with estimates of 19,000 graduate students and 1,200 postdocs supported. There was consensus on these input numbers, but disagreement on per-capita productivity: one participant estimated 2.5 publications per participant (57,000 total) and 0.5 international presentations (9,500 total), while the majority estimated 2 publications (47,500 total) and 0.4 presentations (7,600 total), citing early-stage students, budget constraints, and travel restrictions as limiting factors. The central tendency for publications was around 47,500–57,000, and for international presentations around 7,600–9,500.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus on all outcome estimates, predicting an employment rate of 84.2%, an international collaboration rate of 20% (3,800 participants), and a full-time faculty lecture ratio of 85%. They reasoned that the program's baseline targets and declining trends, along with budget and travel constraints, justified these conservative figures. The evaluation and consulting feedback loops were seen as gradually improving performance but not yet fully realized in 2022. No disagreements emerged, with all estimates identical across participants.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that sustained BK21 investment over a decade creates a critical mass of trained researchers, gradually improving national S&T talent competitiveness and high-impact publications, though systemic changes in research culture take time to materialize. There was strong consensus that South Korea's global S&T talent competitiveness rank would improve modestly from around 10-12 to 9, and that high-impact publications per 1000 researchers would be about 11.2, based on the reasoning that only a small fraction (about 0.5%) of publications reach top 1% citation impact and national averages include less productive non-BK21 researchers. One participant estimated a more optimistic rank of 8 and high-impact publications of 12.5, citing the program's scale and dissemination of best practices, while the majority considered this too optimistic given the early stage of impact and their direct lab experience. The range of predictions was 8-9 for rank and 11.2-12.5 for high-impact publications, with a central tendency of 9 and 11.2 respectively.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group consistently identified the total funding allocated to Hanyang University's BK21 program as 410,597 million KRW, based on the policy context. They estimated graduate scholarship slots at 19,000 and postdoc positions at 500, using the same reasoning: scaling from their own education research group's experience (about 30 students and 2-3 postdocs) to the university level, while considering stipend levels (700,000–1,300,000 KRW per month) and postdoc minimum salary (3,000,000 KRW per month). There was full consensus on all three variables, with no disagreement in estimates or reasoning.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached full consensus on all four activity-level variables, with every participant predicting the same numeric values. The total funds disbursed was uniformly set at 408,080,000,000 KRW, based on the policy's stated annual budget and the 70% March / 30% September payment schedule. Evaluation panels convened were consistently estimated at 3, representing annual, mid-term, and compliance meetings. Consulting sessions held were pegged at 280, reflecting the historical number of groups that requested consulting, and compliance checks executed were fixed at 205, targeting the innovative talent training groups as specified in the policy context. No disagreements or alternative estimates emerged across the stakeholder posts.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed two main disciplinary perspectives on output rates: STEM fields (computer science) estimated 3 publications and 0.5 international conference presentations per graduate student, while humanities/social sciences estimated 2 publications and 0.25 presentations per student. There was consensus on the upstream estimates of 19,000 graduate slots and 500 postdoc positions, and that postdoc output is captured within per-student rates. Disagreement centered on per-capita output rates, with STEM participants arguing for higher rates due to disciplinary norms. Numeric predictions ranged from 38,000 to 57,000 publications and 4,750 to 9,500 international conference presentations, with a central tendency around 38,000 publications and 4,750 presentations from the humanities/social sciences majority.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus on all outcome estimates, predicting a graduate employment rate of 85% (16,150 of 19,000 graduates) and an international collaboration rate of 15% (2,850 participants), with a full-time faculty lecture ratio of 80%. These predictions were consistently grounded in the policy's target of a 0.3% annual increase from an 86.7% baseline, adjusted for a noted declining trend, and supported by the program's emphasis on full-time faculty mentoring, evaluation feedback loops, and prior-phase outputs of 38,000 publications and 4,750 international conference presentations. No disagreements emerged; all participants aligned on the same numeric values and causal reasoning, citing similar evidence from the policy context and their direct experience in education research groups.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that sustained BK21 investment in graduate talent development gradually improves national S&T competitiveness and high-impact publications, but effects are incremental and diluted at the national level. There was consensus that South Korea's S&T talent competitiveness rank remains around 10th, and that the national high-impact publication rate is much lower than the BK21 cohort rate due to the program's small share of total researchers. Disagreement centered on the high-impact publication rate: one education research group head estimated 12.5 per 1000 researchers, while others argued for a more conservative 8.2 per 1000, citing the national dilution effect. Numeric predictions ranged from 8 to 10 for rank (central tendency 10) and from 8.2 to 12.5 for publications per 1000 researchers (central tendency 8.2).

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group consistently used the national BK21 budget of 408,080 million KRW as the total funding allocated, and derived graduate scholarship slots from the stated 19,000 participating students (12,600 future talent + 6,400 innovation talent). Postdoc positions were estimated at 300 nationally, based on the 3 million KRW minimum salary and limited group budgets. All participants agreed on these national-level estimates, but a minor disagreement emerged over Hanyang University's proportional share: regional participants were cautious about overestimating Hanyang's allocation, while Hanyang representatives argued for a higher share due to its status as a top-tier university. The central tendency for all three variables was unanimous: total funding 408,080,000,000 KRW, scholarship slots 19,000, and postdoc positions 300.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached full consensus on all four activity-level variables, estimating total funds disbursed at 408,080,000,000 KRW, evaluation panels convened at 3, consulting sessions held at 280, and compliance checks executed at 205. All participants reasoned that the full annual budget would be disbursed via the scheduled 70% and 30% installments, that three formal panel meetings (annual, mid-term, compliance) would occur despite the mid-term being planned for 2023, and that the policy-stated numbers for consulting requests (280 out of 578 groups) and compliance checks (205 innovative talent training groups) would be realized. No disagreements emerged, as each participant independently arrived at identical estimates and supporting evidence from the policy context.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached full consensus on all output estimates, with the reasoning anchored in the policy context's fixed national totals of 19,000 graduate students and 300 postdocs. Participants consistently applied a productivity rate of 2 publications per graduate student and 0.4 international conference presentations per student, yielding 38,000 publications and 7,600 presentations. The causal mechanism linked these outputs directly to the inputs and activities phases, assuming full fund disbursement and high compliance. No disagreements emerged, and all numeric predictions were identical across participants.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus that the program's full budget disbursement, high compliance (280 consulting sessions, 205 compliance checks), and outputs (19,000 students, 38,000 publications, 7,600 international conference presentations) directly drive outcomes. All participants estimated the international collaboration rate at 40% (count 7,600), employment rate at 85% (count 16,150), and full-time faculty lecture ratio at 75%, reasoning that evaluation feedback loops and internationalization incentives support these values. No disagreements emerged; the central tendency across all numeric predictions was identical, with ranges of 40% for collaboration rate, 85% for employment rate, and 75% for lecture ratio.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that sustained BK21 investment in graduate talent development (19,000 students, 300 postdocs, 38,000 publications) gradually enhances national innovation capacity through alumni career progression, research culture improvements, and dissemination of best practices, but global competitiveness rankings and high-impact publication rates improve slowly. There was consensus that the 2021-2022 window is too early for dramatic shifts, with most participants agreeing on a national S&T talent competitiveness rank of 14 and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers of 12, reflecting cautious regional perspectives. Disagreement centered on the pace of improvement: one participant from a top-tier university estimated a rank of 12 and 15 publications per 1000 researchers, while another regional postdoc estimated 13 and 13.5, citing slower adoption of best practices in regional institutions. Prediction ranges were: national rank 12-14 (central tendency 14) and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers 12-15 (central tendency 12).

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached a strong consensus on all three input variables, with every participant estimating total funding allocated at 410,597,000,000 KRW, graduate scholarship slots at 19,000, and postdoc positions at 1,200. The reasoning was uniform: participants derived total funding from the policy context's stated total budget for 577 education research groups and 20 graduate innovation support universities, then applied proportional allocation to Hanyang University. Scholarship slots were calculated from monthly stipend levels (700,000–1,300,000 KRW) and the total budget for graduate support, while postdoc positions were constrained by a minimum salary of 3,000,000 KRW per month and the limited budget for early-career researchers. No disagreements emerged, as all participants referenced the same policy documents and proportional allocation logic. The central tendency and range are identical across all predictions, indicating complete alignment.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached full consensus on all four activity-level variables, with every participant estimating the same values based directly on explicit policy figures. Total funds disbursed was uniformly set at 408,080,000,000 KRW, reflecting the stated annual budget and two-installment schedule. Evaluation panels convened were consistently estimated at 3, corresponding to annual, mid-term, and compliance meetings. Consulting sessions held were unanimously 280, matching the number of groups that requested consulting. Compliance checks executed were all 205, aligning with the number of innovative talent training groups subject to checks. No disagreements or alternative reasoning emerged; all participants anchored their estimates on the same policy documents and operational experience.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached strong consensus on all output estimates, with every participant predicting 19,000 graduate students supported, 1,200 postdoc researchers supported, 57,000 publications produced, and 9,500 international conference presentations. The central causal mechanism was that efficient fund disbursement and compliance checks enable active research, with each participant averaging about 3 publications per year (accounting for early-stage students) and about half attending at least one international conference. No disagreements emerged; all participants aligned their reasoning on these conservative estimates based on direct program experience. The numeric predictions were identical across all participants, indicating unanimous agreement.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group consistently linked high employment and international collaboration rates to the program's strong research environment, supported by 19,000 graduate students, 1,200 postdocs, and 57,000 publications, along with practical training, network effects, and conference funding. There was full consensus on all numeric estimates, with employment rate at 89.5% (17,000 out of 19,000 graduates), international collaboration rate at 47% (9,500 participants), and full-time faculty lecture ratio at 85%. No disagreements emerged; participants reinforced each other's reasoning, citing evaluation and consulting feedback loops as mechanisms for continuous improvement.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus on both estimates, predicting South Korea's global S&T talent competitiveness rank at 8th and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers at 12.5. Key causal mechanisms included sustained investment in graduate talent development over a decade building a critical mass of skilled researchers, high employment (89.5%) and international collaboration rates (47%) indicating strong talent pipelines and global networking, and dissemination of best practices raising the overall S&T ecosystem. No disagreements were expressed; all participants aligned on the same numeric values and reasoning, with estimates derived from program outputs (57,000 publications from 20,200 participants) adjusted for national scale and the 2021-2022 window.

participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: BK21 사업을 통해 세계적 수준의 기계공학 인재를 양성하고, 연구단의 글로벌 경쟁력을 한 단계 더 높이는 것을 목표로 합니다.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group focused on proportional allocation of national BK21 resources to Hanyang University based on its share of education research groups (approximately 20-25 out of 577, or about 4%). There was strong consensus on all three input variables, with most participants estimating total funding at 16,322 million KRW, graduate scholarship slots at 800, and postdoc positions at 40. One participant initially proposed a higher total funding of 20,000 million KRW but later aligned with the majority. The central tendency for total funding was 16,322 million KRW (range: 16,322–20,000 million KRW), for scholarship slots 800 (range: 800–800), and for postdoc positions 40 (range: 40–40).
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached strong consensus that total funds disbursed to Hanyang University would match the allocated 16,322 million KRW, based on the standard 70/30 payment schedule. All participants agreed that three evaluation panels would be convened (annual, mid-term, compliance). A key point of disagreement emerged: one participant mistakenly used national totals (280 consulting sessions, 205 compliance checks) as Hanyang-specific estimates, while the majority correctly applied proportional reasoning (Hanyang's ~4% share) to estimate 12 consulting sessions and 8 compliance checks. The central tendency for consulting sessions and compliance checks was 12 and 8, respectively, with the outlier at 280 and 205.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed how BK21 support for 800 graduate students and 40 postdocs translates into research outputs, with productivity varying by discipline. There was consensus that graduate students produce 1-3 publications per year and postdocs 2-5, and that about 20% of students and 40% of postdocs present at international conferences. Disagreement centered on the average publication rate per capita, with estimates ranging from 1.5 to 2.5 for students and 2.5 to 5.0 for postdocs, leading to total publication estimates between 1600 and 2400 and international presentations between 320 and 480. The central tendency for publications was around 2000 and for presentations around 400, reflecting a mix of STEM and humanities fields at Hanyang University.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus on all outcome estimates, predicting a graduate employment rate of 90% (720 out of 800 graduates) and an international research collaboration rate of 40% (320 participants), with a full-time faculty lecture ratio of 85%. Key causal mechanisms included BK21's reputation and training networks boosting employment, conference support and collaboration incentives driving international engagement, and program design emphasizing full-time faculty for quality mentoring. Participants consistently noted that humanities fields have slightly lower employment and collaboration rates than STEM, and that evaluation and consulting feedback loops (12 sessions, 8 compliance checks) help identify weaknesses and share best practices. No disagreement was expressed; all estimates and reasoning aligned across stakeholders.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that sustained BK21 investment over a decade would improve South Korea's global S&T talent competitiveness rank from around 10th to 8th, and yield a high-impact publication rate of 12.5 per 1000 researchers. Key causal mechanisms included graduate talent development, dissemination of best practices via webzine and forums, and a strengthened research culture with mandatory ethics education. All participants estimated the same values (rank=8, rate=12.5), with no disagreement. The central tendency and range were identical across all predictions.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group discussed how the total BK21 budget for Hanyang University in 2022 is 410.597 billion KRW, but operating costs capped at 10% and funds for research activities reduce the amount available for stipends and salaries. There was consensus that graduate scholarship slots range from 1,100 to 1,200 and postdoc positions from 130 to 150, with a central tendency around 1,100-1,150 slots and 130-140 postdocs. Disagreement centered on whether the higher estimates (1,200 slots, 150 postdocs) ignored overhead and research costs, while lower estimates (1,100 slots, 130 postdocs) accounted for these constraints. The group also noted that Hanyang's large STEM focus might allow more slots than the national average, but not all funds go directly to personnel.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached full consensus on all activity-level estimates, with every participant predicting the same values: total funds disbursed at 410.597 billion KRW, 3 evaluation panels convened, 280 consulting sessions held, and 205 compliance checks executed. Key causal mechanisms discussed included the two-installment disbursement schedule (70% in March, 30% in September) ensuring early-year funding for research and stipends, and the linkage between evaluation outcomes and budget adjustments. No disagreements emerged, as all participants anchored their reasoning directly on the policy context and their practical experience with fund flow, evaluation cycles, and compliance procedures.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed how BK21 outputs, including graduate student and postdoc support, publications, and international conference presentations, are driven by budget allocations and participant productivity. There was consensus that graduate students produce 2-4 publications annually and postdocs 3-5, with about 50% of participants attending one international conference per year, and that high compliance (575 of 578 groups submitting reports) ensures reliable tracking. Disagreement centered on the total number of supported participants (1,100 vs. 1,130 graduate students; 130 vs. 136 postdocs) and the average publication rate per participant (1.5 vs. 3), reflecting field differences between STEM and humanities. Predictions for graduate students supported ranged from 1,100 to 1,130 (central tendency ~1,115), postdocs from 130 to 136 (~133), publications from 2,200 to 3,390 (~3,100), and international conference presentations from 330 to 565 (~500).
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group consistently estimated a graduate employment rate of 75% and an international collaboration rate of 25%, with a full-time faculty lecture ratio of 85%. They agreed that these rates reflect a program-wide average, with STEM fields performing better than humanities due to stronger industry links and international networks. The evaluation and consulting feedback loops were seen as mechanisms that reallocate funds to improve outcomes, though they disproportionately benefit STEM groups. No significant disagreement emerged, as all participants aligned on the same numeric estimates and reasoning, emphasizing field diversity as a key factor moderating overall rates.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that sustained BK21 investment over a decade yields only modest national rank improvement due to global competition, estimating South Korea's S&T talent competitiveness rank at 12 (from ~15). They agreed high-impact publications per 1000 researchers would rise to 8.5 (from ~7), driven by systemic cultural shifts toward research integrity, collaboration, and interdisciplinary work, plus dissemination of best practices. All participants aligned on these numeric predictions and causal mechanisms, with no disagreement; some noted gains are uneven, with STEM fields leading improvement, but the overall national trend is positive.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus that the total funding allocated to Hanyang University's BK21 program is 410,597 million KRW, based on the policy context. They estimated graduate scholarship slots by scaling the national education research group budget (355,151 million KRW) to Hanyang's share of groups (12 out of 577), accounting for 10% operating costs, yielding about 250 slots. Postdoc positions were estimated by allocating 10% of the budget to postdoc salaries (3M KRW/month), scaling to Hanyang's share, and adjusting for concentration in top groups, resulting in about 40 positions. All participants agreed on these estimates, with no disagreement. The central tendency for all three variables was identical across participants: total funding at 410,597,000,000 KRW, graduate scholarship slots at 250, and postdoc positions at 40.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group unanimously agreed on all four activity estimates, citing the policy context as ground truth. Total funds disbursed was set at 410,597 million KRW, based on the stated budget and 70%/30% installment schedule. Evaluation panels convened were estimated at 3, corresponding to annual, mid-term, and compliance meetings. Consulting sessions held were pegged at 280, matching the number of groups that requested consulting, and compliance checks executed at 205, targeting the innovative talent training groups. No disagreements emerged, as all participants relied on the same policy documents and reasoning.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group focused on translating the prior phase's resource estimates (250 graduate slots, 40 postdoc positions) into concrete outputs, using per-capita productivity rates and accounting for co-authorship overlap. There was strong consensus on the participant counts and the reasoning that average publication productivity is 2.5 per graduate student and 3.5 per postdoc, with international conference participation rates of 60% for students and 80% for postdocs. A key disagreement emerged on the final totals: one participant estimated 750 publications and 200 conference presentations, while the majority converged on 625 publications and 175 presentations, citing that not all groups are equally productive and some students are in early stages. The numeric predictions for publications ranged from 625 to 750, and for conference presentations from 175 to 200, with the central tendency at 625 and 175 respectively.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group consistently linked outcomes to prior outputs, using 175 international conference presentations as a proxy for 175 participants involved in international collaboration, yielding an international collaboration rate of 70%. They estimated a graduate employment rate of 190, derived from a 76% employment rate applied to 250 graduate slots, reflecting a cautious adjustment from the policy target due to early program stage and declining trends. The full-time faculty lecture ratio was uniformly set at 85%, based on observed high full-time faculty involvement. All numeric predictions were identical across participants, indicating full consensus on estimates and reasoning, with no disagreement.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that the BK21 program's impact on national S&T talent competitiveness and high-impact publication rates is gradual and modest due to the program's scale relative to the national researcher workforce (580,000 FTE) and the slow pace of global competitiveness shifts. There was consensus that the program's systemic changes (integrity training, collaboration incentives, evaluation feedback) will improve outcomes, but effects take time. Disagreement centered on the degree of improvement: one participant estimated a rank of 8 and 12.5 publications per 1000 researchers, while the majority estimated a rank of 9 and 11.2 publications, citing the early stage of the program and limited direct contribution. The range for national S&T talent competitiveness rank was 8–9, with a central tendency of 9; for high-impact publications per 1000 researchers, the range was 11.2–12.5, with a central tendency of 11.2.

phase_summaries.Inputs: Participants consistently estimated total funding allocated to Hanyang University's BK21 program at 355,151 million KRW, graduate scholarship slots at 19,000, and postdoc positions at 1,000, with no disagreement. They reasoned that the direct project budget for education research groups (355,151 million KRW) represents the relevant funding, and derived scholarship slots from national totals (12,600 future talent + 6,400 innovative talent) and postdoc positions from budget and salary assumptions (3 million KRW/month, 36 million KRW/year). All estimates were grounded in policy documents and personal experience, reflecting a strong consensus on input values.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached strong consensus on all four activity-level variables, with every participant predicting identical values: total funds disbursed at 355,151 million KRW (the direct project budget), 3 evaluation panels convened (annual, mid-term, compliance), 280 consulting sessions held (matching the number of groups that requested consulting), and 205 compliance checks executed (targeting innovative talent training groups). The shared reasoning centered on direct policy citations—the two-installment payment schedule, mandated panel meetings, voluntary consulting uptake, and specified compliance scope—with participants expressing confidence in administrative efficiency and no delays. No disagreements emerged, as all estimates aligned precisely, reflecting a uniform interpretation of the policy's resource logic and implementation plan.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group consistently estimated outputs directly from input phase figures: 19,000 graduate students and 1,000 postdocs supported. They converged on an average of 3 publications per graduate student per year, yielding 57,000 total publications, and 1 international conference presentation per student per year, totaling 19,000 presentations. This consensus was based on personal experience across fields (humanities, social sciences, engineering, chemistry) and the program's emphasis on internationalization and travel funding. No disagreements emerged; all participants provided identical numeric predictions and similar reasoning, citing the fixed input numbers and typical per-student output rates.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached full consensus on all outcome estimates, predicting a graduate employment rate of 84.2% and an international collaboration rate of 35% for BK21 participants in 2022. They reasoned that the program's substantial inputs (355,151 million KRW, 19,000 graduate slots) and activities (280 consulting sessions, 205 compliance checks) produced outputs of 57,000 publications and 19,000 conference presentations, which directly support the collaboration rate. The employment rate was derived from a policy target of a 0.3% annual increase from an 86.7% baseline, adjusted for a declining trend. The full-time faculty lecture ratio was uniformly estimated at 75%, reflecting a balance between program emphasis on quality mentoring and the continued role of part-time faculty. No disagreements emerged, as all participants independently arrived at identical numeric predictions.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group consistently estimated that the BK21 program's sustained investment over a decade would lead to modest, incremental improvements in South Korea's S&T talent competitiveness rank (from about 15th to 12th) and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers (from about 7 to 8.5). Key causal mechanisms included the program's funding of graduate stipends and postdoc salaries, support for international collaboration, and dissemination of best practices through webzines and forums, which gradually build a stronger research workforce and culture. However, participants noted that global competition and the rarity of top-cited publications, especially in humanities, limit the pace of improvement. There was full consensus on both numeric predictions (rank 12 and 8.5 publications per 1000 researchers) and the reasoning that the impact is positive but not transformative at the national level. No disagreements were expressed.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all three input variables, with every participant estimating total funding allocated at 410,597,000,000 KRW, graduate scholarship slots at 19,000, and postdoc positions at 300. Key themes included reliance on the stated national budget as ground truth, the constraint of fixed stipend levels (700K–1.3M KRW/month for students, 3M KRW/month for postdocs), and a 10% operating cost cap. Causal reasoning centered on top-tier universities like Hanyang receiving a larger share (2–3 billion KRW) but still being limited by the number of education research groups (about 10) and the remaining budget after student scholarships. No disagreements emerged; all narratives aligned on the same numeric predictions and underlying logic.
phase_summaries.Activities: All participants agreed that total funds disbursed would match the allocated budget of 410,597,000,000 KRW, based on the reliable two-installment schedule. There was consensus on three evaluation panel meetings (annual, mid-term, compliance), 280 consulting sessions (matching the prior proportion of groups requesting them), and 205 compliance checks targeting innovative talent training groups. No disagreements emerged; all predictions were identical, reflecting a shared understanding of the activities phase as a standard administrative process. The central tendency for all four variables was a single value: total_funds_disbursed = 410,597,000,000 KRW, evaluation_panels_convened = 3, consulting_sessions_held = 280, compliance_checks_executed = 205.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed how outputs—publications and international conference presentations—are directly driven by the number of supported graduate students (19,000) and postdocs (300), with productivity per participant as the key causal mechanism. There was consensus that compliance checks and consulting ensure accurate reporting, and that not all groups are equally productive due to coursework, thesis demands, and funding constraints. Disagreement centered on the average number of publications per participant: some estimated 3 (yielding ~57,000 publications and ~19,000 presentations), while others argued for a more conservative 2 (yielding ~38,000 publications and ~9,500 presentations), reflecting differing views on productivity variability across institutions. The range for publications was 38,000–57,000, and for presentations 9,500–19,000, with a central tendency around 38,000 publications and 9,500 presentations.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus that the BK21 program's outcomes are driven by its training, industry networks, and feedback mechanisms, with all participants predicting a 90% employment rate, 30% international collaboration rate, and 85% full-time faculty lecture ratio. They agreed that the 19,000 graduate students and 300 postdocs benefit from program design elements like conference support and consulting loops (280 sessions, 205 compliance checks), though regional universities may face slightly lower employment due to weaker industry linkages. No numeric disagreements emerged, as all estimates were identical, but narratives varied on whether top-tier or regional institutions would see slightly higher or lower rates. The central tendency for all predicted variables was uniform: employment rate 90%, international collaboration rate 30%, full-time faculty lecture ratio 85%, with aggregate counts of 5,700 international collaborations and 17,100 employed graduates.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that the impact phase reflects early, modest results from sustained BK21 investment, with causal mechanisms including gradual improvement in research culture (integrity, collaboration) and dissemination of best practices, but limited by the short 2021-2022 window and regional disparities. There was consensus that South Korea's S&T talent competitiveness rank improves only slightly from about 14th to 13th, and that high-impact publications per 1000 researchers are around 12.5, based on ~0.8% of publications from 19,000 graduate students and 300 postdocs reaching top-cited status. A key disagreement emerged: one participant from a top-tier university estimated a rank of 12 and 15.2 publications per 1000, reflecting a more optimistic top-tier perspective, while others from mid-sized or regional universities argued the national average is lower due to slower diffusion of best practices and regional disparities. The numeric predictions ranged from rank 12 to 13 (central tendency 13) and high-impact publications from 12.5 to 15.2 per 1000 researchers (central tendency 12.5).

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all three input variables, estimating total funding at 410.6 billion KRW, graduate scholarship slots at 19,000, and postdoc positions at 1,200. All participants based their reasoning on the national BK21 budget of 408,080 million KRW for education research groups plus 52,896 million KRW for graduate innovation support, then applied proportional allocation for Hanyang University. They consistently assumed about 60% of the budget goes to student support (average monthly stipend of 1 million KRW) and 10% to postdocs (minimum monthly salary of 3 million KRW), with minor adjustments for other expenses and eligibility constraints. No disagreements emerged; the central tendency was identical across all estimates.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached strong consensus that the Activities phase is the operational engine of the BK21 program, with all participants estimating the same numeric values for the four variables. They agreed that total funds disbursed would match the allocated budget of 408,080,000,000 KRW, following the standard 70% March and 30% September payment schedule. Evaluation panels were uniformly estimated at 3 meetings (annual, mid-term, compliance), consulting sessions at 280 (matching the policy's explicit count of groups requesting consulting), and compliance checks at 205 (the number of innovative talent training groups subject to verification). No disagreements emerged; all participants anchored their estimates directly on policy documents and assumed minimal administrative delays.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group consistently estimated 19,000 graduate students and 1,200 postdocs supported, leading to 57,000 publications and 9,500 international conference presentations. They agreed that an average of 3 publications per participant (adjusting for non-publishing first-year students) and 0.5 presentations per participant were reasonable, based on typical lab productivity and the policy's emphasis on internationalization. High compliance with self-evaluation reporting (575 out of 578 groups) was cited as supporting the reliability of these outputs. No disagreements emerged; all participants independently converged on the same estimates.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus on all outcome estimates, with every participant predicting an 85% graduate employment rate, 50% international collaboration rate, and 75% full-time faculty lecture ratio, leading to aggregate counts of 9,500 for international research collaborations and 85 for graduate employment. The central causal mechanism was that BK21's inputs (19,000 graduate slots, 1,200 postdoc positions) and activities (57,000 publications, 9,500 international conference presentations) directly drive these outcomes through enhanced networks, reputation, and training. Participants consistently adjusted the policy's baseline employment target (86.7% with 0.3%p annual increase) downward to 85% for 2022, citing slightly lower rates at regional universities. The international collaboration rate of 50% was justified by the high volume of conference presentations and program incentives, while the 75% full-time faculty lecture ratio reflected the policy's emphasis on quality mentoring. No disagreements emerged; all estimates were identical, and participants independently arrived at the same numbers based on similar reasoning about regional university contexts and program design.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached full consensus, estimating that sustained BK21 investment over a decade—19,000 graduate slots, 1,200 postdoc positions, 57,000 publications, and 9,500 international conference presentations—improves South Korea's global S&T talent competitiveness rank from around 10th to 8th and raises high-impact publications per 1000 researchers to 12.5. Key causal mechanisms include building a critical mass of skilled researchers, fostering a quality-focused research culture through 50% international collaboration and 75% full-time faculty lecture ratios, and disseminating best practices via 280 consulting sessions and commendations. All participants agreed on the same numeric estimates (rank: 8, publications: 12.5) and causal logic, with no disagreement.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group focused on estimating Hanyang University's BK21 inputs based on the national budget of 408,080 million KRW for 577 education research groups. There was strong consensus on the total funding allocated (40.8 billion KRW), graduate scholarship slots (180), and postdoc positions (20), with all participants converging on these values. A key point of disagreement was the number of education research groups at Hanyang: some initially assumed 9 groups, but the majority argued for about 40 groups based on the university's size and research profile, which drove the final estimates. The causal mechanism centered on budget constraints: stipend levels (master's 700K, doctoral 1.3M, candidate 1M KRW/month), a 10% operating cost cap, and postdoc minimum salary of 3M KRW/month limited each group to roughly 4-5 students and 0.5 postdocs, scaling to 180 slots and 20 positions across 40 groups.
phase_summaries.Activities: Participants consistently estimated full disbursement of 40,808,000,000 KRW, citing the two-installment schedule and no flagged fraud. There was consensus on 3 evaluation panels (annual, mid-term, compliance) and 205 compliance checks targeting innovative talent training groups. Disagreement arose over consulting sessions: one participant used the national total of 280, while others scaled it to Hanyang's 40 groups, yielding estimates of 280 vs. 40. The central tendency for consulting sessions was 40, reflecting proportional scaling.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed how the confirmed inputs of 180 graduate students and 20 postdocs would translate into research outputs, focusing on per-capita publication and conference rates. There was consensus on the input numbers and that productivity is driven by funding, evaluation pressure, and travel support. Disagreement centered on productivity assumptions: some participants estimated 720 publications (3 per student, 5 per postdoc) and 144 international conference presentations (60% of students, 80% of postdocs), while others estimated 540 publications (2.5 per student, 4.5 per postdoc) and 108 presentations (50% of students, 70% of postdocs). The range for publications was 540–720, and for conference presentations 108–144, with no clear central tendency.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed how prior-phase outputs (publications, conference presentations) and program features (travel support, full-time faculty incentives, industry networks) drive outcomes. There was consensus that full-time faculty lecture ratio is high (70-85%) and that evaluation/consulting feedback loops support standards. Disagreement centered on international collaboration and employment rates: some estimated 80% collaboration and 85% employment, citing strong incentives and reputation, while others estimated 60% collaboration and 80% employment, citing travel budget limits, pandemic effects, and job search delays. Numeric predictions ranged: international collaboration rate 60-80% (108-144 participants), employment rate 80-85% (144-153 participants), full-time faculty lecture ratio 70-85%.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that sustained BK21 investment over a decade gradually improves South Korea's S&T talent competitiveness and high-impact publication output, with causal mechanisms including the cumulative effect of training ~12,600 graduate students annually, dissemination of best practices via webzine and forums, and slow cultural changes in research integrity and collaboration. Consensus emerged that the national S&T talent competitiveness rank would improve modestly from its current ~15-20 range, and that high-impact publications per 1000 researchers would increase but remain moderate due to time lags and structural constraints. Disagreement centered on the magnitude of improvement: the graduate student estimated a rank of 15 and 8.5 publications, while the education research group head predicted a rank of 12 and 9.2 publications, with early-career researchers taking a middle ground of rank 14 and 8.8 publications. The numeric predictions ranged from rank 12 to 15 for competitiveness and from 8.5 to 9.2 for high-impact publications per 1000 researchers, with a central tendency around rank 14 and 8.8 publications.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all three input variables, with unanimous estimates of 408,080,000,000 KRW for total funding, 19,000 graduate scholarship slots, and 577 postdoc positions. All participants anchored their predictions directly on explicit figures from the policy context: the fixed annual budget, the sum of 12,600 future talent and 6,400 innovative talent students for slots, and the count of 577 education research groups for postdocs. No disagreements or alternative reasoning emerged, as each stakeholder viewed these as fixed inputs rather than variables subject to interpretation. The central tendency and range are identical across all estimates, reflecting a shared understanding of the program's resource allocation.
phase_summaries.Activities: All participants converged on identical numeric estimates for the activities phase, reflecting a strong consensus that the policy's fixed inputs directly determine activity levels. The total funds disbursed were unanimously set at 408,080,000,000 KRW, based on the mandated 70% March and 30% September payment schedule. Evaluation panels convened were estimated at 3, interpreted as covering annual, mid-term (planned for 2023), and compliance purposes. Consulting sessions held were pegged at 280, matching the policy's explicit count of groups that received consulting, while compliance checks executed were set at 205, targeting the innovative talent training groups. No disagreements emerged, as all reasoning relied on the same policy text and operational experience.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group consistently estimated outputs based on fixed inputs of 19,000 graduate slots and 577 postdoc positions, assuming full uptake due to competitive stipends. They converged on 28,500 publications and 5,700 international conference presentations, derived from per-participant rates of 1.5 publications and 0.3 presentations, adjusted for non-productive participants. All participants agreed on these values, citing personal experience and high compliance in self-reporting (575 of 578 groups). No disagreement emerged; the central tendency was unanimous across all four variables.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group consistently linked high international collaboration rates (30%) and employment rates (86.8%) to policy-funded international activities, full-time faculty lecture ratios (85%), and strong industry networks, with all participants converging on identical numeric estimates. They agreed that evaluation and consulting feedback loops (280 sessions, 205 compliance checks) help underperformers improve, reinforcing overall positive outcomes. No disagreements emerged, as all posts shared the same predictions and causal reasoning, reflecting a unified view of the program's direct impact on graduate employment and international research collaboration.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that sustained BK21 investment in graduate slots and postdocs builds a talent pipeline that gradually improves South Korea's S&T competitiveness rank and high-impact publication rates, with dissemination of best practices and evaluation feedback loops supporting research culture and quality. There was consensus that the national rank would improve modestly from around 18th to 15th, with most participants estimating 15, though one estimated 16. For high-impact publications, estimates ranged from 11.2 to 12.5 per 1000 researchers, with a central tendency around 12.0; the main disagreement was whether humanities and social sciences groups contribute less directly to top-cited S&T publications, leading to slightly more conservative estimates from some participants.

participants.professional_persona: 강원대학교 사회학과 박사 과정에 있으며, BK21 지원을 받아 지역 소멸과 청년 인구 이동에 관한 연구를 수행하고 있습니다. 석사 과정에서부터 지역 문제에 천착해 왔습니다.

participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 한국보건사회연구원이나 지방자치단체 정책연구소에서 근무하며 지역 맞춤형 정책 개발에 이바지하는 것이 목표입니다.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group focused on estimating Hanyang University's share of the national BK21 budget, consistently using the total national budget of 408,080 million KRW and assuming Hanyang receives about 10% (40.8 billion KRW). There was broad consensus on total funding (40.8 billion KRW) and postdoc positions (100), but disagreement on graduate scholarship slots, with one participant estimating 19,000 while others converged on 17,000-18,000, citing the active cohort size and eligibility constraints. The central tendency for graduate slots was around 17,000, and for postdocs 100, reflecting a conservative view of resource allocation.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group focused on the operational activities of BK21, emphasizing the disbursement of funds, evaluation panels, consulting sessions, and compliance checks as key mechanisms. There was consensus that total funds disbursed should be 40.8 billion KRW, following a 70% March and 30% September schedule, and that three evaluation panels are convened annually for annual, mid-term, and compliance purposes. A disagreement emerged on the scale of consulting sessions and compliance checks: one participant estimated 280 consulting sessions and 205 compliance checks nationally, while others argued these figures should be proportionally scaled to Hanyang University's share (about 10%), resulting in 28 consulting sessions and 20 compliance checks. The numeric predictions ranged from 28 to 280 for consulting sessions and 20 to 205 for compliance checks, with the majority favoring the lower, proportional estimates.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed outputs as direct results of funding and activities, with a consensus on 17,000 graduate students and 100 postdocs supported, leading to 34,000 publications and 3,400 international conference presentations. They agreed on a conservative average of 2 publications per participant and a 20% conference presentation rate, citing high compliance (575 of 578 groups reporting) and prior phase allocations (40.8 billion KRW, 3 evaluation panels, 28 consulting sessions, 20 compliance checks) as ensuring accountability. A key disagreement emerged: one participant estimated 51,000 publications (3 per participant), while others argued this was too high, especially for early-stage students, and aligned on 34,000. Numeric predictions ranged from 34,000 to 51,000 for publications, with a central tendency around 34,000, and conference presentations were consistently 3,400.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus that the outcomes phase directly results from prior funding, activities, and outputs, with all participants estimating an 80% employment rate (13,600 of 17,000 graduates) and a 20% international collaboration rate (3,400 of 17,000 participants), citing the declining trend from the 86.7% policy target and personal lab observations. They also agreed on a 75% full-time faculty lecture ratio, attributing it to BK21 incentives. No disagreements emerged; all predictions were identical, reflecting shared reasoning that evaluation panels, consulting sessions, and compliance checks create feedback loops to improve these rates.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that sustained BK21 investment over a decade builds a critical mass of skilled researchers, fostering a culture of integrity and collaboration, and that dissemination of best practices via webzine and forums raises overall S&T productivity. All participants estimated national S&T talent competitiveness rank at 12 and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers at 15, with no disagreement. The reasoning uniformly cited a 1.5% top-cited publication rate from lab experience, applied to 34,000 publications from 17,000 participants, yielding 510 top-cited papers or 15 per 1000 researchers, while acknowledging global competition limits rank improvement.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group focused on estimating Hanyang University's BK21 inputs by scaling from the total education research group budget (355,151 million KRW for 577 groups, averaging ~615 million per group) and the graduate innovation support fund (52,896 million KRW across 20 universities). There was consensus that Hanyang likely hosts 10-12 groups, yielding a total funding range of 8-10 billion KRW, with a central tendency around 8-10 billion. Graduate scholarship slots were estimated at 100-120, based on an average monthly stipend of ~1 million KRW and a scholarship budget of roughly 14-15% of total funding. Postdoc positions were estimated at 12-15, with each costing at least 36 million KRW per year. Disagreement centered on whether Hanyang's allocation is at the lower end (8 billion, 100 slots, 12 postdocs) or higher end (10 billion, 120 slots, 15 postdocs), reflecting differing assumptions about the number of groups and budget share for a major research university.
phase_summaries.Activities: Stakeholders discussed the activities phase of the BK21 program, focusing on fund disbursement, evaluation panels, consulting sessions, and compliance checks. There was consensus that total funds disbursed should match the allocated amount (77,830,200,000 KRW or 10 billion KRW) following the two-installment schedule, and that three evaluation panels (annual, mid-term, compliance) would be convened. Disagreement centered on consulting sessions and compliance checks: some estimated national-level figures (280 consulting sessions, 205 compliance checks), while others argued for Hanyang-specific estimates (12 sessions, 12 checks). Predictions for consulting sessions ranged from 12 to 280, and for compliance checks from 12 to 205, reflecting differing interpretations of scope.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed how prior phase inputs (graduate slots and postdoc positions) directly determine the number of supported participants, with estimates for graduate students ranging from 100 to 120 and postdocs from 12 to 15. Consensus emerged that high compliance with national reporting (575 of 578 groups) ensures accurate output counts. Disagreement centered on publication and conference presentation productivity, driven by field-specific differences: participants from high-output fields like computer engineering and biotechnology estimated 360 publications and 100 presentations, while those from environmental or food science fields estimated 250 publications and 60 presentations. The central tendency for publications was around 300, and for international conference presentations around 80, reflecting the STEM-heavy composition of Hanyang University.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed how program inputs (graduate slots, postdocs) and outputs (publications, conferences) drive employment and international collaboration rates, with evaluation and consulting feedback loops providing gradual improvement. Consensus emerged that full-time faculty lecture ratios would be around 70-75%, reflecting policy emphasis but persistent part-time lecturers. Disagreement centered on employment and collaboration rates: some participants estimated high values (employment 85-90%, collaboration 80-85%) based on Hanyang's top-tier STEM reputation and resource advantages, while others argued for lower rates (employment 75%, collaboration 60%) citing field-specific barriers, moderate policy targets, and regional university experiences. Numeric predictions ranged from 75% to 90% for employment rate and 60% to 85% for international collaboration rate, with central tendencies around 80% and 70% respectively.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that sustained BK21 investment in graduate talent development gradually improves national innovation capacity, but the short 2021-2022 window captures only early impacts. There was consensus that South Korea's S&T talent competitiveness rank would improve modestly, with estimates clustering around 8-10, and that high-impact publications per 1000 researchers would range from 35 to 45, reflecting moderate short-term effects. Disagreement centered on the magnitude of improvement: participants from leading universities estimated higher values (rank 8, 45 publications) based on stronger research cultures and output assumptions, while regional participants gave lower estimates (rank 10, 35 publications), citing field-specific barriers and slower systemic change. The central tendency for rank was 9, and for high-impact publications, 40 per 1000 researchers.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on Hanyang University's BK21 inputs, estimating total funding at 12 billion KRW, graduate scholarship slots at 400, and postdoc positions at 30. These estimates were derived by proportionally allocating national totals: 410.6 billion KRW overall budget, 19,000 national graduate slots, and an average of 2 postdocs per education research group, assuming Hanyang hosts about 15 of the 577 groups nationally. No disagreements emerged, as all participants applied similar proportional reasoning based on the policy's resource allocation logic.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed the disbursement of BK21 funds via a standard 70% March / 30% September schedule, with full disbursement of 12 billion KRW to Hanyang University expected. There was consensus on three formal evaluation panel meetings per year (annual, mid-term, compliance). For consulting sessions and compliance checks, most participants used proportional reasoning (280 sessions nationally for 578 groups, 205 compliance checks for innovative talent groups) to estimate about 7 sessions and 15 checks for Hanyang, though one participant used national totals (280 sessions, 205 checks). Predictions ranged narrowly: total_funds_disbursed 12 billion KRW (all), evaluation_panels_convened 3 (all), consulting_sessions_held 7 (four participants) or 280 (one), compliance_checks_executed 15 (four) or 205 (one).
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed how the number of graduate students (400) and postdocs (30) supported by BK21 scholarships drives research outputs, with publication productivity varying by field: STEM participants produce 2-3 publications per year, while humanities/social sciences produce 1-2. There was consensus that international conference presentations represent about 20% of publications and that high reporting compliance (575 out of 578 groups) ensures accurate output data. Disagreement centered on the overall publication estimate, with STEM-focused participants proposing 1200 publications and 240 presentations, while those considering a mix of fields estimated 800 publications and 160 presentations; a middle-ground estimate of 1000 publications and 200 presentations also emerged. The range for publications was 800–1200, with a central tendency around 800–1000, and for presentations 160–240, centering near 160–200.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus that the BK21 program's outcomes are highly positive, with all participants estimating a graduate employment rate of 85% (350 out of 400 graduates) and an international collaboration rate of 40%. They consistently attributed these outcomes to the program's conference support, collaboration incentives, strong industry ties, and full-time faculty hiring, which they estimated yields a 75% full-time faculty lecture ratio. No disagreements emerged on the numeric estimates or causal reasoning, though one participant noted that humanities and social sciences groups might have slightly lower rates than STEM fields, but overall averages remain high. The central tendency across all predictions is uniform: employment rate 85%, international collaboration rate 40%, and full-time faculty lecture ratio 75%.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed how BK21's decade-long investment in graduate students and postdocs has built a pipeline of skilled researchers, boosting South Korea's innovation capacity and research culture through dissemination of best practices. There was consensus that the program positively impacts both targets, but disagreement on the magnitude due to field-specific productivity differences, with STEM fields (especially AI) seen as having higher publication rates than humanities/social sciences. Predictions for national S&T talent competitiveness rank ranged from 8 to 10, with a central tendency around 9-10, and for high-impact publications per 1000 researchers ranged from 10.0 to 12.5, centering around 10.0-11.0.
