phase_summaries.Inputs: The group discussed the allocation of BK21 program inputs at Hanyang University, focusing on how the national budget of 408,080 million KRW for education research groups translates into local resources. There was consensus that Hanyang's share funds approximately 20-30 research groups, with graduate stipends fixed at 700K-1.3M KRW/month and postdoc minimum salary at 3M KRW/month. Disagreement centered on the exact number of slots, with estimates ranging from 1,100 to 1,200 graduate scholarship slots and 140 to 150 postdoc positions, influenced by field-specific eligibility constraints (e.g., humanities groups having fewer slots). The central tendency was around 1,150 graduate slots and 145 postdoc positions, reflecting a moderate view that balances STEM-heavy assumptions with budget limitations.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group focused on translating policy inputs into concrete activities, emphasizing the budget disbursement schedule (70% in March, 30% in September) and the direct link between allocated funds and total_funds_disbursed, which was universally estimated at 40,808 million KRW. There was consensus that evaluation_panels_convened would be 3, reflecting annual, mid-term, and compliance meetings. For consulting_sessions_held, estimates ranged from 10 to 280, with most participants scaling the national figure of 280 sessions for 578 groups down to Hanyang's share (10-15), though one participant used the national total. Compliance_checks_executed estimates clustered around 10, based on the national count of 205 groups subject to checks and Hanyang's proportional share. Disagreement centered on consulting uptake, with some participants reasoning that humanities and social sciences groups underutilize consulting, leading to lower estimates (10-12) compared to the higher end (15).
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed how outputs—publications and conference presentations—are driven by the number of supported graduate students and postdocs, with productivity varying by academic field. There was consensus that graduate students produce 1-3 publications per year and postdocs 2-4, and that international conference presentations are less frequent than publications. Disagreement centered on average productivity: STEM participants estimated higher per-capita rates (2.5 publications per graduate student, 3.5 per postdoc), while humanities/social sciences participants estimated lower rates (1.5 and 2.5, respectively). The range for total publications was 2,300 to 3,450, with a central tendency around 2,875; for international conference presentations, the range was 460 to 690, with a central tendency around 575.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed how field-specific norms and policy incentives shape outcomes, with causal mechanisms linking inputs (graduate slots, postdoc positions) and activities (consulting, compliance checks) to outputs (publications, conference presentations) and finally to employment and collaboration rates. There was consensus that the full-time faculty lecture ratio is high at 85%, reflecting policy emphasis on full-time faculty. Disagreement centered on employment and collaboration rates: humanities/social sciences participants estimated 80% employment and 30% collaboration, while STEM participants estimated 90% employment and 40% collaboration, citing stronger industry ties and global networks in STEM. The range for employment rate was 80-90% (central tendency ~85%), for international collaboration rate 30-40% (central tendency ~35%), with corresponding participant counts of 920-1035 and 345-460, respectively.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed how BK21's sustained investment in graduate stipends and postdoc salaries enables focused research, but its modest scale relative to global competitors limits dramatic improvements in national S&T talent competitiveness. There was consensus that the 2021-2022 window captures only early-stage effects, with systemic changes in research culture being gradual. Disagreement centered on field disparities: STEM participants estimated higher national competitiveness (rank 10) and high-impact publications (15.0 per 1000 researchers), while humanities and social sciences participants estimated lower values (rank 12, 12.5 publications). Predictions for national S&T talent competitiveness rank ranged from 10 to 12, and for high-impact publications per 1000 researchers from 12.5 to 15.0, with a central tendency around rank 11 and 13.75 publications.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group focused on estimating Hanyang University-specific BK21 inputs, with a consensus that the university hosts 10-15 education research groups, each with typical budgets of 800 million to 1.2 billion KRW, supporting 30-40 graduate students and 2-3 postdocs. There was strong agreement on Hanyang-specific estimates: total funding around 12 billion KRW, 400 graduate scholarship slots, and 30 postdoc positions, with a central tendency of 12 billion KRW, 400 slots, and 30 positions. A key disagreement emerged with one participant who used national totals (355.151 billion KRW, 19,000 slots, 1,200 postdocs) instead of Hanyang-specific numbers, which others rejected as misaligned with the prediction fields. The causal reasoning linked group budgets and student/postdoc counts to Hanyang's scale as a major research university, with engineering groups perceived as larger than humanities groups.
phase_summaries.Activities: Participants focused on translating national program-level activities to Hanyang University, with a central tension between using national totals as proxies versus scaling down to Hanyang-specific figures. There was strong consensus that total funds disbursed would be 12 billion KRW (full disbursement via the 70/30 schedule) and that 3 evaluation panels would be convened (annual, mid-term, compliance). Disagreement emerged on consulting sessions and compliance checks: one participant used national figures (280 consulting sessions, 205 compliance checks), while the majority argued for Hanyang-specific estimates of 12 consulting sessions and 15 compliance checks, based on the university's ~10-15 education research groups. The range for consulting sessions was 12-280, and for compliance checks 15-205, with the majority converging on the lower, Hanyang-specific values.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed how outputs like publications and conference presentations depend on field-specific research productivity, with engineering groups producing higher per-capita outputs than humanities groups. There was consensus on 400 graduate students supported and 30 postdocs, based on prior phases and Hanyang's scale. Disagreement centered on publications and presentations: engineering-focused participants estimated 1200 publications and 200 presentations, while humanities participants estimated 800 and 120. A middle-ground estimate of 1000 publications and 160 presentations emerged, reflecting a weighted average across fields. The final range for publications was 800–1200, and for presentations 120–200.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed outcomes based on prior inputs, activities, and outputs, with causal mechanisms linking program reputation, training quality, and evaluation feedback loops to employment and collaboration rates. There was consensus on an employment rate around 85% and international collaboration rate of 40%, with full-time faculty lecture ratio at 75%, reflecting engineering-heavy perspectives. A humanities participant disagreed, estimating lower rates (employment 80%, collaboration 30%, faculty ratio 70%) due to tighter job markets and less international collaboration in humanities. Numeric predictions ranged from 80-85% for employment rate, 30-40% for international collaboration, and 70-75% for full-time faculty lecture ratio, with graduate employment count at 320-340 and international collaboration count at 120-160.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that sustained BK21 investment in graduate talent development, particularly in engineering and natural sciences, drives national innovation capacity by producing a skilled workforce that improves S&T talent competitiveness and increases high-impact publications. There was consensus that South Korea's global S&T talent competitiveness rank would improve from around 14th to 11th, and that high-impact publications per 1000 researchers would rise to about 9.0, driven by engineering and natural sciences groups. A point of disagreement was the humanities participant's more conservative estimate of rank 12 and 8.5 publications, citing slower cultural change and lower publication rates in humanities, while engineering participants argued for more optimistic outcomes due to higher productivity and technology transfer. The numeric predictions ranged from rank 11 to 12 for competitiveness and 8.5 to 9.2 for publications, with a central tendency around rank 11 and 9.0 publications.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all three input variables, treating the total funding allocated as a fixed policy parameter of 408,080 million KRW. Graduate scholarship slots were uniformly estimated at 19,000, derived by summing the stated 12,600 future talent and 6,400 innovative talent participants. Postdoc positions were consistently estimated at 1,200, based on 577 education research groups with typical allocations of 2-3 postdocs per group, tempered by budget constraints. No disagreements emerged; all participants anchored their reasoning directly on the policy document's stated figures and their own operational experience.
phase_summaries.Activities: All participants agreed that the total funds disbursed would be exactly 408,080 million KRW, based on the policy's two-installment schedule (70% in March, 30% in September). There was unanimous consensus that three evaluation panels would be convened (annual, mid-term, compliance), 280 consulting sessions would be held (as requested by groups), and 205 compliance checks would be executed (targeting innovative talent training groups). No disagreements emerged; all estimates were identical across stakeholders. The central tendency and range for each variable were: total_funds_disbursed = 408,080,000,000 KRW; evaluation_panels_convened = 3; consulting_sessions_held = 280; compliance_checks_executed = 205.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached strong consensus that the fixed inputs of 19,000 graduate students and 1,200 postdocs, combined with an average of 2 publications per supported participant per year, yield approximately 38,000 publications. International conference presentations were estimated at 0.4 per participant, totaling about 7,600, based on the observation that roughly 40% of participants present annually. All participants agreed on these estimates, citing consistent personal experiences and the high compliance rate of 575 out of 578 groups submitting self-evaluation reports as evidence of reliable data capture. No disagreements emerged, and the central tendency for all predictions was uniform across the group.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus on all outcome estimates, predicting an employment rate of 84.2% (range: 84.2%–84.2%), an international collaboration rate of 40% (range: 40%–40%), and a full-time faculty lecture ratio of 75% (range: 75%–75%). They reasoned that the employment rate reflects a declining trend from the 86.7% baseline, while international collaboration is driven by conference support and incentives, and the faculty ratio benefits from program emphasis on quality mentoring. All participants agreed that evaluation and consulting feedback loops help refine outcomes by identifying weaknesses and promoting best practices. No disagreements or divergent estimates were present.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus, estimating South Korea's national S&T talent competitiveness rank at 15 and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers at 12.5. They reasoned that sustained BK21 investment in 19,000 graduate slots and 1,200 postdocs, producing 38,000 publications, gradually enhances innovation capacity but faces stiff global competition and time lags in cultural change. Only about 5% of publications become top-cited, and the national researcher denominator (≈150,000 FTE) dilutes BK21's contribution, leading to conservative near-term impact estimates. No disagreements emerged; all participants aligned on the gradual, indirect nature of the program's effects on national metrics.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group consistently used the national education research group budget of 355,151 million KRW as a proxy for Hanyang University's total funding, with a consensus that operating costs are capped at 10% and that stipend levels (700K–1.3M KRW monthly) and postdoc minimum salaries (3M KRW/month) constrain the number of slots and positions. There was broad agreement on total funding (355,151 million KRW) and on the need to balance graduate scholarships with postdoc positions and operating costs. Disagreements centered on the number of graduate scholarship slots (ranging from 1,100 to 1,400) and postdoc positions (130 to 180), driven by differing assumptions about average stipend levels (higher averages leading to fewer slots) and whether postdoc salaries exceed the minimum. The central tendency for graduate slots was around 1,100–1,200, and for postdoc positions around 130–150, reflecting a more conservative view from biosciences and policy participants compared to economics participants.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group focused on the standard two-installment disbursement schedule (70% in March, 30% in September) and the total allocated budget of 355,151 million KRW, with consensus that full disbursement occurs. There was broad agreement on 3 evaluation panel meetings per year (annual, mid-term, compliance). A key divergence emerged on consulting sessions and compliance checks: one participant used national totals (280 consulting sessions, 205 compliance checks), while the majority scaled proportionally to Hanyang University's estimated 10-15 education research groups, yielding estimates of 140 consulting sessions and 100 compliance checks. The central tendency across predictions was total_funds_disbursed = 355,151,000,000 KRW; evaluation_panels_convened = 3; consulting_sessions_held = 140 (range 140–280); compliance_checks_executed = 100 (range 100–205).
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group's discussion centered on translating inputs (graduate slots and postdoc positions) into research outputs, using per-capita productivity rates for publications and conference presentations. There was strong consensus on productivity norms: each graduate student produces about 3 publications per year and 0.6 international conference presentations, while each postdoc produces about 4 publications and 1 presentation. The main disagreement was over the number of supported participants: most participants used the consensus input of 1,180 graduate students and 144 postdocs, yielding 3,540 publications and 708 presentations, but one education research group leader estimated lower slots (1,100 graduate students, 130 postdocs) due to assumed higher stipends and operating costs, resulting in 3,300 publications and 660 presentations. The range for publications was 3,300–3,540, and for presentations 660–708, with the majority favoring the higher end.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group focused on how outputs (publications, conference presentations) and activities (evaluation, consulting) drive outcomes. They consistently linked international collaboration to conference presentations, estimating 660 participants involved, and set graduate employment at 85% and full-time faculty lecture ratio at 75%. Consensus emerged on absolute collaboration numbers and employment rate, but disagreement existed on the denominator for collaboration rate (some used 1,230 participants, others 1,324), leading to rates of 50-60%. Numeric predictions were uniform: international collaboration rate 660, graduate employment rate 85%, employment rate 85%, international collaboration rate 60%, full-time faculty lecture ratio 75%.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that the 2021-2022 impact window is too short for significant changes in national S&T talent competitiveness, requiring a decade for systemic shifts. They agreed that the program's focus on research integrity and collaboration will gradually increase high-impact publications, but effects are nascent. Consensus emerged around a rank of 13 and 7.5 high-impact publications per 1000 researchers, with one outlier predicting a rank of 12 and 8.5 publications. The majority reasoned that other nations' investments and the time needed for cultural change limit early improvements, leading to conservative estimates.

participants.professional_persona: 충남 지역 국립대학교에서 전자공학 석사과정을 밟고 있으며, BK21 교육연구단의 지원을 받아 반도체 소자 연구를 하고 있습니다. 매달 70만 원의 장학금으로 생활하고 있으며, 연구실 내에서 학부생 멘토링도 담당합니다.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached consensus on total funding allocated (410,597,000,000 KRW) and graduate scholarship slots (19,000), both directly from policy documents. For postdoc positions, all participants estimated 1,200, derived from budget calculations (remaining ~91,636 million KRW after graduate stipends) and typical group sizes (2-3 per 577 groups). No disagreements emerged; all reasoning aligned on budget constraints and explicit policy numbers.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached full consensus on all four activity-level estimates, drawing directly from the 2022 BK21 Basic Plan. They agreed that total funds disbursed were 408,080,000,000 KRW (the stated annual budget, excluding carryover), evaluation panels convened were 3 (annual, mid-term, and compliance), consulting sessions held were 280 (matching the number of groups that requested consulting), and compliance checks executed were 205 (the number of groups subject to implementation checks). No disagreements emerged; all participants cited the same policy evidence, and the central tendency for each variable was identical across all predictions.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group focused on translating funded participant counts (19,000 graduate slots and 1,200 postdoc positions) into research outputs, with a central mechanism being per-capita publication and presentation rates. There was strong consensus among most participants that each supported participant produces about 2 publications per year (yielding 38,000 total publications) and that about half attend an international conference (yielding 9,500 presentations), based on STEM field experience and high reporting compliance (575 of 578 groups). A dissenting humanities perspective argued for lower per-capita rates (1 publication per graduate student, 2 per postdoc; 40% conference attendance), leading to estimates of 28,500 publications and 7,600 presentations, citing field-specific productivity differences and travel constraints. The numeric predictions thus ranged from 28,500 to 38,000 for publications and 7,600 to 9,500 for international conference presentations, with the majority converging on the higher end.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus on all outcome estimates, with every participant predicting an 80% graduate employment rate (15,200 of 19,000 graduates) and a 24% international collaboration rate (4,560 of 19,000 participants). They consistently cited the 86.7% policy target (adjusted for a declining trend), personal observations of peer outcomes, and the program's evaluation and consulting feedback loops (3 panels, 280 sessions, 205 compliance checks) as key causal mechanisms driving performance. The full-time faculty lecture ratio was uniformly estimated at 75%, attributed to program incentives and evaluation criteria. No disagreements emerged, and all numeric predictions were identical across participants.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed how sustained BK21 investment over a decade builds a pipeline of skilled graduates, boosting national S&T talent competitiveness and high-impact publications through improved research culture, international collaboration, and dissemination of best practices. There was consensus that the program positively impacts both indicators, with most participants estimating a national S&T talent competitiveness rank of 9 and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers around 10.1. Disagreement centered on the magnitude of impact: one participant from bioscience estimated a rank of 8 and 12.5 publications, citing stronger effects in STEM, while a humanities representative estimated a rank of 10 and 8.2 publications, noting lower visibility and citation impact in their field. The range for rank was 8–10, and for publications 8.2–12.5 per 1000 researchers, with a central tendency of rank 9 and publications 10.1.

participants.professional_persona: 컴퓨터공학 박사 후 연구원으로, BK21 교육연구단에서 인공지능 기반 의료 영상 진단 알고리즘을 개발 중입니다. 연구단의 인프라를 활용해 대규모 데이터를 다룰 수 있습니다.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group focused on interpreting the 'total_funding_allocated' variable as either the national BK21 budget (≈410.6 billion KRW) or Hanyang University's share (≈20.5 billion KRW), with a central tendency around 20.5 billion KRW for Hanyang. Consensus emerged that graduate scholarship slots should be derived from the national total of 19,000 (12,600 future talent + 6,400 innovative talent), leading to estimates of either 19,000 (national) or 950 (Hanyang's 5% share). Postdoc positions were estimated at 300 nationally or 15 for Hanyang, based on the number of education research groups. The main disagreement was whether the prediction field referred to national or university-specific values, with most participants ultimately favoring Hanyang-specific estimates. Causal reasoning linked funding levels directly to the number of scholarship slots and postdoc positions, assuming proportional allocation across universities.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group focused on scaling national BK21 activity targets to Hanyang University, using a 5% share estimate (20.53 billion KRW) for total funds disbursed and 3 evaluation panels as consensus. A key disagreement emerged: some participants used national totals (280 consulting sessions, 205 compliance checks) while others scaled proportionally to Hanyang (14 consulting sessions, 10 compliance checks), reflecting a split between university-specific and national-level reasoning. The central tendency for consulting sessions was 14 (range 14–280) and for compliance checks 10 (range 10–205), with all agreeing on funds disbursed (20.53 billion KRW) and evaluation panels (3).
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed how BK21 outputs—graduate students and postdocs supported, publications, and conference presentations—are driven by the number of funded positions and field-specific productivity norms. There was consensus that Hanyang University supports 950 graduate students and 15 postdocs, based on a 5% share of national totals. Disagreements centered on publication and presentation rates: engineering-focused participants estimated higher outputs (2,375–2,850 publications; 1,425–1,900 presentations), while humanities and social science participants estimated lower outputs (1,900 publications; 950 presentations). The computer engineering postdoc offered a middle-ground estimate (2,375 publications; 1,425 presentations). Overall, publication estimates ranged from 1,900 to 2,850, and presentation estimates from 950 to 1,900.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed how field-specific factors (humanities vs. engineering) influence employment and international collaboration outcomes, with causal mechanisms including industry demand, co-authorship norms, and full-time faculty mentoring. Consensus emerged that full-time faculty lecture ratios are high (85-90%) and that evaluation feedback loops support gradual improvement. Disagreement centered on employment and collaboration rates: humanities participants estimated 73.5% employment and 15% collaboration, while engineering participants estimated 78.9% and 21%, reflecting stronger industry ties and international norms in STEM. Numeric predictions ranged from 700 to 750 for graduate employment count and 150 to 200 for international collaboration count, with central tendencies around 725 and 175, respectively.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed how sustained BK21 investment builds a skilled researcher pipeline, gradually improving national S&T talent competitiveness and high-impact publication rates, though effects are modest due to the program's limited scale and concentration in a few universities. Consensus emerged that the program's impact on national rankings is gradual, with systemic changes in research culture taking time to materialize. Disagreement centered on the magnitude of impact: humanities-focused participants estimated a national S&T talent competitiveness rank of 15 and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers at 12.5, citing lower citation rates in their fields, while engineering-focused participants estimated a rank of 12 and publications at 18.5, emphasizing higher STEM output. Predictions for the competitiveness rank ranged from 12 to 15, and for high-impact publications from 12.5 to 18.5, reflecting disciplinary differences in research output and citation impact.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all three input variables, treating the national BK21 budget of 408,080 million KRW and the total of 19,000 graduate scholarship slots (12,600 future talent + 6,400 innovative talent) as ground truth from the policy document. Postdoc positions were uniformly estimated at 1,154 by dividing roughly 10% of the total budget by the annual minimum salary of 36 million KRW per postdoc. No disagreements emerged, as all participants aligned their reasoning with the explicit policy figures and the same calculation method for postdocs. The central tendency and range for each variable were identical across all predictions: total funding = 408,080,000,000 KRW, graduate scholarship slots = 19,000, and postdoc positions = 1,154.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached full consensus on all four activity-level variables, with every participant estimating total funds disbursed at 408,080,000,000 KRW, evaluation panels convened at 3, consulting sessions held at 280, and compliance checks executed at 205. The central reasoning was that the policy's two-installment disbursement schedule (70% in March, 30% in September) is a procedural mechanism that does not alter the total allocated budget unless sanctions apply, and that the three distinct evaluation types (annual graduate innovation evaluation, mid-term future talent training evaluation, and innovative talent compliance checks) each require a formal panel meeting. Consulting sessions were uniformly set at 280 based on the policy's explicit statement that 280 out of 578 groups received consulting in the prior year, and compliance checks were pegged at 205 because the policy specifies that the 205 innovative talent training groups are subject to implementation checks. No disagreements or alternative estimates emerged across the five participants, who represented education research groups, early-career researchers, graduate students, and a policy role.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached consensus on the number of graduate students (19,000) and postdocs (1,154) supported, treating these as ground truth from policy documents. They agreed that compliance checks and consulting sessions ensure accurate reporting. Disagreement centered on publication output: one participant estimated 57,000 publications (assuming 2-3 per graduate student and 3-4 per postdoc), while the majority estimated 38,000 (assuming 1.5 per graduate student and 2.5 per postdoc). For international conference presentations, the majority estimated 5,700 (30% participation rate), while one participant estimated 7,600 (40% participation rate). The central tendency for publications was 38,000 and for conference presentations 5,700.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus on all outcome estimates, predicting a graduate employment rate of 85% (range 85-85), an international collaboration rate of 30% (range 30-30) yielding 5,700 participants, and a full-time faculty lecture ratio of 75% (range 75-75). Key causal mechanisms included the effective deployment of 19,000 graduate slots and 1,154 postdoc positions, supported by 280 consulting sessions and 205 compliance checks, which were seen as feedback loops sustaining these rates. The employment estimate was slightly below the policy baseline of 86.7% due to a competitive job market, while collaboration and lecture ratios reflected typical program and university norms. No disagreements emerged, as all participants aligned their reasoning on the impact of program resources and evaluation processes.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that sustained BK21 investment in graduate stipends and postdoc salaries, combined with consulting and compliance checks, supports a stable research environment, but systemic cultural changes take time to affect national S&T talent competitiveness. There was consensus that the national rank would improve only modestly due to global competition, with estimates ranging from 12 to 15 and a central tendency around 13-14. For high-impact publications per 1000 researchers, estimates ranged from 12.5 to 18.5, with most clustering near 15-16, reflecting disagreement over field-specific citation rates and the active researcher pool size. The education researcher's lower estimate (12.5) was countered by STEM participants who argued for higher citation impact in their fields, while the Seoul postdoc's higher estimate (18.5) was seen as too optimistic by others.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group focused on interpreting the policy context to estimate inputs for Hanyang University's BK21 program, with a key theme being the challenge of allocating program-wide figures to a single university. There was strong consensus on the numeric estimates: total funding allocated at 355,151,000,000 KRW, 6,400 graduate scholarship slots, and 205 postdoc positions, with all participants providing identical values. However, a major disagreement emerged over whether the total funding figure represented Hanyang's allocation or the sum for all 577 education research groups across all universities, with some arguing it was Hanyang-specific and others insisting it was a program-wide total. The central tendency of predictions was uniform across all three variables, with no variation in the reported numbers.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group consistently estimated total funds disbursed at 355,151 million KRW, evaluation panels convened at 3, consulting sessions held at 280, and compliance checks executed at 205, citing the policy context for these figures. Key themes included the two-installment disbursement schedule (70% in March, 30% in September) and the three types of evaluation panels (annual, mid-term, compliance). There was a minor disagreement about whether the total funds figure applied to all education research groups or specifically to Hanyang University, but most participants used the program-wide total. All numeric predictions were identical across participants, indicating strong consensus on the activity-level estimates.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed how the estimated 6,400 graduate slots and 205 postdoc positions for Hanyang University in 2022 would translate into research outputs, with a key causal mechanism being that typical publication and presentation rates vary by academic field. There was consensus that the high compliance rate (575 out of 578 groups submitting self-evaluation reports) supports reliable reporting, and that outputs should be estimated conservatively to account for non-publishing participants and domestic conference preferences. Disagreement centered on field-specific productivity: STEM and life sciences participants estimated 2.5 publications per participant (yielding ~16,000 publications) and 0.5 international presentations per participant (yielding ~3,200 presentations), while humanities and social sciences participants estimated 1.5–2.0 publications per participant (yielding ~12,800 publications) and 0.3–0.5 presentations per participant (yielding ~2,560 presentations). The range of predictions for publications was 12,800–16,000, and for international conference presentations was 2,560–3,200, with a central tendency around 14,400 publications and 2,880 presentations.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed how the BK21 program's prior phases (6,400 graduate slots, 205 postdoc positions, 12,800-16,000 publications, 2,560-3,200 international conference presentations) influence graduate employment and international collaboration rates, with causal mechanisms including program reputation, research training, and evaluation feedback loops. There was strong consensus on all three outcome estimates: employment rate at 85% (slightly below the national target of 86.7%), international collaboration rate at 10%, and full-time faculty lecture ratio at 75%. No disagreement emerged, as all participants independently arrived at identical values, though some noted that STEM fields might yield slightly higher rates but chose conservative estimates due to funding constraints and language barriers. The numeric predictions were uniform across all participants: employment rate 85%, international collaboration rate 10%, and full-time faculty lecture ratio 75%.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed the long-term impact of sustained BK21 investment on national innovation capacity, emphasizing that improvements in S&T talent competitiveness and high-impact publications would be gradual due to the program's modest scale relative to the national system and time lags in research culture change. Consensus emerged that South Korea's global rank would improve only slightly from around 15, with most participants estimating a rank of 12 or 13, and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers would show modest gains, ranging from 7.2 to 8.5. Disagreement centered on field-specific publication rates: social science and humanities participants estimated lower values (rank 13, 7.2 publications) due to lower top-cited output, while STEM and life sciences participants estimated higher values (rank 12, 8.5 publications) reflecting greater citation potential. The central tendency for national rank was 12-13, and for high-impact publications was 7.2-8.5 per 1000 researchers.

participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 석사 졸업 후 국내 정유·화학 대기업 연구소에 입사하여 실용화 연구를 주도하고 싶습니다. BK21 지원을 통해 연구 역량을 키우고 글로벌 네트워크를 확장하는 것이 목표입니다.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group consistently interpreted the input variables as national-level figures for the entire BK21 program, not Hanyang University-specific, based on the policy context providing only national totals. There was strong consensus on all three estimates: total funding allocated at 410,597 million KRW (derived from 408,080 million for education research groups, 52,896 million for graduate innovation, and 2,550 million for evaluation management), graduate scholarship slots at 19,000 (from 12,600 future talent and 6,400 innovative talent students), and postdoc positions at 300 (estimated from the 355,151 million KRW project budget and a minimum annual salary of 36 million KRW per postdoc, assuming roughly 3% allocation). No disagreements emerged, as all participants aligned on the national interpretation and the same numeric predictions.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group unanimously agreed that total funds disbursed would match the annual budget of 408,080 million KRW, based on the standard two-installment schedule. They also converged on 3 evaluation panel meetings per year (annual, mid-term, compliance), 280 consulting sessions (as requested by groups), and 205 compliance checks (targeting innovative talent training groups). No disagreements emerged; all participants cited the same policy evidence and causal mechanisms linking these activities to program operations. The numeric predictions were identical across all variables, reflecting strong consensus.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group consistently estimated 19,000 graduate students and 300 postdocs supported, based on the policy's stated national slots. They converged on 57,000 publications and 9,500 international conference presentations, derived from a conservative average of 3 publications and 0.5 presentations per supported researcher, citing lab experience and the program's quality focus. No disagreements emerged; all participants aligned on the same numeric predictions and reasoning, emphasizing the high compliance rate (575 of 578 groups) as ensuring reliable reporting. The central tendency and range were identical across all estimates, reflecting strong consensus.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus on all outcome estimates, predicting an employment rate of 84.2% (just below the 86.7% target) and an international collaboration rate of 50%, with 16,000 of 19,000 graduates employed and 9,500 international conference presentations. They attributed these outcomes to the program's substantial research output (57,000 publications) and active international engagement, while noting that competitive job markets and the time needed for job search slightly suppress employment, and that only about half of participants engage in international collaborations. The full-time faculty lecture ratio was consistently estimated at 75%, reflecting program incentives but continued reliance on part-time instructors. All participants agreed that evaluation and consulting feedback loops help improve outcomes gradually, and no disagreements emerged across any estimates or reasoning.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached a strong consensus that the impact phase represents a gradual, long-term outcome of sustained BK21 investment, with all participants predicting a modest improvement in South Korea's global S&T talent competitiveness rank from around 15th to 12th and an increase in high-impact publications per 1000 researchers to about 8.5. Key causal mechanisms discussed include the program's support for 19,000 graduate students and 300 postdocs, producing 57,000 publications and 9,500 international conference presentations, which builds a skilled researcher pipeline; alumni driving innovation and collaboration; and dissemination of best practices via the BK21 webzine and forums. Participants consistently noted that systemic changes in research culture, integrity, and interdisciplinarity take time to manifest, explaining the gradual improvement. No disagreements were expressed; all estimates and reasoning aligned across the five posts.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached full consensus on all input estimates, grounding their reasoning directly in the policy context. They unanimously set total funding allocated at 410,597 million KRW, graduate scholarship slots at 6,400, and postdoc positions at 176. The central causal mechanism was that the national budget and participant numbers from the policy document directly determine Hanyang University's share, with postdoc slots derived from the count of education research groups. No disagreements or alternative estimates emerged across the five participants.
phase_summaries.Activities: All participants reached full consensus on the four activity-level variables, each directly citing the policy context. The total funds disbursed was uniformly estimated at 408,080,000,000 KRW, matching the stated annual budget. Evaluation panels convened were consistently set at 3, corresponding to annual, mid-term, and compliance meetings. Consulting sessions held were uniformly 280, as per the number of groups requesting consulting, and compliance checks executed were 205, targeting the innovative talent training groups. No disagreements or alternative estimates emerged, with all reasoning grounded in the same policy documents.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group consistently estimated outputs based on the policy's stated 6,400 graduate scholarship slots and 176 postdoc positions, with a strong consensus on 9,600 publications (1.5 per participant) and 1,280 international conference presentations (20% participation rate). All participants converged on these values, deriving them from typical research productivity in their fields and the active 2022 cohort, with no disagreement on estimates or reasoning.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached full consensus on all outcome estimates, with every participant predicting an 85% employment rate, a 20% international collaboration rate, a 75% full-time faculty lecture ratio, and counts of 5,500 employed graduates and 1,280 international collaborations. Their reasoning consistently linked these outcomes to prior phase outputs (9,600 publications and 1,280 international conference presentations) and program mechanisms such as conference support, collaboration requirements, and evaluation/consulting feedback loops. The employment rate was anchored to the policy target of 86.7% baseline with a 0.3% annual decline, while the collaboration rate was derived directly from the output estimate of 1,280 presentations out of 6,576 participants. No disagreements or alternative estimates were expressed; all participants explicitly noted alignment with each other's reasoning.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that sustained BK21 investment (410.6 billion KRW, 6,400 graduate slots, 176 postdoc positions) builds a pipeline of highly trained researchers, boosting national innovation capacity and gradually improving South Korea's S&T talent competitiveness rank from around 10-15 to an estimated 12 in 2022. They also agreed that the program's focus on quality and international collaboration, evidenced by 9,600 publications and 1,280 conference presentations, yields about 8.5 high-impact publications per 1,000 researchers, derived from 1.46 publications per participant and a typical top 1% citation rate of 0.5-1%. All participants independently arrived at identical numeric predictions (rank 12, 8.5 publications), with no disagreement on estimates or reasoning. The central tendency and range for both variables are thus uniform: national_st_talent_competitiveness_rank = 12, high_impact_publications_per_1000_researchers = 8.5.

participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 교육연구팀의 자체평가를 철저히 수행하고, 보고서를 기한 내에 종합정보관리시스템으로 제출하여 불이익을 받지 않도록 하는 것이 우선 목표입니다.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group focused on the causal link between the Ministry of Education's total BK21 budget and Hanyang University's allocation, with all participants estimating total funding at 40.8–41.06 billion KRW, graduate scholarship slots at 19,000, and postdoc positions at 1,200. They consistently used the national budget (408,080–410,597 million KRW), monthly stipend rates (master's 700K, doctoral 1.3M, postdoc minimum 3M KRW), and the 10% operating cost cap to derive these estimates. No disagreements emerged, as all narratives aligned on the proportional allocation logic and eligibility constraints.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group focused on the operational execution of BK21 activities, with strong consensus that evaluation panels convene three times annually (annual, mid-term, compliance), consulting sessions total 280 (based on 280 of 578 groups requesting them), and compliance checks target 205 innovative talent training groups. Total funds disbursed estimates ranged from 41,009,360,000 to 41,059,700,000 KRW, with most participants aligning on the full allocated amount of 41,059,700,000 KRW following the 70% March and 30% September schedule. One participant slightly lowered their estimate to 41,009,360,000 KRW, citing potential administrative delays or minor adjustments. No other disagreements emerged, as all reasoning was directly grounded in the policy context.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group agreed that the active cohort in 2022 consisted of 19,000 graduate students and 1,200 postdocs, based on the inputs phase. A consensus emerged that average publication output was about 2 per participant, yielding 38,000 publications, and that about half of participants presented at international conferences, resulting in 9,500 presentations. One participant from a materials science background argued for higher productivity (3 publications per participant, 57,000 publications; 19,000 presentations), citing STEM field norms, but most others from humanities, social sciences, engineering, and life sciences fields supported the lower estimates. The central tendency for publications was 38,000 (range 38,000–57,000) and for international conference presentations was 9,500 (range 9,500–19,000).
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed how international collaboration, employment, and faculty lecture outcomes are shaped by prior program inputs and field-specific conditions. There was consensus among STEM participants that the international collaboration rate is around 30% (5,700 participants), the graduate employment rate is about 85% (16,150 employed), and the full-time faculty lecture ratio is 75%. A dissenting view from an arts/design participant estimated lower rates: 20% collaboration (3,800), 80% employment (15,200), and 70% faculty lecture ratio, citing field differences in collaboration norms and employment pathways. Numeric predictions ranged from 20-30% for collaboration, 80-85% for employment, and 70-75% for faculty lecture ratio, with central tendencies near 30%, 85%, and 75% respectively.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that the impact of BK21 on national S&T talent competitiveness and high-impact publications is gradual and constrained by global competition and time lags. All participants estimated South Korea's rank improving modestly from around 10th to 8th, and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers at 12.5, reflecting that only a fraction of BK21 outputs become top-cited and the denominator includes all national researchers. Key causal mechanisms discussed include sustained investment in graduate talent, dissemination of best practices via the BK21 webzine and forums, and the slow progression of alumni into industry and academia. No disagreements emerged; all estimates and reasoning were aligned, with participants consistently citing the same evidence and rejecting higher estimates due to time lags and global competition.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all three input variables, with every participant estimating total funding at 410,597 million KRW, graduate scholarship slots at 19,000, and postdoc positions at 577. The reasoning consistently cited the policy context: total budget breakdown (355,151 million KRW for education research groups, 52,896 million KRW for graduate innovation support, 2,550 million KRW for evaluation management), stipend levels (700K–1.3M KRW/month), and the assumption that about 60% of the education research group budget funds graduate stipends, yielding ~17,757 slots before rounding to the policy-stated total of 19,000 graduate students. Postdoc estimates were derived from the 577 education research groups and a minimum salary of 3M KRW/month, assuming roughly one postdoc per group. No disagreements emerged; all participants aligned on the same numeric predictions and causal logic.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached strong consensus that total funds disbursed would match the allocated 408,080 million KRW, with three evaluation panels convened annually (annual, mid-term, compliance), 280 consulting sessions held (based on the policy statement that 280 of 578 groups requested consulting), and 205 compliance checks executed (targeting innovative talent training groups). All five participants estimated identical values for all four variables, reflecting unanimous agreement on the direct translation of policy specifications into activity counts. No disagreements emerged. The central tendency is a single point estimate for each variable: 408,080,000,000 KRW, 3 panels, 280 sessions, and 205 checks.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed how outputs from BK21 inputs and activities are shaped by field-specific productivity norms and resource constraints, with STEM fields generally yielding higher publication and presentation rates than social sciences, and regional universities facing tighter budgets and weaker networks than Seoul-based institutions. There was consensus that graduate students produce fewer publications than postdocs, with estimates for graduate student publications per year ranging from 1 to 2.5, and postdoc publications from 2 to 4. Disagreement centered on overall output levels: one participant estimated 57,000 publications and 9,500 presentations, while others estimated 38,000 and 5,700, or 28,500 and 3,800, reflecting differing assumptions about field and regional productivity. The range for publications was 28,500 to 57,000, and for international conference presentations 3,800 to 9,500, with a central tendency around 38,000 publications and 5,700 presentations.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group focused on how inputs (19,000 graduate slots, 577 postdoc positions) and activities (conference support, joint projects) drive outcomes, with evaluation and consulting feedback loops providing gradual improvement. There was strong consensus on all numeric estimates: an 85% graduate employment rate (consistent with the policy target of 0.3% annual increase from an 86.7% baseline), a 30% international collaboration rate (constrained by budget and regional resource disparities), and a 75% full-time faculty lecture ratio (due to continued reliance on part-time lecturers in regional universities). No disagreements emerged, as all participants shared similar reasoning and predictions, with the international collaboration rate being the most debated but ultimately agreed upon due to budget limitations.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that sustained BK21 investment over a decade builds a larger pool of trained researchers and gradually improves research culture through ethics education and collaboration incentives, but structural barriers like regional resource disparities and limited international networks slow national innovation capacity gains. Consensus emerged that South Korea's global S&T talent competitiveness rank would improve only modestly from the current ~15-20 range, with estimates clustering between 12 and 15, and a central tendency around 14. For high-impact publications per 1000 researchers, estimates ranged from 6.2 to 8.5, with most participants converging near 6.5-7.5, reflecting that many researchers are still early-career and that field-specific productivity norms vary, especially between STEM and social sciences. Disagreement centered on the pace of improvement: top-tier institution participants projected higher values (rank 12, 8.5 publications) while regional and social science participants offered lower estimates (rank 14-15, 6.2-6.5 publications), citing tighter budgets, weaker networks, and lower publication rates in their fields.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on the total BK21 program budget of 410,597 million KRW, with all participants using this as the baseline for Hanyang University's allocation. They agreed that Hanyang's share is proportional to its number of education research groups (estimated 30-40 out of 577 total), leading to unanimous estimates of 19,000 graduate scholarship slots and 600 postdoc positions. These numbers were derived by dividing the education research group budget (355,151 million KRW) by average annual stipends (~12.6M KRW) and postdoc minimum salaries (36M KRW annually), after accounting for a 10% operating cost limit. No disagreements emerged, as all participants aligned on the proportional allocation method and the resulting numeric predictions.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached full consensus on all four activity-level variables, with every participant estimating total_funds_disbursed at 408,080,000,000 KRW (the full allocated budget disbursed in two installments), evaluation_panels_convened at 3 (annual, mid-term, and compliance meetings), consulting_sessions_held at 280 (matching the policy-stated number of groups requesting consulting), and compliance_checks_executed at 205 (the number of innovative talent training groups subject to checks). The central causal mechanism was that the National Research Foundation efficiently executes the planned budget and operational activities as specified in the policy context, with no divergence in estimates or reasoning across graduate students, early-career researchers, education research groups, or policy roles.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached a strong consensus on output estimates, with all participants predicting 19,000 graduate students supported, 600 postdocs, 38,000 publications, and 5,700 international conference presentations. Key themes included the influence of regional constraints (e.g., fewer international opportunities for non-Seoul groups) and lower publication productivity in humanities and social sciences compared to STEM fields. Causal mechanisms linked prior-phase funding and activities (e.g., 408 billion KRW budget, consulting sessions) to these outputs, with participants rounding down from theoretical averages to account for non-publishing students and domestic presentations. No disagreements emerged, as all estimates aligned with a shared regional perspective and conservative assumptions.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group focused on how regional constraints and program design elements shape graduate employment and international collaboration outcomes. All participants estimated an employment rate of 81.6% (below the national target of 86.7%) and an international collaboration rate of 30%, citing regional disadvantages in industry networks and global opportunities. Full-time faculty lecture ratio was uniformly set at 85%, reflecting a shared emphasis on stable mentoring. No disagreement emerged, with all estimates converging on the same values (employment rate 81.6%, international collaboration rate 30%, full-time faculty lecture ratio 85%). The aggregate counts for graduate employment and international collaboration were 15,500 and 5,700, respectively.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group consistently estimated South Korea's S&T talent competitiveness rank at 12 and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers at 8.5, reflecting a consensus that sustained BK21 investment yields only incremental national gains due to persistent regional disparities, with Seoul-based groups dominating high-impact research and global networks. They reasoned that while the program trains 19,000 graduate students and 600 postdocs, producing 38,000 publications, structural gaps in basic research and industry-academia collaboration limit improvement from the current rank of ~14-15. The high-impact publication estimate was derived from a raw calculation of 95 per 1000 researchers (assuming 1% of 38,000 publications are top-cited among 4,000 FTE researchers), but rounded down to 8.5 because many BK21 publications are in lower-tier journals, especially from regional and non-STEM fields. Participants agreed that systemic changes in research culture and dissemination of best practices are gradual, with slow adoption in regional universities, leading to conservative predictions. No disagreement emerged; all six participants provided identical estimates and aligned reasoning.

participants.name: 대구
participants.entity_name: Education Research Group Participants and Graduate Innovation Practitioners | KG-Gen-h9flf1qn
participants.representative_type: Education Research Group Participants and Graduate Innovation Practitioners | KG-Gen-h9flf1qn
participants.professional_persona: 대구 소재 국립대학교에서 컴퓨터공학 박사과정을 밟고 있으며, 인공지능 기반 의료 영상 분석 연구를 수행하고 있습니다. 온라인 간담회에서 BK21 장학금 생활 안정성과 연구 몰입도 향상 방안에 대해 발언합니다.

participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 박사 후 해외 포스트닥을 거쳐 국내 의료 AI 스타트업에서 CTO로 성장하는 것이 꿈입니다. BK21이 글로벌 연구 네트워크 형성에 실질적인 도움이 되길 바랍니다.

participants.name: 광주 (vqwzli)
participants.professional_persona: 광주 지역 인문계열 대학에서 국어국문학 교육연구단을 이끄는 석좌교수입니다. 인문학 위기 속에서도 BK21 지원을 통해 대학원생 연구 장려금을 안정적으로 지급하고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 한국 고전문학 및 문화콘텐츠 연구, 인문학 교육과정 혁신, 연구단 성과 확산을 위한 웹진 발간 경험이 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 교육연구단의 우수 사례를 발굴하여 BK21 소개 책자에 수록되도록 하고, 지역 인문학 연구 거점으로 자리매김하는 것이 목표입니다.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group focused on estimating Hanyang University's share of the national BK21 budget, using the total national budget of 408,080 million KRW and Hanyang's proportional number of education research groups (about 35-40 out of 577, or 6-7%). There was strong consensus that Hanyang's total funding is around 28 billion KRW, supporting about 1,800 graduate scholarship slots and 60 postdoc positions, based on average annual stipends of 12 million KRW and postdoc salaries of 36 million KRW. One participant's estimate of 19,000 slots was widely rejected as unrealistic, given it would require over 200 billion KRW. The numeric predictions converged tightly: total_funding_allocated ranged from 28 to 408 billion KRW (with most at 28 billion), graduate_scholarship_slots from 1,800 to 19,000 (most at 1,800), and postdoc_positions from 60 to 1,000 (most at 60).
phase_summaries.Activities: The group focused on translating national-level BK21 activities to Hanyang University, with a strong consensus that total funds disbursed would be 28 billion KRW (full allocation via the standard 70/30 payment schedule) and that 3 evaluation panels would be convened. The main disagreement centered on consulting sessions and compliance checks: one participant mistakenly used national totals (280 and 205, respectively), while the majority correctly applied proportional estimates for Hanyang's ~35 groups, converging on 12 consulting sessions and 10 compliance checks. All participants assumed no major budget reallocations in the first year.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group focused on translating prior-phase funding and slot estimates (1,800 graduate students, 60 postdocs) into research outputs, with a central mechanism being per-capita publication and conference rates. There was strong consensus on the base counts of supported participants. Disagreement centered on publication productivity: one participant estimated 3 publications per graduate student (5,400 total), while the majority estimated 2 per graduate student and 3 per postdoc (3,600 total), citing lower output in humanities and slower research cycles. For international conference presentations, estimates converged on 720 (30% of graduate students, 50% of postdocs), with one outlier at 1,200. The central tendency for publications was 3,600 and for presentations was 720.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus on all outcome estimates, predicting a 30% international collaboration rate (540 participants) and an 80% graduate employment rate (1,440 employed) for Hanyang University's BK21 program in 2022, with an 85% full-time faculty lecture ratio. Key causal mechanisms included Hanyang's strong reputation and network, BK21's employability focus and industry links, conference support and joint project incentives for collaboration, and the program's emphasis on full-time faculty hiring. The evaluation and consulting feedback loops (12 sessions, 10 compliance checks) were seen as helpful for identifying weaknesses but insufficient to overcome structural challenges in humanities fields, which tempered estimates. All participants explicitly rejected overly optimistic predictions above 85% employment or 40% collaboration, citing that even in STEM fields not all students secure jobs quickly or collaborate internationally.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed how sustained BK21 investment builds a critical mass of trained researchers, improving national S&T talent competitiveness, but debated whether benefits concentrate at top universities, limiting national rank improvement. They agreed that research culture changes (integrity training, collaboration incentives) and dissemination of best practices gradually boost high-impact publications, though effects take time. Consensus emerged that the national rank would improve modestly from around 10th, with estimates ranging from 8 to 10 (central tendency 9-10). For high-impact publications per 1000 researchers, predictions ranged from 8.0 to 12.5, with a central tendency around 10.0, reflecting disagreement over STEM vs. humanities field balance and regional disparities.

participants.name: 전북 (l8t867)
participants.entity_name: Education Research Group Participants and Graduate Innovation Practitioners | KG-Gen-l8t867zp
participants.representative_type: Education Research Group Participants and Graduate Innovation Practitioners | KG-Gen-l8t867zp
participants.professional_persona: 전북 전주 소재 사립대학교에서 신소재공학 박사과정을 밟고 있으며, 차세대 배터리 소재 연구를 수행하고 있습니다. 온라인 미팅에서 지역 대학원생의 연구 장비 접근성 향상 방안을 제안합니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 전기화학 분석과 소재 합성에 깊은 지식을 보유하고 있으며, 연구실 내 예산 관리 및 기자재 구매 절차에 능숙합니다. 특허 출원 경험이 2건 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 박사 졸업 후 국내 배터리 제조사 연구소에서 차세대 전지 기술 개발을 주도하는 인재로 성장하고 싶습니다. BK21이 지역 산업과 연계된 연구를 지원해 주길 바랍니다.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all three input variables, estimating Hanyang University's BK21 funding at 40.8 billion KRW (10% of the national 408.08 billion KRW budget), 1,200 active graduate scholarship slots, and 80 postdoc positions for 2022. All participants used the same causal logic: total funding is derived from Hanyang's proportional share of education research groups; scholarship slots are calculated from the remaining budget after 10% operating costs and postdoc salaries, with an active cohort adjustment due to graduation cycles; postdoc positions are limited by group priorities despite sufficient budget. No disagreements emerged, as all five participants independently arrived at identical numeric predictions. The central tendency and range are identical: total_funding_allocated = 40.808 billion KRW, graduate_scholarship_slots = 1,200, postdoc_positions = 80.
phase_summaries.Activities: All participants converged on identical estimates for the four activity variables, drawing directly from the policy document. The total funds disbursed were unanimously set at 40,808,000,000 KRW, based on the two-installment schedule (70% in March, 30% in September) and the allocated budget. Evaluation panels convened were estimated at 3, reflecting the annual, mid-term preparatory, and compliance check meetings. Consulting sessions held were pegged at 280, as explicitly stated for groups that requested consulting, and compliance checks executed at 205, matching the number of groups subject to implementation checks. No disagreements emerged; all narratives emphasized efficient execution by the National Research Foundation and evaluation panels, with no adjustments expected at this phase.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group focused on translating BK21 inputs (1,200 graduate students and 80 postdocs) into outputs, with a central mechanism being per-capita publication and presentation rates adjusted for co-authorship. There was strong consensus on the number of supported participants and the high reporting compliance (575 of 578 groups). Disagreement centered on productivity: one participant estimated 2,400 publications (2.0 per participant) and 480 presentations (0.4 per participant), while the majority estimated 1,800 publications (1.5 per participant) and 360 presentations (0.3 per participant), citing more conservative rates and regional constraints. The range for publications was 1,800–2,400, and for presentations 360–480, with the majority converging on the lower end.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus, estimating the international research collaboration rate at 30% (360 participants) and the graduate employment rate at 85%, with a full-time faculty lecture ratio of 75%. All participants based their estimates on observed lab experiences, noting that about a third of peers engage in international collaborations and that BK21 training boosts employment outcomes. No disagreements emerged, as all numeric predictions were identical across the board.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed how BK21's sustained investment in graduate talent has built a critical mass of skilled researchers, boosting national innovation capacity, but noted that benefits are concentrated in top-tier institutions, limiting overall impact. There was consensus that the program improved research culture and output quality, but disagreement on the magnitude of national-level effects. Estimates for national S&T talent competitiveness rank ranged from 7 to 9, with a central tendency around 8-9; high-impact publications per 1000 researchers ranged from 11.0 to 14.2, centering near 11-12.5. Regional participants argued that uneven distribution of resources and slow adoption of best practices at non-top-tier universities temper national averages, while Seoul-based participants offered more optimistic projections based on their direct experience in high-performing labs.

participants.name: 인천 (fidfbh)

participants.skills_and_expertise: 분자생물학 실험 기술, 연구비 집행 및 정산, 연구 성과 지표 관리에 능숙합니다. 연구단의 고충 상담도 도맡고 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 교육연구단의 성과를 높여 우수 연구단으로 선정되고, 자신도 박사 과정에 진학하여 연구자로서 성장하고 싶습니다.

participants.name: 부산대학교 (cucdpg)
participants.professional_persona: 부산대학교 화학과 박사 과정에 재학 중이며, BK21 혁신인재양성사업 교육연구단의 일원입니다. 촉매 반응 메커니즘을 전공하고 있으며, 매월 130만 원의 장학금을 받아 안정적으로 연구할 수 있었습니다. 학술 대회 참가를 위해 사이비 학회를 피하는 온라인 교육도 성실히 이수했습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 유기금속화학 및 촉매 반응 연구에 강점이 있으며, GC-MS와 NMR 분석에 숙련되어 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 박사 후 국책연구소에서 차세대 에너지 소재 개발에 기여하는 연구자가 되는 것이 목표입니다.

participants.skills_and_expertise: 수질 분석 실험과 환경 모니터링 데이터 처리에 능숙하며, 환경영향평가 관련 자격증을 보유하고 있습니다. 연구 결과를 지역 환경 단체와 공유하는 활동을 하고 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 석사 후 환경 공기업에 입사하여 깨끗한 물 공급 정책 구현에 기여하고 싶습니다. BK21 장학금이 생활비 부담을 덜어줘 연구에 집중할 수 있게 해줍니다.

participants.name: 부산대학교에서
participants.professional_persona: 부산대학교에서 나노광학 박사학위를 취득하고, 현재 동남권 BK21 교육연구팀에서 박사후연구원으로 활동하고 있습니다. 플라즈모닉 나노구조를 이용한 고감도 바이오센서 개발에 집중하고 있습니다.

participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 교육연구단이 지역 혁신을 선도하는 인재 배출소로 자리 잡도록 하고, 연구단장으로서 교육부 장관 표창을 추천받는 것이 꿈입니다.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group focused on estimating Hanyang University's BK21 inputs by scaling down national budget figures, using the number of education research groups as the primary causal mechanism. There was strong consensus that Hanyang hosts around 30 groups, leading to a total funding estimate of approximately 21 billion KRW (range 20-25 billion), graduate scholarship slots of about 1,000 (range 900-1,200), and postdoc positions of about 50 (range 40-60). A minor disagreement emerged over whether Hanyang's share should be slightly higher (25 billion KRW, 1,200 slots) or lower (21 billion KRW, 1,000 slots), with the majority favoring the lower estimates based on a 30-group assumption.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group focused on the practical execution of budget disbursement and evaluation activities, with a strong consensus that the full 21 billion KRW would be disbursed via the standard 70% March and 30% September schedule, and that exactly 3 formal evaluation panel meetings would be convened in 2022. A key disagreement emerged over whether to report national totals or Hanyang-specific proportional estimates for consulting sessions and compliance checks: one participant initially used national figures (280 consulting sessions, 205 compliance checks), while the majority argued for proportional shares based on Hanyang's 30 groups out of 577, yielding 15 consulting sessions and 11 compliance checks. The final numeric predictions converged on total_funds_disbursed: 21 billion KRW, evaluation_panels_convened: 3, consulting_sessions_held: 15, and compliance_checks_executed: 11.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached consensus on the number of graduate students (1,000) and postdocs (50) supported, based on prior phases. Most participants estimated 2,500 publications (range 2,500–3,000) and 350 international conference presentations (range 350–400), with the higher estimates from one participant seen as overly optimistic. The central tendency for publications was 2,500, and for presentations 350, reflecting typical productivity of 2.5 publications per student and one-third of participants presenting annually. Disagreement centered on productivity assumptions, with the majority citing field-specific experience that students often co-author fewer first-authored or high-impact papers and present internationally less frequently. Compliance checks and consulting were noted as ensuring reporting accuracy but not directly boosting output.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group consistently estimated the graduate employment rate at 80% and the international collaboration rate at 35%, with the full-time faculty lecture ratio at 75%, reflecting a consensus that BK21 outcomes show steady but modest progress. They attributed these outcomes to strong STEM demand tempered by job market mismatches, policy pushes for full-time faculty constrained by budgets, and gradual improvements from evaluation feedback loops. No disagreements emerged, as all participants aligned on the same numeric predictions and causal reasoning, grounded in practical experience with their respective education research groups.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that sustained BK21 investment in graduate talent development, combined with systemic cultural changes (e.g., research integrity, collaboration) and dissemination of best practices, would gradually improve South Korea's S&T competitiveness and high-impact publication output. There was strong consensus across all participants, with unanimous agreement on both the causal mechanisms and the numeric estimates. All participants predicted a modest improvement in national S&T talent competitiveness rank from around 10th to 8th, and high-impact publications per 1,000 researchers at 12.5, reflecting the time needed for systemic changes to yield top-cited work. No disagreements were expressed.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group discussed how the national BK21 budget (410.6 billion KRW) is allocated to Hanyang University, with most participants estimating Hanyang's total funding at 4.5 billion KRW based on its status as a major Seoul university hosting 20-30 education research groups. They agreed that graduate scholarship slots are determined by fixed stipend levels (700K-1.3M KRW/month) and a 10% operating cost cap, leading to a consensus estimate of 550 slots for Hanyang. Postdoc positions were consistently estimated at 25, constrained by a minimum salary of 3M KRW/month and typical group sizes of 1-2 postdocs per group. A key disagreement emerged: one participant estimated national-level slots (19,000 graduate slots, 577 postdocs) rather than Hanyang-specific numbers, while others focused on Hanyang alone. Overall, the central tendency for Hanyang was 4.5 billion KRW total funding, 550 graduate slots, and 25 postdoc positions, with a range of 4-5 billion KRW for funding and 500-600 graduate slots.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group focused on the causal mechanisms of fund disbursement, evaluation, consulting, and compliance checks as key activities driving BK21 outcomes. There was strong consensus that total funds disbursed would equal the allocated 4.5 billion KRW, disbursed in two installments, and that evaluation panels would convene 3 times per year. A major disagreement emerged on the scope of consulting sessions and compliance checks: one participant interpreted these as national totals (280 consulting sessions, 205 compliance checks), while the majority scaled them to Hanyang University (approximately 10 consulting sessions and 7 compliance checks). The range of predictions was narrow for funds and panels, but wide for consulting (10–280) and compliance (7–205), with the majority favoring the lower, university-specific estimates.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached strong consensus on all output estimates for Hanyang University, based on the prior phase's allocation of 550 graduate scholarship slots and 25 postdoc positions. Participants consistently applied a productivity rate of 2-3 publications per graduate student and 4-5 per postdoc, yielding a total of 1,650 publications. International conference presentations were uniformly estimated at 20% of publications, or 330 presentations, reflecting budget constraints that favor domestic conferences despite BK21 support. No disagreements emerged, and the high compliance rate in self-evaluation reporting (575 out of 578 groups) was cited to support the reliability of these numbers.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus on all outcome estimates, predicting an international research collaboration rate of 330 participants (20% collaboration rate) and a graduate employment rate of 450 participants (82.5% employment rate), with a full-time faculty lecture ratio of 75%. Key causal mechanisms discussed included budget constraints limiting international travel to about 20% of publications, strong network effects from Seoul-based programs supporting employment despite a declining national trend from the 86.7% target, and BK21's emphasis on full-time faculty for mentoring while some courses still rely on part-time lecturers. Participants consistently referenced evaluation and consulting feedback loops as mechanisms to identify weaknesses and adjust budgets, supporting gradual improvement in outcomes. No disagreements emerged, as all five participants aligned on estimates and reasoning.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that sustained BK21 investment over a decade, combined with dissemination of best practices and spillover effects from top-tier universities, will gradually improve South Korea's S&T talent competitiveness rank from its current 8-10 to 8, and raise the national average of high-impact publications per 1000 researchers to 12.5. All five participants estimated identical values (rank 8, publications 12.5), citing consistent funding, high compliance, strong outputs (e.g., 1,650 publications, 330 international presentations), and systemic cultural changes as key mechanisms. No disagreements emerged; the central tendency and range are both exactly 8 for rank and 12.5 for publications.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group focused on national-level inputs, consistently using the policy's stated total budget of 408,080 million KRW and 19,000 graduate scholarship slots (12,600 master's and 6,400 doctoral students). Postdoc positions were estimated at 1,200 based on 577 education research groups with 2-3 postdocs each. There was full consensus on all three estimates, with participants independently verifying numbers from the policy context. No disagreements emerged, though some noted that Hanyang University's share would be proportional to its number of groups.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached full consensus on all activity estimates, drawing directly from the policy's explicit statements. They agreed that total funds disbursed would be 408,080 million KRW, following the two-installment schedule (70% in March, 30% in September) with no adjustments at this stage. Evaluation panels convened were uniformly set at 3, corresponding to the annual, mid-term, and compliance check meetings. Consulting sessions held were pegged at 280, matching the number of groups that requested consulting, and compliance checks executed were 205, aligning with the innovative talent training groups subject to implementation checks. No disagreements or alternative estimates were presented, indicating a shared understanding that activities strictly follow the documented plan without deviation.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed how BK21 outputs—graduate students supported (19,000), postdocs (1,200), publications, and international conference presentations—are direct results of funding and activities, with productivity varying by field. There was consensus that all funded slots are fully utilized. Disagreement centered on average publications per researcher (ranging from 1 to 3) and the proportion presented at international conferences (10% to 20%), reflecting disciplinary differences in research cycles and collaboration. Central tendency estimates for publications clustered around 28,500–38,000, and for international presentations around 4,275–7,600, with a few lower estimates (19,000 publications, 1,900 presentations) from humanities perspectives.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed how graduate employment and international collaboration rates are influenced by program reputation, training quality, and field-specific factors, with feedback loops from evaluations and consulting providing gradual improvements. There was consensus on an 80% employment rate for 2022, within the policy's 70-75% target range, and a full-time faculty lecture ratio of 85-90%. Disagreement emerged on international collaboration rates, with estimates ranging from 10% (social sciences, humanities, agriculture, and some engineering fields) to 20% (engineering, particularly AI), reflecting disciplinary differences. The aggregate predictions for the simulation targets were 1,900 for international research collaboration rate and 15,200 for graduate employment rate, with the former showing a split between 10% and 20% collaboration rates.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed how the BK21 program's sustained investment over a decade gradually improves national innovation capacity, but effects are modest and field-dependent. Consensus emerged that high-impact publications are rare, with estimates ranging from 1.2 to 4.2 per 1000 researchers, centering around 2.5-3.0, and that national S&T talent competitiveness rank improves incrementally, with estimates from 14 to 18, centering around 16-17. Disagreements centered on the magnitude of impact, with STEM-focused participants predicting higher values (rank 14, 4.2 publications) while social science and regional agriculture participants predicted lower values (rank 18, 1.2 publications). Key causal mechanisms included the concentration of benefits in STEM fields, slow systemic changes in research culture, and the role of dissemination of best practices through webzines and forums.

participants.name: 한양대학교에서
participants.professional_persona: 한양대학교에서 기계공학 박사학위를 받고, 서울 소재 BK21 교육연구팀에서 연구교수로 재직 중입니다. 로봇 매니퓰레이터의 강인 제어 알고리즘 개발에 주력하고 있습니다.

participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 교육연구단의 특성화 모델을 만들어 타 연구단의 모범 사례가 되고, 연구단의 중간 평가에서 좋은 점수를 받아 지원금을 안정적으로 확보하고 싶습니다.

participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 삼성전자나 SK하이닉스의 반도체 연구소에서 차세대 메모리 회로를 설계하는 엔지니어가 꿈입니다.
