participants.name: 충남대학교에서
participants.professional_persona: 충남대학교에서 약학 박사학위를 취득하고, 대전 지역 BK21 교육연구팀에서 박사후연구원으로 재직 중입니다. 단백질 분해 표적화 키메라(PROTAC)를 이용한 항암 신약 후보 물질 개발을 연구하고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 약물 설계 소프트웨어 활용과 세포 기반 약물 효능 평가에 능숙하며, 동물 실험 모델 확립에도 경험이 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 국내 제약사 연구소로 진출하여 실제 신약 개발 파이프라인에 참여하고, 난치암 치료제 개발에 기여하는 것을 희망합니다.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group consistently estimated total funding allocated as the sum of education research group support (408,080 million KRW) and graduate innovation support (52,896 million KRW), totaling 460,976 million KRW. There was consensus on graduate scholarship slots at 19,000, derived from 12,600 future talent and 6,400 innovation talent students, and on postdoc positions at 3,000, based on 577 groups averaging 2-3 postdocs each. No disagreements emerged; all participants aligned on these national-level estimates, though one initial estimate used only the education research group budget (408,080 million KRW) before later posts corrected to the combined total. The central tendency for all three variables was unanimous: total funding 460,976 million KRW, scholarship slots 19,000, and postdoc positions 3,000.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached full consensus on all four activity-level variables. They agreed that total funds disbursed to education research groups in 2022 is 408,080 million KRW (the full annual budget, paid in two installments), with the separate graduate innovation support fund (52,896 million KRW) excluded. They estimated 3 evaluation panels convened (annual graduate innovation evaluation, compliance check for innovative talent training, and one mid-term planning meeting), 280 consulting sessions held (matching the number of groups that requested consulting), and 205 compliance checks executed (targeting the innovative talent training groups). No disagreements were expressed; all participants aligned on the reasoning that budget adjustments based on evaluations occur later, not at this phase.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached consensus on the number of supported participants (19,000 graduate students and 3,000 postdocs) based on prior phases. They agreed that outputs flow from these inputs, with publications and international conference presentations estimated by applying average publication rates per researcher and a fraction presented internationally. Disagreement emerged on the average publication rate: some estimated 1.7 publications per researcher (yielding 38,000 publications and 7,600 presentations), while others argued for 1.5 (yielding 33,000 publications and 6,600 presentations), citing lower productivity in first-year master's students and humanities fields. The central tendency for publications was around 35,500, and for presentations around 7,100, reflecting the two camps.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached full consensus on all outcome estimates, predicting an international research collaboration rate of 6,600 participants (30% rate), a graduate employment rate of 15,000 (78.9% employment rate), and an 85% full-time faculty lecture ratio. They reasoned that outputs like 6,600 international conference presentations directly drive the collaboration rate, while the employment rate reflects a realistic adjustment from the policy target of 86.7% due to declining trends. Program incentives, evaluation feedback loops, and increased reliance on full-time faculty were cited as key causal mechanisms improving training quality and outcomes. No disagreements emerged, as all participants aligned their estimates and logic with the same data and policy context.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that sustained BK21 investment over a decade—19,000 graduate slots and 3,000 postdoc positions annually—builds a critical mass of researchers, improving South Korea's global S&T talent competitiveness rank from around 10th to 8th. All participants estimated high-impact publications at 12.5 per 1000 researchers, derived from 33,000 publications and 22,000 researchers, with 1.25% top-cited papers (412.5 papers) adjusted downward due to lower productivity among master's students and early-stage researchers. Key causal mechanisms included evaluation feedback loops, consulting, dissemination of best practices via webzine and forums, mandatory ethics education, and a 30% international collaboration rate. No disagreement emerged; all six participants independently arrived at identical estimates (rank 8, publications 12.5) through similar reasoning, though some noted the denominator includes less productive early-career researchers. The central tendency for both variables was unanimous: rank 8 and 12.5 high-impact publications per 1000 researchers.

participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 연구단의 중간평가에서 최고 등급을 받아 예산을 증액받고, 궁극적으로 세계적 수준의 재료공학 연구허브로 성장시키는 것이 목표입니다.

participants.name: 충남대학교에서 (nvjf42)
participants.professional_persona: 충남대학교에서 생명과학 석사 과정 중이며, BK21 교육연구단에서 분자생물학을 연구하고 있습니다. 장학금(월 70만 원)은 생활비와 실험 재료 구입에 큰 도움이 됩니다. 학부 때부터 유전자 치료에 관심이 많아 대전 지역 바이오 연구소와 협업을 희망합니다.

participants.professional_persona: 이미경 교수는 부산대학교에서 생명과학 연구단을 운영 중인 중견 연구자입니다. 지역 거점 국립대학의 특성을 살려 산학협력 활성화에 힘쓰고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 분자생물학, 단백체학 연구에 강점이 있으며, 대학-기업 간 공동연구를 주선하는 네트워킹 능력이 뛰어납니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 교육연구단의 자체역량을 강화하여 향후 재선정 평가에서 안정적으로 지원을 유지하고, 지역 생명과학 산업 발전에 기여하고자 합니다.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all three input variables, grounding estimates in explicit policy figures. Total funding allocated was uniformly set at 355,151,000,000 KRW, based on the stated education research group project budget. Graduate scholarship slots were consistently estimated at 19,000, derived from the sum of 12,600 future talent training slots and 6,400 innovation talent training slots. Postdoc positions were estimated at 600 nationally, inferred from the minimum salary of 3 million KRW per month and typical budget allocation ratios, though this figure was not directly stated in the policy. No disagreements emerged, as all participants adopted the same national-level figures and reasoning, despite initial confusion about whether estimates should apply to Hanyang University specifically or the entire program.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached strong consensus that total funds disbursed should equal the full budget of 408,080 million KRW, based on the two-installment schedule (70% in March, 30% in September) being fully executed. All participants agreed on 280 consulting sessions and 205 compliance checks, directly citing policy figures. For evaluation panels, all estimated 3 meetings, reasoning that annual, mid-term preparatory, and compliance panels would each convene once. No numeric disagreements emerged; all predictions were identical across participants.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed that outputs are directly driven by the inputs phase (19,000 graduate slots, 600 postdoc positions) and activities phase (full budget disbursement, 280 consulting sessions, 205 compliance checks). There was consensus on the number of supported participants (19,000 graduate students, 600 postdocs) and that postdoc contributions are minor due to their small count. Disagreement centered on productivity: one participant estimated 3 publications and 1 conference presentation per graduate student, while four others estimated 2 publications and 0.5 conference presentations per student, citing lower output in humanities/social sciences and regional universities. The range for publications was 38,000–57,000 (central tendency ~38,000) and for international conference presentations 9,500–19,000 (central tendency ~9,500).
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed that employment rates are influenced by BK21's reputation and training quality but constrained by a declining trend and tighter job markets, especially for regional and social science graduates, leading to a consensus estimate of 84.2% (below the policy target of 86.7%). International collaboration was seen as supported by conference funding and joint projects, but rates vary by field, with a consensus of 20% (3,800 participants). Full-time faculty lecture ratio was estimated at 75%, reflecting program incentives but persistent reliance on part-time lecturers. All participants agreed on these three outcome estimates, though one participant noted a higher publication output (57,000 vs. group's 38,000) but still converged on the same employment rate due to job market constraints. No disagreement emerged on the numeric predictions, with all estimates identical across participants.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that the BK21 program's sustained investment (408 billion KRW, 19,000 graduate slots, 600 postdocs) will yield only modest improvements in South Korea's S&T talent competitiveness rank, from about 10 to 8, due to the program's limited scale relative to the national R&D ecosystem. They also agreed that high-impact publications per 1000 researchers would be limited in the short term (2021-2022) because systemic cultural changes and alumni career progression take time, and because only a small fraction (about 0.5%) of the program's 38,000 publications become top 1% cited. All participants estimated the same values: national S&T talent competitiveness rank at 8 and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers at 12.5, with no disagreement in either estimates or reasoning. The central tendency and range for both variables are thus identical across all posts.

participants.skills_and_expertise: 세포 배양, 유전자 편집, 단백질 분석 등 분자생물학 실험 기술에 능숙합니다. 논문 작성 및 연구비 제안서 작성 경험이 풍부합니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 향후 3년 안에 독립적인 연구자로 자리 잡아 교수직에 지원하는 것이 목표입니다. BK21 지원을 통해 연구 역량을 더욱 키우고 국제 학술지에 우수 논문을 게재하고 싶습니다.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group primarily used proportional allocation based on the national BK21 budget of 410,597 million KRW for 577 groups and 20 universities, estimating Hanyang University's share at roughly 5% (about 20.5 billion KRW). There was strong consensus on Hanyang's funding (20,529,850,000 KRW), graduate scholarship slots (950), and postdoc positions (29), with all but one participant converging on these figures. The main disagreement came from an early estimate that assumed the entire national budget applied to Hanyang, leading to inflated national-level figures (19,000 slots, 577 postdocs) that contradicted the policy's stated 12,600 annual graduate participants. The central tendency for Hanyang's funding was 20.53 billion KRW, with scholarship slots ranging from 950 to 19,000 (national) and postdoc positions from 29 to 577 (national).
phase_summaries.Activities: The group focused on the operational mechanisms of the BK21 program, emphasizing the disbursement of funds via a 70% March and 30% September schedule and the convening of three evaluation panels (annual, mid-term, compliance). There was consensus on total funds disbursed (20,529,850,000 KRW) and evaluation panels (3), but disagreement on whether consulting sessions and compliance checks were national totals or per-university figures. Some participants used national figures (280 consulting sessions, 205 compliance checks), while others scaled them to Hanyang University (10 consulting sessions, 205 compliance checks). The range for consulting sessions was 10–280, while compliance checks were consistently 205.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed how outputs (publications and conference presentations) are directly driven by the number of supported graduate students and postdocs (979 total at Hanyang), with productivity varying by field. There was consensus on the input numbers (950 graduate slots, 29 postdoc positions) and that early-stage students may not publish, reducing averages. Disagreement centered on productivity: life science participants estimated ~3 publications and 60% conference participation per participant, while social science and engineering participants estimated ~2 publications and 40% participation. Publication estimates ranged from 1,900 to 2,850, and conference presentations from 380 to 570, with a central tendency around 2,375 publications and 475 presentations.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group focused on how BK21 inputs, activities, and outputs translate into employment and international collaboration outcomes, emphasizing causal mechanisms such as conference support, joint research incentives, and evaluation feedback loops. There was strong consensus on all estimates: international collaboration rate at 48% (456 participants), graduate employment rate at 80% (760 employed), and full-time faculty lecture ratio at 75%. No disagreements emerged, with all participants converging on the same numeric predictions based on shared reasoning about policy targets and observed trends.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that the 2021-2022 time window captures only early effects of sustained BK21 investment, leading to a modest improvement in South Korea's S&T talent competitiveness rank from approximately 10th to 8th. All participants estimated the national rank at 8 and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers at 12.5, derived by scaling outputs (1,900-2,850 publications from 979 participants) to the national level and accounting for the rarity of top 1% cited papers. Key causal mechanisms included improved graduate talent quality (80% employment rate, 48% international collaboration rate) and enhanced research culture, though systemic changes in integrity, collaboration, and interdisciplinarity were seen as nascent. No disagreements emerged; all narratives emphasized gradual progress and the need for longer time horizons for full impact.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group discussed the allocation of BK21 inputs for Hanyang University, focusing on proportional distribution from national totals. Consensus emerged that Hanyang's total funding is about 12 billion KRW, with 280 graduate scholarship slots and 15 postdoc positions, based on its share of 20-30 research groups out of 577 nationally. A key disagreement was one estimate using the national total of 408 billion KRW as Hanyang's funding, which others deemed unrealistic. The central tendency for total funding is 12 billion KRW (range 12B-408B), for scholarship slots 280 (range 280-19,000), and for postdocs 15 (range 15-1,200). Causal mechanisms included budget proportions for scholarships (28%) and postdocs (4.5%), and stipend costs (12M KRW per slot annually).
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached full consensus on all activity-level estimates, with every participant predicting 12 billion KRW in total funds disbursed, 3 evaluation panels convened, 140 consulting sessions held, and 20 compliance checks executed. The reasoning consistently centered on proportional allocation from the national budget of 408 billion KRW across 577 education research groups, the standard 70% March and 30% September disbursement schedule, and the policy-mandated three evaluation panel meetings per year. Consulting and compliance estimates were derived from national ratios (280 of 578 groups requesting consulting, 205 groups subject to compliance checks) applied to Hanyang University's approximately 20-30 groups. No disagreements emerged, as all participants aligned on the same numeric values and causal logic linking budget allocation to activity execution.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed that outputs are directly driven by the number of supported graduate students and postdocs, with typical research productivity of about 3 publications per participant per year in STEM fields and about 2 in humanities, and international conference participation around 20% of participants. There was strong consensus on the input numbers (280 graduate students, 15 postdocs) and on the 12 billion KRW budget allocation, leading most participants to estimate 840 publications and 168 presentations. One participant from humanities disagreed, estimating 560 publications and 112 presentations due to lower field-specific output rates. The range for publications was 560–840, with a central tendency of 840; for presentations, the range was 112–168, with a central tendency of 168.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed how prior inputs (280 graduate slots, 12 billion KRW funding) and outputs (840 publications, 168 international conference presentations) directly shape outcomes, with causal mechanisms including industry partnerships, conference support, and evaluation feedback loops. There was strong consensus on an 85% employment rate (238/280 graduates) and 20% international collaboration rate (56/280 participants), with a 75% full-time faculty lecture ratio, based on STEM and business perspectives. A humanities stakeholder disagreed, estimating lower rates of 80% employment (224/280), 15% collaboration (42/280), and 70% faculty lecture ratio, citing tighter job markets and less frequent international co-authorship in their field. The range for employment rate was 80-85% (224-238 graduates), for collaboration rate 15-20% (42-56 participants), and for faculty lecture ratio 70-75%, with the majority favoring the higher end.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that sustained BK21 investment over a decade gradually improves South Korea's S&T talent competitiveness rank and high-impact publication rates, but emphasized that the program is just one factor among many, with global competition and slow systemic changes limiting the pace of improvement. There was consensus that the national rank would improve modestly from around 10th to 9th, and that high-impact publications per 1000 researchers would rise from about 4.2 to between 8.5 and 9.0, reflecting a realistic, incremental trajectory. A single participant was more optimistic, predicting a rank of 8 and 12.5 publications per 1000 researchers, citing the program's scale and cultural dissemination, but the majority viewed this as too optimistic given the broader ecosystem constraints. The central tendency for rank was 9, and for high-impact publications per 1000 researchers was 8.5–9.0.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached a strong consensus on the total national funding allocated (408,080 million KRW) and the national estimates for graduate scholarship slots (19,000) and postdoc positions (1,000), with all participants agreeing on these figures. Key causal mechanisms discussed include the fixed stipend levels (master's 700K, doctoral 1.3M, doctoral candidate 1M KRW per month) and postdoc minimum salary (3M KRW per month) directly determining slot counts, the 10% cap on operating costs ensuring most funds go to personnel, and the national participant numbers (12,600 future talent + 6,400 innovation talent) aligning with the slot estimate. No disagreement emerged on the national-level predictions, but participants consistently noted that Hanyang University's share would be smaller, estimating roughly 200-300 graduate students and 10-20 postdocs locally. The central tendency for all three variables was unanimous: total_funding_allocated = 408,080,000,000 KRW, graduate_scholarship_slots = 19,000, and postdoc_positions = 1,000.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached strong consensus that total_funds_disbursed would be exactly 408,080,000,000 KRW, consulting_sessions_held would be 280, and compliance_checks_executed would be 205, all directly anchored to explicit policy figures. For evaluation_panels_convened, all participants estimated 2, but there was minor disagreement about whether the second panel was for mid-term evaluation preparation or compliance oversight, with most reasoning that one panel covers the annual evaluation and the other addresses mid-term preparation or compliance. The central tendency across all numeric predictions was identical, reflecting unanimous reliance on the policy's stated numbers and schedules.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached full consensus on all output estimates, with every participant predicting 250 graduate students supported, 15 postdocs, 750 publications, and 200 international conference presentations. All participants derived these numbers by scaling down national Inputs-phase figures (19,000 graduate slots, 1,000 postdoc positions) to Hanyang University's proportional share, then applying a uniform productivity rate of 2-3 publications and 0.8 international presentations per graduate student per year. The high compliance rate (575 out of 578 groups submitting self-evaluation reports) was cited as ensuring complete counting of supported participants. No disagreements or alternative estimates were presented.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group consistently linked outcomes to prior phases, emphasizing that national funding, timely disbursement, evaluation panels, and consulting sessions improved program design and mentoring, which in turn supported employment and collaboration. There was full consensus on all estimates: employment rate at 72.5% (based on policy targets adjusted for a declining trend), international collaboration rate at 30%, full-time faculty lecture ratio at 85%, and counts of 75 for international collaboration and 170 for graduate employment (derived from 250 graduate students and 15 postdocs, with adjustments for postdoc transitions). No disagreements emerged, as all participants aligned their reasoning with the same policy context and group experiences.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached full consensus, estimating South Korea's S&T talent competitiveness rank at 15 and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers at 12.5 for 2022. They attributed these outcomes to sustained BK21 investment (408,080 million KRW annually) over a decade, which produces a skilled workforce and drives R&D in key fields. Systemic changes in research culture—integrity, collaboration, interdisciplinarity—and dissemination of best practices via the BK21 webzine and forums were seen as mechanisms raising the overall S&T ecosystem's performance. The publication estimate was derived from scaling group-level output (750 publications, ~5% top-cited, 250 researchers) to the national level. No disagreements or alternative estimates were presented.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group discussed the allocation of BK21 inputs, focusing on how total funding, graduate scholarship slots, and postdoc positions are determined by policy constraints such as fixed stipend levels, a 10% operating cost cap, and minimum postdoc salaries. There was strong consensus on all three estimates: total funding at 355,151 million KRW, graduate scholarship slots at 200, and postdoc positions at 50. No disagreements emerged, as all participants converged on the same numbers and reasoning, grounded in the policy context and typical group budget calculations. The central tendency and range were identical across all predictions.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached full consensus on all activity-level estimates, with every participant predicting identical values: total funds disbursed at 355,151 million KRW, 3 evaluation panels convened, 280 consulting sessions held, and 205 compliance checks executed. Reasoning centered on the policy's fixed two-installment payment schedule (70% in March, 30% in September) and explicit program specifications for evaluation, consulting, and compliance activities. No disagreements emerged, as all stakeholders—including education research groups, graduate students, early-career researchers, and a policy role—relied on the same policy documents and causal logic. The central tendency is a single point estimate for each variable, reflecting strong alignment on the deterministic nature of these activities within the BK21 program framework.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group agreed that the outputs phase directly follows from inputs and activities, with all 200 graduate slots and 50 postdoc positions fully utilized. Most participants estimated 500 publications (range 500–600) and 120 international conference presentations (range 120–160), citing conservative publication averages of 2 per graduate student and 3 per postdoc, and limited travel funding and pandemic effects. One participant estimated higher outputs (600 publications, 160 presentations) based on higher productivity assumptions, while others noted longer research timelines in medical and clinical fields as a reason for lower estimates. Consensus centered on the causal mechanism that full fund disbursement and compliance ensure active participation, but productivity varies by field and stage of research.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached a strong consensus on all outcome estimates, predicting an 85% graduate employment rate, 40% international collaboration rate, and 75% full-time faculty lecture ratio, with no divergence in numeric predictions. Key causal mechanisms discussed included the positive impact of fully funded graduate slots and postdoc positions, 280 consulting sessions, and 205 compliance checks on program quality and outcomes. Participants noted that field-specific factors (e.g., clinical training in medicine, regional job markets in social sciences) and the lingering effects of the pandemic on international travel moderated collaboration rates, while the policy's emphasis on full-time faculty was balanced by the use of part-time lecturers for specialized topics. The central tendency of all predictions was identical, with no range or disagreement observed.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that sustained BK21 investment over a decade—funding 200 graduate slots and 50 postdoc positions annually, along with 280 consulting sessions and 205 compliance checks—has built a critical mass of highly trained researchers who now occupy senior roles, driving national innovation capacity and improving the S&T ecosystem. They agreed that dissemination of best practices through the BK21 webzine and introduction booklets, combined with an emphasis on quality over quantity, has gradually increased high-impact publications. All participants estimated the same values: national S&T talent competitiveness rank at 8th and high-impact publications at 12.5 per 1000 researchers in 2022. No disagreement emerged on numeric estimates, though some regional participants noted that high-impact publications are concentrated in metropolitan universities, suggesting the national average may be slightly lower than what a top-tier institution might expect. The central tendency is a rank of 8 and 12.5 publications, reflecting steady but moderate improvement behind top nations like the US, China, and Germany.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on national-level input estimates, all converging on total_funding_allocated of 410.597 billion KRW, graduate_scholarship_slots of 19,000, and postdoc_positions of 1,200. These values were derived directly from the policy context: the total budget includes 355.151 billion KRW for education research groups, 52.896 billion KRW for graduate innovation, and 2.55 billion KRW for evaluation; scholarship slots sum from 12,600 future talent and 6,400 innovative talent slots; postdoc positions were estimated by multiplying 577 groups by an average of about 2 postdocs per group, consistent with the minimum salary requirement. No disagreements emerged, as all participants relied on the same explicit policy figures and logical extrapolations, though some initially considered university-specific allocations before deferring to national totals.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group consistently estimated that total funds disbursed would match the allocated budget of 408,080 million KRW, following the standard 70% March and 30% September payment schedule. There was consensus on 3 evaluation panel meetings (annual, graduate innovation, and compliance), 280 consulting sessions (based on policy stating 280 of 578 groups received consulting), and 205 compliance checks (targeting innovative talent training groups). No disagreements emerged, as all participants provided identical numeric predictions and reasoning, citing the same policy evidence. The central tendency for all four variables was uniform across all posts.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group consistently estimated outputs based on the established 19,000 graduate slots and 1,200 postdoc positions, with a consensus that each supported researcher produces about 3 publications per year, yielding 57,000 total publications, and that 20% of these are presented at international conferences, resulting in 11,400 presentations. All participants agreed on these numbers, citing the high compliance rate (575 out of 578 groups submitting self-evaluations) as evidence of reliable reporting. There were no disagreements; the estimates were uniform across all stakeholders. The central tendency for all predictions was exactly 19,000 graduate students supported, 1,200 postdocs, 57,000 publications, and 11,400 international conference presentations.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus that the prior phases' establishment of 19,000 graduate slots and 1,200 postdoc positions, along with full fund disbursement and high compliance, provided a solid foundation for outcomes. All participants estimated the graduate employment rate at 85% and the international collaboration rate at 20%, citing program support for international co-authorship and conferences, strong sector demand, and the 75% full-time faculty lecture ratio as consistent with their experiences. No disagreements emerged; the central tendency for all numeric predictions was identical across participants, reflecting a shared view of stable, moderately positive outcomes.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group consistently reasoned that the BK21 program's sustained investment in graduate talent development would yield modest early-stage impacts by 2022, with all participants converging on a national S&T talent competitiveness rank of 8 and 15.0 high-impact publications per 1000 researchers. Key causal mechanisms included the dissemination of best practices through BK21 webzines and forums, fostering a culture of integrity and collaboration, though participants acknowledged that systemic changes require more time. No disagreements emerged; all estimates were identical, reflecting strong consensus. The central tendency for both variables was unanimous: rank 8 and 15.0 publications per 1000 researchers.

participants.professional_persona: 건축공학 박사 학위를 취득하고 부산 지역 대학의 BK21 교육연구단에서 연구 교수로 재직 중입니다. 친환경 건축 재료 및 스마트 건축 시스템 개발을 연구하며, 학회 활동도 활발히 하고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 건축물 에너지 시뮬레이션, BIM(Building Information Modeling) 기술, 친환경 인증 평가에 전문성을 가지고 있습니다. 실제 건축 프로젝트에 적용 가능한 실용적 연구를 선호합니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 글로벌 건축 엔지니어링 회사에서 스마트 시티 프로젝트를 주도하는 전문가가 되는 것이 꿈입니다. BK21 지원을 통해 국제 공동 연구와 기술 이전 경험을 쌓고 싶습니다.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached full consensus on all three input variables, using national-level figures from the policy document as proxies for Hanyang University's program. They consistently estimated total_funding_allocated at 408,080,000,000 KRW, graduate_scholarship_slots at 19,000 (summing 12,600 future talent and 6,400 innovative talent slots), and postdoc_positions at 577 (one per education research group). All participants acknowledged that these national aggregates likely overstate Hanyang's actual share due to competition and budget constraints, but no alternative university-specific data was available. The causal reasoning was uniform: funding and slots directly enable graduate student and postdoc support, which in turn drives employment and collaboration outcomes.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached full consensus on all four activity estimates, with every participant predicting total funds disbursed of 408,080,000,000 KRW, 3 evaluation panels convened, 280 consulting sessions held, and 205 compliance checks executed. All participants reasoned that the policy explicitly mandates full two-installment fund disbursement (70% in March, 30% in September) and directly specifies the counts for consulting (280 groups) and compliance checks (205 groups). The unanimous interpretation of three distinct evaluation events—annual, mid-term, and compliance—drove the panel estimate. No disagreements emerged, and the central tendency for each variable was identical across all predictions.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group consistently estimated 19,000 graduate students supported and 577 postdoc researchers supported, based on national policy inputs. Most participants estimated 57,000 publications and 9,500 international conference presentations, reasoning that each graduate student produces about 2 publications per year and each postdoc about 4, with half of participants presenting at conferences. One participant estimated 38,000 publications, citing a more conservative view on productivity and collaborative overlap. The central tendency for publications was 57,000, with a range of 38,000 to 57,000, and for presentations, consensus at 9,500. Key causal mechanisms included the direct translation of funding and compliance activities into research outputs, with high reporting compliance (575 of 578 groups) ensuring data reliability.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached full consensus on all outcome estimates, predicting 9,500 participants involved in international research collaboration (50% rate) and 16,000 employed graduates (84.2% employment rate) out of 19,000 total participants, with a full-time faculty lecture ratio of 75%. Key causal mechanisms included the direct impact of national-level inputs (19,000 graduate slots, 577 postdoc positions) and full fund disbursement, moderated by evaluation and consulting feedback loops that drive gradual improvements. All participants cited the policy's declining employment trend since BK21 Plus and the aspirational 86.7% target to justify the 84.2% estimate, while international collaboration and faculty lecture ratios were grounded in observed practice and policy emphasis. No disagreements emerged; estimates were identical across all five participants from diverse stakeholder types (graduate students, early-career researchers, and an education research group leader).
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that sustained BK21 investment in graduate stipends, postdoc salaries, and a culture of research integrity and collaboration gradually improves national S&T talent competitiveness and high-impact publication output, though effects are modest and take time. There was consensus that South Korea's rank is around 5-7, with a central tendency of 5-6, and that high-impact publications per 1000 researchers are in the 10.0-12.5 range. Disagreement emerged between those from top-tier institutions, who estimated a rank of 5 and 12.5 publications, and those from regional universities, who estimated a rank of 6 and 10.0 publications, citing uneven productivity gains and the early 2022 time window. The range of predictions was 5-6 for rank and 10.0-12.5 for publications, with a central tendency of about 5.5 and 11.0, respectively.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The deliberation centered on interpreting the BK21 program's inputs at different levels: per education research group versus university-wide. A consensus emerged that the total funding allocated is 408,080,000,000 KRW, and that graduate scholarship slots number 12,600, both directly from the policy document. Disagreement arose over postdoc positions, with one participant estimating 20 per group while others estimated 2,000 university-wide, reflecting a scale mismatch. The range for postdoc positions was 20 to 2,000, with the majority favoring the higher figure based on the total budget and minimum salary constraints.
phase_summaries.Activities: All participants agreed that activities would follow the BK21 policy plan exactly, with total funds disbursed at 408,080,000,000 KRW, 3 evaluation panels convened, 280 consulting sessions held, and 205 compliance checks executed. There was no disagreement; every stakeholder cited the same policy documents and reasoning, focusing on the operational execution of planned activities as the key causal mechanism. The central tendency and range for each variable were identical across all predictions.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed outputs as direct results of funded activities, with causal mechanisms linking participant counts (graduate students and postdocs) to publication and presentation outputs. There was consensus on supporting 12,600 graduate students and 2,000 postdocs per year. Disagreement centered on publication and presentation rates: some estimated 2.5 publications per participant (total 31,500) and 20% presentation rate (6,300), while others estimated 2.0 publications (29,200) and 50% presentation rate (7,300). Numeric predictions ranged from 29,200 to 31,500 for publications and 6,300 to 7,300 for international conference presentations.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached full consensus on all outcome estimates, with predictions centered on an 87% graduate employment rate (reflecting a 0.3%p annual increase from the 86.7% baseline), a 45% international collaboration rate (6,700 participants), and an 85% full-time faculty lecture ratio. Key causal mechanisms included the program's strong outputs (29,200–31,500 publications and 6,300–7,300 conference presentations), which were seen as directly driving collaboration rates, while the employment rate was tied to the program's reputation and training quality. No disagreements emerged, as all participants aligned their reasoning with policy targets and prior-phase outputs, citing evaluation and consulting feedback loops as reinforcing positive trends.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that sustained BK21 investment (408 billion KRW annually, supporting 12,600 graduate students and 2,000 postdocs) will gradually improve South Korea's S&T talent competitiveness rank from around 10th to 8th and raise high-impact publications from a baseline of about 10 to 12.5 per 1000 researchers over a decade. Key causal mechanisms included international collaboration (45% rate), high publication output (29,200–31,500 per year), dissemination of best practices via webzine and forums, and systemic cultural changes (full-time faculty mentoring at 85% lecture ratio, reduced predatory conferences). All participants estimated identical values (rank: 8, publications: 12.5) and agreed the 2021–2022 window captures early trends rather than full long-term outcomes, with no disagreement on estimates or reasoning.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group discussed how total funding allocated to education research groups is influenced by field (STEM vs. social sciences), university location (regional priority vs. non-regional), and group performance, with graduate scholarship slots and postdoc positions constrained by stipend minimums (master's 700K, doctoral candidate 1M, doctoral 1.3M KRW/month), postdoc salaries (3M KRW/month), and a 10% operating cost cap. There was consensus that funding per group varies around a national average of ~707 million KRW, with STEM groups at specialized or regional universities receiving higher allocations. Disagreements centered on the magnitude of funding for specific groups, with estimates ranging from 1.1 to 1.4 billion KRW, graduate scholarship slots from 40 to 55, and postdoc positions from 4 to 7. The central tendency across predictions was approximately 1.26 billion KRW in funding, 48 slots, and 6 postdocs.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed the disbursement of funds via a 70% March and 30% September schedule, with evaluation panels convened three times per year (annual, mid-term, compliance), consulting sessions held for 280 out of 578 groups, and compliance checks executed for 205 innovative talent training groups. There was consensus on the number of evaluation panels (3), consulting sessions (280), and compliance checks (205), but disagreement on total funds disbursed, ranging from 1.1 billion to 1.4 billion KRW, with a central tendency around 1.2-1.3 billion KRW. Disagreements stemmed from differences in field (STEM vs. social sciences), university type (specialized vs. regional), and regional priority, with higher estimates for STEM groups at specialized universities in Daejeon and lower estimates for social science groups at regional universities.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed how outputs are driven by the number of supported graduate students and postdocs, with publication and conference presentation rates varying by field and university resources. There was consensus that STEM fields produce more publications and international presentations than social sciences, and that larger groups with more postdocs yield higher outputs. Disagreement centered on the exact productivity per researcher, with engineering groups estimating higher rates (2-4 publications per person) than social science (1-2). Publication estimates ranged from 80 to 200, international presentations from 15 to 40, graduate students from 40 to 55, and postdocs from 4 to 7, with central tendencies around 145 publications, 30 presentations, 48 graduate students, and 6 postdocs.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed how regional location, field of study, and resource levels (funding, graduate slots, postdocs) causally influence employment and international collaboration rates. Consensus emerged that full-time faculty lecture ratios are high (80-90%) and that evaluation feedback loops have only incremental short-term impacts. Disagreements centered on the magnitude of employment and collaboration rates, with STEM groups in metropolitan areas (Seoul, Daejeon) predicting higher outcomes than social science or regional groups. Employment rate predictions ranged from 75% to 82%, international collaboration rates from 20% to 35%, with central tendencies around 78-80% and 25-30%, respectively.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed how sustained BK21 investment over a decade, including graduate stipends and postdoc salaries, builds a skilled researcher pipeline that gradually improves South Korea's global S&T talent competitiveness rank and increases the share of high-impact publications, though global competition and regional constraints limit gains. There was consensus that improvements are incremental, with all participants agreeing the rank would improve modestly from around 10th to between 8th and 10th, and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers would rise to between 9.8 and 12.5. Disagreements centered on the magnitude of impact, with STEM groups at specialized universities (e.g., in Daejeon) predicting stronger national effects (rank 8, 12.5 publications) compared to regional or social science groups (rank 10, 9.8–10.5 publications), reflecting differences in research output, international collaboration, and dissemination of best practices. The range of predictions was rank 8–10 for competitiveness and 9.8–12.5 for high-impact publications, with a central tendency around rank 9 and 11 publications.

participants.name: 서울
participants.professional_persona: 서울 소재 명문대학에서 화학공학을 전공한 석사과정생으로, 학부 시절부터 촉매 반응 연구에 관심이 많았습니다. 현재 BK21 교육연구단에 소속되어 차세대 친환경 촉매 개발 연구를 수행하며 월 70만 원의 연구장학금을 받고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 실험 설계 및 촉매 특성 분석에 능숙하며, 논문 작성과 연구 발표 경험이 풍부합니다. 또한 Python을 활용한 데이터 분석에도 관심이 많습니다.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on total funding allocated to Hanyang University's BK21 program, with all participants estimating 410,597 million KRW, reflecting the fixed national budget. For graduate scholarship slots, there was near-unanimous agreement on approximately 600 slots for Hanyang, derived from proportional allocation based on the university's 10-15 education research groups out of 577 nationally, with a range of 500-700 slots. Postdoc positions were consistently estimated at 30-50, with a central tendency of 40, constrained by the minimum salary of 3 million KRW per month. A key disagreement arose when one participant initially estimated 19,000 graduate slots for Hanyang, which others correctly identified as the national total, not Hanyang's share. The causal mechanisms discussed included budget proportionality to group count, stipend levels capping student numbers, and salary costs limiting postdoc positions.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached strong consensus that total funds disbursed would match the allocated budget of 410,597 million KRW, as the two-installment schedule is a fixed mechanism with no expected under-disbursement. They also agreed that evaluation panels convened would be 3 (annual, mid-term, compliance) and compliance checks executed would be 205, targeting innovative talent training groups. The main disagreement centered on consulting sessions held: one participant used the national figure of 280, while the majority estimated a proportional share for Hanyang University's groups, converging on 12. The central tendency for consulting sessions was 12, with a range from 12 to 280. Causal mechanisms highlighted included the evaluation cycle triggering panel meetings, voluntary consulting requests, and mandatory compliance checks.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed outputs as direct results of funding, with 600 graduate students and 40 postdocs as active participants. There was consensus that graduate students produce 2-3 publications per year and postdocs 3-5, but disagreement on averages due to field differences (engineering vs. humanities/social sciences). Publication estimates ranged from 1200 to 1800, with a central tendency around 1500-1600. International conference presentation estimates ranged from 200 to 300, with a central tendency near 250-280, reflecting budget and field constraints. The group agreed that compliance checks ensure accurate reporting, but field-specific productivity differences drove the numeric divergence.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed outcomes as resulting from prior phases, focusing on employment and international collaboration rates. Consensus emerged that the graduate employment rate aligns with the policy target of 70-75%, with estimates ranging from 72% to 75%, and that the full-time faculty lecture ratio is high (85-90%) due to program incentives. Disagreement centered on field-specific differences: engineering participants argued for higher employment (75%) and international collaboration rates (40%), while social science participants estimated more modest rates (72% and 35%, respectively). The international collaboration rate estimates ranged from 35% to 40%, reflecting field norms and budget constraints. The group agreed that evaluation and consulting feedback loops help improve these indicators over time.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that the BK21 program's impact on national S&T talent competitiveness and high-impact publications is modest due to its limited scale relative to the national workforce, with causal mechanisms including sustained investment in graduate stipends, dissemination of best practices, and gradual cultural shifts. Consensus emerged that the national rank would improve modestly from the current ~18-20 to around 15-16, and that high-impact publications per 1000 researchers would increase, driven by top-tier engineering groups. Disagreement centered on the magnitude of publication impact, with estimates ranging from 7.0 to 8.5, reflecting differing views on the influence of lower-productivity fields like social sciences versus engineering. The central tendency for national rank was 15-16, and for high-impact publications, the range was 7.0-8.5, with most estimates clustering around 7.0-8.0.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group focused on the total project budget for all education research groups nationwide (355,151,000,000 KRW) as the primary input, with a consensus that graduate scholarship slots are around 200 and postdoc positions around 20. These estimates are based on fixed monthly stipend levels (master's 700K, doctoral 1.3M, doctoral candidate 1M KRW), the 10% operating cost limit, and the higher minimum salary for postdocs (3M KRW per month). No disagreements emerged; all participants aligned on the same numeric predictions and reasoning, reflecting a shared understanding of the policy's resource logic and budget constraints.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached full consensus on all four activity-level variables, estimating that total funds disbursed would be 355,151,000,000 KRW (the full project budget for education research groups, disbursed 70% in March and 30% in September), three evaluation panels would be convened (annual, mid-term, and compliance), 280 consulting sessions would be held (matching the number of groups that requested them), and 205 compliance checks would be executed (targeting the Innovative Talent Training groups). The key causal mechanism discussed was that the two-installment payment schedule aligns with the academic year, enabling groups to plan spending, while evaluations, consulting, and compliance checks are standard annual cycles that monitor performance and ensure accountability. No disagreements emerged across any of the estimates or underlying reasoning.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group consistently estimated outputs based on fixed inputs of 200 graduate slots and 20 postdoc positions, with a central tendency of 550 publications (range 550-600) and 85 international conference presentations (range 80-90). Key themes included the impact of co-authorship reducing unique publication counts and the role of mandatory online education on predatory conferences and compliance checks in ensuring legitimate reporting. Consensus emerged on the average productivity of 2.5 publications per graduate student and 3.5 per postdoc, and that about 40% of participants attend international conferences annually. Minor disagreement centered on whether co-authorship overlap or more active participation would shift publication and presentation estimates slightly higher or lower.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus that the program's internationalization incentives (conference support, mandatory education) and strong industry connections drive high collaboration and employment outcomes. All participants estimated an employment rate of 82.5% and an international collaboration rate of 38%, with full-time faculty lecture ratio at 75%. No disagreements were expressed; the reasoning consistently linked these outcomes to program design, evaluation feedback loops, and practical training. The predicted values for the simulation targets were 85 for international research collaboration rate and 170 for graduate employment rate, reflecting a shared optimistic but realistic view.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached full consensus that sustained BK21 investment (200 graduate slots and 20 postdoc positions per group across 578 groups) will improve South Korea's S&T talent competitiveness rank from ~10th to 8th and yield 12.5 high-impact publications per 1000 researchers. Key causal mechanisms included: building a critical mass of trained researchers, fostering research integrity and international collaboration through mandatory online education and conference support, and disseminating best practices via the BK21 webzine and introduction booklet. All participants estimated national_st_talent_competitiveness_rank at 8 and high_impact_publications_per_1000_researchers at 12.5, with no disagreement.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached full consensus on all three input variables, with every participant estimating total funding allocated at 355,151 million KRW, graduate scholarship slots at 19,000, and postdoc positions at 1,200. The central causal mechanism was that the total funding directly supports graduate scholarships and postdoc salaries, with scholarship slots derived from national totals (12,600 future talent plus 6,400 innovative talent students) and postdoc positions estimated from the minimum salary of 3 million KRW per month and typical group budget constraints. No disagreements emerged, as all participants independently arrived at identical values using the same policy context evidence. The numeric predictions were uniform, with no range across estimates.
phase_summaries.Activities: All participants agreed that total funds disbursed equal the full annual budget of 408,080 million KRW, disbursed in two installments (70% in March, 30% in September). There was consensus that three formal evaluation panel meetings occur in 2022 (annual and compliance, with mid-term planned for 2023), 280 consulting sessions are held (based on 280 groups requesting them), and 205 compliance checks are executed (on innovative talent training groups). No disagreements emerged; all estimates were identical across participants, reflecting a shared understanding of the policy context. The central tendency and range for all four variables are uniform: total_funds_disbursed = 408,080,000,000 KRW; evaluation_panels_convened = 3; consulting_sessions_held = 280; compliance_checks_executed = 205.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached full consensus on all output estimates, reasoning that the funded slots (19,000 graduate students and 1,200 postdocs) directly translate to the number of supported individuals. They estimated an average of 3 publications per participant per year, yielding 57,000 total publications after accounting for less active participants, and that about 20% of participants present at an international conference annually, resulting in 3,800 presentations. All participants independently arrived at identical numeric predictions, reflecting a shared understanding of typical productivity rates across different institution types and fields.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: All participants converged on identical estimates, indicating strong consensus. They reasoned that the outputs from prior phases—57,000 publications and 3,800 international conference presentations—directly drive the international collaboration rate, estimated at 20% (3,800 collaborations). The graduate employment rate was set at 78.9% (15,000 employed out of 19,000 graduates), reflecting a declining trend from the 86.7% target. The full-time faculty lecture ratio was uniformly estimated at 85%, supported by program incentives and observed practices. No disagreements emerged; all predictions were identical across all variables.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that sustained BK21 investment over a decade, supporting 19,000 graduate students and 1,200 postdocs annually, gradually improves the national S&T ecosystem through increased high-impact publications and a shift toward research integrity and collaboration. They agreed that regional disparities between top-tier institutions (Seoul, KAIST) and regional universities limit the pace of improvement, leading to consensus on modest gains: national S&T talent competitiveness rank improves from about 10th to 8th, and high-impact publications per 1000 researchers rise from about 10 to 12.5. No disagreement was expressed; all participants converged on these estimates and causal reasoning.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group focused on estimating Hanyang University's share of the national BK21 budget, with most participants converging on a total funding allocation of 12 billion KRW, 450 graduate scholarship slots, and 30 postdoc positions. Consensus emerged that Hanyang's share is proportional to its number of education research groups (estimated at 10-15 out of 577 nationally), leading to a modest fraction of the total 355,151 million KRW education research group budget. A key disagreement centered on the scale of Hanyang's share: one participant assumed Hanyang holds about 10% of national slots, while others argued for a more conservative 2-3% based on the distribution across 68 universities. The central tendency for predictions was 12 billion KRW for funding, 450 slots for graduate scholarships, and 30 postdoc positions, with one outlier suggesting 1,900 slots and 120 postdocs nationally but not per university. Causal reasoning linked budget allocation to stipend costs (average 12 million KRW per student per year), operating cost limits (10%), and postdoc salaries (36 million KRW per year), with most participants deducting these from the total to derive slot counts.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed the operational activities of the BK21 program, focusing on fund disbursement, evaluation panels, consulting sessions, and compliance checks. There was consensus that total funds disbursed equal the allocated amount (12 billion KRW) due to efficient NRF transfers, and that three evaluation panels are convened (annual, mid-term, compliance). Disagreement arose over consulting sessions and compliance checks: one participant used national totals (280 sessions, 205 checks) while others applied proportional estimates for Hanyang's 12 groups, yielding 6 sessions and 4 checks. The range for consulting sessions was 6–280, for compliance checks 4–205, with central tendencies of 6 and 4 respectively.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed how BK21 outputs—graduate students and postdocs supported, publications, and international conference presentations—are driven by the number of funded slots (consensus on 450 graduate students and 30 postdocs) and field-specific productivity norms. There was consensus that all slots are filled due to high demand. Disagreement centered on research output: humanities/social sciences participants estimated lower publications (675–900) and presentations (135–180), while STEM participants estimated higher (1,200–1,350 publications and 360 presentations). The range for publications was 675–1,350, with a central tendency around 1,200; for presentations, the range was 135–360, with a central tendency around 360.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus that the graduate employment rate for BK21 participants in humanities fields would be 82.5% and the international collaboration rate 30%, yielding 135 collaborations and 360 employed graduates, with a full-time faculty lecture ratio of 85%. Key causal mechanisms included BK21's network and training boosting employment, modest international engagement in humanities due to language and field constraints, and Hanyang's prioritization of tenured faculty improving mentoring quality. Evaluation feedback loops from consulting sessions were seen as reinforcing these outcomes. No disagreements emerged; all participants aligned their estimates and reasoning, noting that STEM fields would likely achieve higher rates.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that sustained BK21 investment over a decade builds a critical mass of trained researchers, enhancing national innovation capacity and improving South Korea's global S&T talent competitiveness rank. They agreed that dissemination of best practices through BK21 webzines and forums fosters research integrity and collaboration, but the impact on high-impact publications is tempered by field-specific citation norms, with STEM fields driving improvements while humanities/social sciences moderate the national average. There was consensus that the competitiveness rank improves incrementally, with estimates ranging from 7 to 8, centering around 7. Disagreement emerged on high-impact publications per 1000 researchers: most participants estimated 15.0, but one estimated 10.0, arguing that the national average is pulled down by humanities/social sciences fields, while another estimated 12.5 from a humanities perspective. The range for publications was 10.0 to 15.0, with a central tendency near 15.0.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached full consensus on all four input variables, grounding their estimates in explicit policy commitments and practical experience. They agreed that total funding is S$500 million and the co-funding cap is S$330,000 per project, both directly from the policy document. For compute capacity, they estimated 500,000 GPU hours, reasoning that a national program of this scale would allocate hundreds of thousands of hours to serve multiple industry and research projects. The trainer count was set at 50, reflecting a lean but expert team sufficient to guide enterprises through the AI Apprenticeship Programme and similar upskilling initiatives. No disagreements emerged, as all participants' reasoning aligned with the stated policy figures and their own operational experience.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed that with 50 trainers and S$500 million funding, activities must balance breadth with quality engagement. There was consensus on estimates of 100 enterprise outreach sessions, 25 researcher matchmaking events, 35 training workshops, and 150 platform onboarding assists, based on the need for deep, sector-specific interactions and the 100E programme's target of 100 projects. One participant proposed higher estimates of 120 outreach sessions and 30 matchmaking events, but others argued these would strain trainer capacity and reduce interaction quality. The central tendency across all predictions was 100-120 outreach sessions, 25-30 matchmaking events, 35-40 workshops, and 150-200 onboarding assists.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group focused on the rigorous selection process of the 100E programme, estimating that only 60-70% of the 120-150 proposals generated from outreach and matchmaking events would be approved, yielding a consensus estimate of 75-80 approved projects. They highlighted that not all approved projects result in working prototypes due to technical hurdles, with a typical 60-75% success rate, leading to a prototype range of 50-60. For trainees, the 9-month AIAP structure with 35 workshops and 50 trainers was seen as supporting cohorts of 35-40 per intake, with a 10% dropout rate, resulting in 100-120 completions. Enterprises with at least one solution were estimated at 60-70, reflecting that some enterprises have multiple projects while others fail to deploy. The central tendency across all variables was: approved projects ~75, prototypes ~55, trainees ~110, enterprises ~65.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed that the number of deployed 100E projects is constrained by a typical 60% conversion rate from approved projects to production deployment, due to integration and scaling challenges. There was strong consensus on most estimates: 75 approved projects (range 75-80), 45 deployed projects (range 45-48), 12,000 AI practitioners, 100 generative AI use cases, and a 25% enterprise AI adoption rate. A minor disagreement emerged on deployed projects, with one participant suggesting 48 based on the 100E programme's co-funding and support potentially improving deployment success. Overall, the group aligned on a cautious but optimistic outlook, with deployment and adoption rates reflecting early-stage maturity.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group unanimously estimated that Singapore's global AI readiness rank would improve to 4th, the AI sector's GDP share would reach 4.5%, public AI trust would be 65%, and AI talent retention would be 80%. They attributed these outcomes to the tangible outputs from the prior phases—75-80 approved 100E projects, 45-48 deployed, 12,000 AI practitioners, 100 generative AI use cases, and 25% enterprise adoption—which collectively demonstrate concrete progress and strengthen Singapore's ecosystem. No disagreements emerged; all participants shared aligned reasoning, citing the deployed projects and governance efforts as key drivers for economic impact and trust, while the hands-on programme experience and vibrant ecosystem were seen as retaining talent. The central tendency across all predictions was identical, with no variation in estimates.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached full consensus on all four input variables, with every participant estimating total funding at S$500 million, max co-funding at S$330,000 per project, 1 million GPU hours, and 50 trainers. These estimates were anchored on explicit policy statements (funding and co-funding caps) and reasoned extrapolations from government compute investments and program scale (GPU hours, trainer count). No disagreements emerged, and participants reinforced each other's reasoning by citing shared policy context and ecosystem observations. The central tendency is identical to the range for each variable.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached full consensus on all activity estimates, with every participant independently predicting 30 enterprise outreach sessions, 15 researcher matchmaking events, 20 training workshops, and 100 platform onboarding assists. The central causal mechanism discussed was that the available resources—S$500M funding, S$330K co-funding per project, 1M GPU hours, and 50 trainers—directly enable a structured rollout of these activities, with outreach and matchmaking generating problem statements and collaborations, while workshops and onboarding support enterprise capability building. No disagreements emerged; all participants cited the same resource constraints and operational experience (e.g., CTO-as-a-Service onboarding bottlenecks) to justify their estimates. The prediction range was uniform across all four variables, with no variance among the five participants.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached full consensus on all output estimates, with every participant predicting 100 approved projects, 80 AI prototypes, 150 trainees, and 60 enterprises with solutions. Key causal mechanisms included the programme's name and S$500M funding supporting 100 projects, 30 outreach and 15 matchmaking events generating a pipeline for 80 prototypes, 50 trainers each supervising ~3 trainees yielding 150 completions, and typical attrition leading to 60 enterprises with deployable solutions. No disagreements emerged; all reasoning converged on the same numeric targets.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus on all outcome estimates, predicting 50 deployed projects from 100 approved and 80 prototypes, citing integration and validation hurdles especially in regulated sectors like healthcare and logistics. They estimated the AI practitioner pool at 12,000 (below the 15,000 target), generative AI use cases at 80 (short of the 100 goal), and enterprise AI adoption at 25%, reflecting slow SME uptake despite support programs. No disagreements emerged; all participants aligned on the same numeric values and causal reasoning.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that the 100E programme's tangible outputs (100 approved projects, 80 prototypes, 150 trainees, 60 enterprises with solutions) will improve Singapore's global AI readiness rank to 5th and raise AI's GDP share to 4.5%, driven by deployed solutions and increased enterprise adoption. Public trust in AI was uniformly estimated at 65%, with participants citing persistent concerns about data privacy and job displacement despite government trust-building efforts. AI talent retention was consistently predicted at 80%, attributed to the programme's hands-on training and industry exposure creating clear career pathways. No disagreements emerged; all five participants converged on identical numeric estimates and causal reasoning, reflecting a shared interpretation of the policy context and observed outputs.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all input estimates, drawing directly from policy documents for total funding (S$500 million) and per-project co-funding (S$330,000), and inferring compute capacity (500,000 GPU hours) and trainer personnel (50) from analogous national programmes and effective mentor-to-participant ratios. No disagreements emerged, as all participants aligned on the same numeric values and causal logic: adequate funding, compute, and expert guidance are critical enablers for the 100E programme to support dozens of concurrent projects and achieve broader AI adoption goals.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed that the 100E programme's activities—enterprise outreach, researcher matchmaking, training workshops, and platform onboarding—are causally linked to achieving the target of 100 projects approved and deployed, with each activity designed to engage enterprises, pair them with researchers, upskill participants, and provide technical support. There was unanimous consensus on all estimates, with no disagreements. The numeric predictions were identical across all participants: 40 enterprise outreach sessions, 12 researcher matchmaking events, 20 training workshops, and 150 platform onboarding assists.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group converged on estimates of 80 approved projects, 60 prototypes, 300 trainees completing the programme, and 70 enterprises with a solution, citing real-world constraints like enterprise readiness, matchmaking delays, technical hurdles in the 100-day sprint, and typical attrition rates of 25-30% in training. Key causal mechanisms included limited viable problem statements, eligibility criteria, and integration challenges preventing full deployment. No disagreements emerged, with all participants aligning on the same numeric predictions and reasoning.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus that approximately 80 of 100 proposed 100E projects would be approved, with about 50 of 60 prototypes deployed into production, citing a consistent 15-20% drop from prototype to deployment due to integration and production-readiness challenges. They estimated the AI practitioner pool at 12,000, short of the 15,000 target, as training programs add only 2,000-3,000 new practitioners annually. Generative AI use cases were predicted at 80 out of a 100-day target, reflecting enterprise readiness and technical hurdles, while enterprise AI adoption was seen at 18.5%, constrained by data quality, talent shortages, and early digital transformation stages. No disagreements emerged; all participants aligned on estimates and reasoning.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group agreed that the 100E programme's 50 deployed projects and 80 generative AI use cases, alongside a growing practitioner pool of 12,000, strengthen Singapore's AI ecosystem but are insufficient to reach top-3 global status due to an 18.5% enterprise adoption rate and talent shortages. They reached consensus on all numeric estimates: global AI readiness rank improving from 7th to 5th, AI sector GDP share rising to 4.2%, public AI trust at 65%, and AI talent retention at 80%. The reasoning was consistent across participants, citing tangible deployments and governance frameworks as drivers of readiness and trust, while job displacement concerns and competitive global salaries limited trust and retention. No disagreements emerged; all predictions were identical.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on the four input variables, with all participants estimating total funding at S$500 million, co-funding cap at S$330,000, GPU hours at 500,000, and trainer count at 50. The first two values were taken as explicit policy ground truth, while the latter two were reasoned estimates based on the scale of a national AI initiative and the need to support multiple 100E projects. No disagreements emerged, as all narratives aligned on the policy context and the required resource scale. The central tendency for all variables is a single point estimate, with no range observed.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed that the Activities phase is the operational engine converting the S$500M budget and 50 trainers into enterprise engagement, with outreach, matchmaking, training, and onboarding as key causal mechanisms to drive 100E project approvals and deployments. There was strong consensus on the need for balanced, quality interactions across all activities, with most participants converging on estimates of 45 outreach sessions, 22 matchmaking events, 35 training workshops, and 110 onboarding assists. Minor disagreements centered on the scale of outreach and matchmaking, with one participant advocating for lower numbers (40, 20, 30, 100) and another for higher numbers (50, 25, 40, 120), reflecting different views on resource intensity versus breadth of engagement. The central tendency across all predictions was 45-50 outreach sessions, 22-25 matchmaking events, 35-40 workshops, and 110-120 onboarding assists, with the majority settling on the midpoint values.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed that the Outputs phase directly results from Activities, with resource constraints (co-funding cap, compute, trainer capacity) and quality requirements limiting approvals. There was strong consensus on estimates: approved projects around 100-120 (central 100), prototypes 70-90 (central 70), trainees 60-80 (central 60), and enterprises with solutions 80-100 (central 80). The main divergence was AIPractitioner A's higher estimates (120, 90, 80, 100) versus the cautious majority (100, 70, 60, 80), attributed to practical deployment delays and attrition. All agreed that not all approved projects yield prototypes due to technical hurdles and shifting priorities, and that some enterprises run multiple projects while others fail to complete any.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed that the translation of 100E prototypes into production deployments is hindered by integration, compliance, and governance challenges, leading to a consensus that only about 50 of the 70 prototypes would be deployed in 2023, though one participant estimated 60. There was strong agreement that the AI practitioner pool would reach around 12,000 (short of the 15,000 target), generative AI use cases would be about 80 (below the 100 target), and enterprise AI adoption would be around 25%. The main divergence was on deployed projects, with estimates ranging from 50 to 60, reflecting differing emphasis on deployment hurdles versus potential success. Overall, predictions were tightly clustered, with central tendencies of 100 approved projects, 50-60 deployed, 12,000 practitioners, 80 use cases, and 25% adoption.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that the 100E programme's solid but not stellar outcomes (50 deployed projects, 12,000 practitioners, 25% enterprise adoption) would yield modest impacts: Singapore's global AI readiness rank would slip to 6th, AI sector GDP share would rise to 3.5%, public trust would plateau at 55%, and talent retention would hold at 70%. All participants converged on identical numeric predictions, with no disagreement. Key causal mechanisms included deployment hurdles (only ~50% of prototypes reach production), global competition limiting rank improvement, and ethical concerns tempering public trust despite responsible AI practices.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all input estimates, drawing directly from explicit policy statements: total funding of S$500 million, co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project, 500,000 GPU hours for compute, and 50 trainers. All participants independently cited the same evidence, with no disagreement on values or reasoning. The central tendency for each variable was uniform, reflecting a shared interpretation of the policy context and resource logic.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed the need to scale outreach, matchmaking, training, and onboarding activities to generate sufficient problem statements and pairings for the 100E programme, given S$500 million funding, 50 trainers, and 500,000 GPU hours. There was strong consensus on the causal link between these activities and achieving the target of 100 projects, with most participants converging on estimates of 110 enterprise outreach sessions, 28 matchmaking events, 38 training workshops, and 190 platform onboarding assists. Minor disagreements centered on the precise scale of outreach, with one participant proposing 120 sessions and another 100, reflecting differing views on enterprise caution and coordination constraints. The overall numeric range was narrow: enterprise outreach sessions 100–120, matchmaking events 25–30, training workshops 35–40, and onboarding assists 180–200, with central tendencies near 110, 28, 38, and 190 respectively.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group agreed that the 100E programme would approve exactly 100 projects in 2023, citing sufficient funding and explicit policy targets. There was broad consensus that not all approved projects would yield prototypes, with estimates ranging from 80 to 85, and that around 180 trainees would complete the programme (range 180-200), with lower estimates reflecting practical constraints like data quality and trainee dropout. Similarly, enterprises with a solution were estimated at 75-80, with most leaning toward 75 due to cautious adoption. The central tendency across all variables was 100 approved projects, 80 prototypes, 180 trainees, and 75 enterprises with a solution.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus that the 100E programme will approve exactly 100 projects in 2023, but only about 60 will be deployed due to technical, data privacy, and integration challenges. They agreed the AI practitioner pool will reach approximately 14,000, short of the 15,000 target, and that the AI Trailblazers programme will achieve its goal of 100 generative AI use cases in 100 days. Enterprise AI adoption was uniformly estimated at 25%, reflecting cautious uptake in regulated sectors. No disagreements emerged; all participants aligned on both estimates and underlying reasoning.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus on all four impact variables, with every participant predicting a global AI readiness rank of 5, AI sector GDP share of 3.5%, public AI trust score of 65%, and AI talent retention rate of 70%. Key causal mechanisms included the visible success of 100E projects (60 deployed) and 180 trained practitioners boosting Singapore's readiness rank, while cautious adoption in regulated sectors (legal, finance, healthcare) due to data privacy and integration challenges limited GDP growth and trust. Public trust was seen as constrained by persistent concerns over data privacy and job displacement despite responsible AI efforts, and talent retention was moderated by competitive global demand. No disagreements emerged in numeric estimates or reasoning, though some participants emphasized sector-specific barriers like privacy in healthcare or integration in retail.
