phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all input variables, with every participant estimating total funding at S$500 million, co-funding cap at S$330,000 per project, 500,000 GPU hours, and 50 trainers. These estimates were consistently anchored to explicit policy statements (e.g., 'Over S$500 million committed' and 'Co-funding up to S$330,000 per project') and plausible benchmarks for a national AI programme. No disagreements emerged; all narratives emphasized the sufficiency of these inputs to attract SMEs and support experimentation, though some participants expressed concern that 50 trainers might be stretched thin across many enterprises.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed how the S$500 million funding and 50 trainers constrain activities, with a focus on quality over quantity to generate 100 use cases in 100 days. There was consensus that trainer bandwidth limits workshops and matchmaking events, with most participants converging on 70 enterprise outreach sessions, 25 matchmaking events, 35 training workshops, and 90 platform onboarding assists. A minority estimated higher (80, 30, 40, 100) or lower (60, 20, 30, 80) values, reflecting differing views on whether the programme's ambition would push for more activity or risk overloading trainers. The central tendency across all predictions was 70-80 outreach, 25-30 matchmaking, 35-40 workshops, and 90-100 onboarding assists.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached strong consensus that the 100E programme's substantial resources (S$500M funding, S$330K co-funding, 500K GPU hours, 50 trainers) and outreach activities (70 sessions, 25 matchmaking events) would lead to approximately 80 approved projects, with 60 prototypes developed, 120 trainees completing the programme, and 70 enterprises obtaining at least one AI solution. All participants agreed that not all approved projects would yield prototypes due to feasibility filtering, data quality issues, and scope creep, and that deployment challenges would further reduce the number of enterprises with solutions. No disagreements were expressed; all estimates were identical across participants. The central tendency for all four variables was 80, 60, 120, and 70 respectively, with no variation in the numeric predictions.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed that the 100E programme's strong inputs (S$500M, S$330K co-funding, 500K GPU hours, 50 trainers, 70 outreach sessions, 25 matchmaking events) lead to 80 approved projects, but deployment to production faces integration, data quality, and validation hurdles, reducing deployed projects to 50. There was full consensus on all estimates: 80 approved projects, 50 deployed, 14,000 AI practitioners (approaching but below the 15,000 target), 90 generative AI use cases (slightly below the 100 target due to onboarding delays and learning curves), and a 25% enterprise AI adoption rate (reflecting early-stage SME adoption). No disagreements emerged. The central tendency and range for each variable were identical across all participants.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that the NAIS 2.0 programme's substantial inputs (S$500 million funding, 50 trainers) and outputs (50 deployed 100E projects, 90 generative AI use cases, 14,000 practitioner pool) would improve Singapore's global AI readiness rank from 6th to 5th, raise AI sector GDP share to 4.5%, increase public AI trust to 65%, and achieve an 80% AI talent retention rate. All participants agreed on these exact numeric predictions, citing mechanisms such as sustained government commitment, ecosystem maturity, visible project successes, and hands-on training creating strong talent attachment, while acknowledging that early-stage adoption and public caution temper further gains. No disagreements emerged.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached consensus on total funding (S$500M) and max co-funding per project (S$330K) as explicit policy anchors. They discussed causal mechanisms linking compute and trainer availability to project throughput, noting that GPU hours are shared across industry, research, and government, and that trainer capacity is reduced by administrative duties. Disagreement centered on GPU hours (range 400,000–500,000, central tendency ~420,000–450,000) and trainer count (range 120–150, central tendency ~130–140), driven by differing assumptions about industry readiness and trainer-to-practitioner ratios.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed how to balance the ambition of the 100E programme with practical constraints, focusing on the causal link between high-quality, targeted activities and successful project completions. There was consensus that outreach, matchmaking, workshops, and onboarding must be substantial but not so numerous as to dilute quality or overwhelm trainer capacity. Disagreement centered on the optimal scale: some argued for higher numbers to maximize engagement, while others warned that overestimating activities would spread resources too thin. Numeric predictions ranged from 60–80 for outreach sessions, 10–12 for matchmaking events, 40–50 for training workshops, and 150–200 for platform onboarding assists, with central tendencies around 70, 11, 45, and 175 respectively.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group focused on how resource constraints—trainer capacity, GPU hours, and administrative overhead—limit throughput from strong financial incentives (S$500M funding, S$330K co-funding) and outreach activities. There was consensus on mid-range estimates for approved projects (42), prototypes (28), trainees (110), and enterprises with solutions (35), reflecting a balance between ambition and practical limits. Disagreement centered on whether higher estimates (e.g., 45 projects, 120 trainees) were too optimistic given shared compute and trainer duties, or whether lower estimates (e.g., 40 projects, 100 trainees) underutilized funding and outreach. The numeric ranges were: approved projects 40–45, prototypes 25–30, trainees 100–120, enterprises with solutions 30–40.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group focused on resource constraints—trainer capacity, GPU hours, and enterprise readiness—as key mechanisms limiting outcomes. They broadly agreed that of the 28-30 prototypes, about 25 would be deployed, the AI practitioner pool would reach 13,000-13,500 (below the 15,000 target), generative AI use cases would hit 80-85 (below the 100 target), and enterprise AI adoption would be 17-18.5%. A minority held more conservative estimates (40 approved projects, 22 deployed, 13,000 practitioners, 80 use cases, 17% adoption) due to lower assumed trainer counts and GPU hours, while the majority consensus settled on 42 approved projects, 25 deployed, 13,500 practitioners, 85 use cases, and 18.5% adoption.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that strong financial inputs and infrastructure, combined with successful deployment of 100E projects and generative AI use cases, improve Singapore's global AI readiness rank to around 5th. Consensus emerged that public trust in AI reaches 70-72%, supported by responsible AI governance but tempered by ongoing concerns about misuse and privacy. Disagreement centered on AI sector GDP share (4.0-4.2%) and talent retention (82-85%), with some participants citing resource constraints like limited trainers and GPU hours as limiting factors, while others emphasized the ecosystem's attractiveness. Numeric predictions ranged: global AI readiness rank 5, AI sector GDP share 4.0-4.2%, public trust 70-72%, and talent retention 82-85%.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group agreed that total funding (S$500M) and the co-funding cap (S$330K per project) are fixed by policy. They discussed causal mechanisms linking compute and trainer availability to project throughput, noting that first-year provisioning delays and competing priorities would likely reduce effective GPU hours and trainer scaling. Consensus emerged on total funding and co-funding, but estimates diverged for GPU hours (range 350K–500K, central tendency ~400K) and trainer count (range 30–50, central tendency ~35–40), reflecting differing assumptions about infrastructure readiness and program ramp-up speed.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group focused on translating substantial inputs (S$500M, GPU hours, trainers) into concrete activities for the 100E programme, emphasizing the need for realistic scaling in the first year due to enterprise readiness and trainer capacity constraints. There was consensus that enterprise outreach, matchmaking events, training workshops, and platform onboarding assists should be moderate, with enterprise outreach ranging from 55 to 80 (central tendency ~60), matchmaking events from 18 to 30 (~20), training workshops from 30 to 50 (~35), and onboarding assists from 130 to 200 (~150). Disagreement centered on the pace of scaling: some argued for aggressive outreach to meet the '100 use cases in 100 days' goal, while others cautioned that first-year momentum would be slower due to enterprise assessment delays and limited trainer bandwidth. The causal mechanism highlighted was that trainer availability and enterprise uptake directly constrain the number of activities, with quality of pairings and hands-on support prioritized over quantity.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group focused on first-year ramp-up constraints, including enterprise readiness, trainer bandwidth, and the strict 'no commercial off-the-shelf solution' criterion, which limit the conversion of outreach into approved projects and prototypes. There was consensus that not all approved projects yield prototypes due to complexity and ethics reviews, and that trainee completions are constrained by the 9-month programme duration and attrition. Disagreement centered on the scale of outputs, with one participant estimating higher numbers (60 approved projects, 45 prototypes, 120 trainees, 50 enterprises) while others were more conservative (50-55 approved, 35-40 prototypes, 80-100 trainees, 40-45 enterprises). The overall ranges were: approved projects 50-60, prototypes 35-45, trainees 80-120, enterprises with solutions 40-50, with central tendencies around 55, 40, 100, and 45 respectively.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed the transition from AI prototypes to production deployment as a major bottleneck, with estimates for deployed 100E projects ranging from 25 to 30, reflecting a consensus that only about half of approved projects succeed in deployment due to engineering, integration, and validation challenges. There was broad agreement that the AI practitioner pool would fall short of the 15,000 target, with estimates between 11,500 and 12,000, and that generative AI use cases would reach 75-80, below the 100 target, constrained by enterprise readiness and compute availability. Enterprise AI adoption rate was estimated at 22-25%, indicating limited adoption beyond early adopters. Disagreement centered on the deployment rate: some participants were more optimistic (30 deployed) while others were more conservative (25 deployed), citing additional hurdles like ethics review and resource constraints. Overall, the predictions clustered around 50-55 approved projects, 25-30 deployed, 11,500-12,000 practitioners, 75-80 generative AI use cases, and 22-25% adoption rate.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that first-year outcomes, including 25-30 deployed 100E projects and 22-25% enterprise AI adoption, would modestly improve Singapore's global AI readiness rank to 6th, driven by strong governance and investments but constrained by limited deployment scale. Consensus emerged that AI talent retention would be 78-82%, supported by hands-on training and ethical focus, while public trust in AI would range 63-68%, tempered by limited citizen interaction and concerns about misuse. Disagreement centered on AI sector GDP share, with estimates from 3.8% to 4.2%, reflecting differing views on how much limited production deployments and moderate adoption would constrain economic impact in the first year. The central tendency for GDP share was around 4.0%, with trust and retention clustering near 65% and 80% respectively.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached full consensus on all input estimates, drawing on explicit policy commitments and shared experiential knowledge from the AIAP and national compute resources. They agreed on total funding of S$500 million, a co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project, 500,000 GPU hours for compute, and 80 trainer personnel. No disagreements emerged, as all participants cited the same policy documents and similar firsthand observations of programme operations.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed how to translate S$500 million funding and 80 trainers into concrete activities for the 100E programme, focusing on generating 100 use cases in 100 days. There was consensus on the need for targeted, quality-driven outreach and careful curation in matchmaking, with estimates clustering around 100-120 enterprise outreach sessions, 25-30 researcher matchmaking events, 50-60 training workshops, and 150-200 platform onboarding assists. Disagreement centered on the pace of activities: some argued for higher volumes (120 outreach, 30 matchmaking, 60 workshops, 200 assists) based on national program benchmarks, while others favored lower numbers (100 outreach, 25 matchmaking, 50 workshops, 150 assists) citing constraints from preparation time and selective pairing. The central tendency across all estimates was approximately 110 outreach sessions, 27 matchmaking events, 55 workshops, and 175 onboarding assists.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached strong consensus that the 100E programme's outputs would be constrained by enterprise readiness and technical challenges, with all participants estimating 80 approved projects, 60 prototypes developed, 120 trainees completing the programme, and 70 enterprises with a solution. Key causal mechanisms included the filtering of outreach sessions (108) into viable proposals due to co-funding caps and problem statement definition, prototype attrition from technical or data issues, and trainee completion rates around 60-80% over 9 months. No disagreements emerged; all estimates were identical, reflecting shared conservative assumptions based on firsthand experience with enterprise adoption hurdles and programme dynamics.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus that of the 80 approved 100E projects, about half (40) would be deployed in 2023, citing integration challenges, data quality issues, and organizational inertia as key barriers. They agreed the AI practitioner pool would reach approximately 12,000, short of the 15,000 target, as upskilling takes time beyond a single year. All participants estimated 100 generative AI use cases would be created via the AI Trailblazers programme, viewing the 100-day sprint as achievable with free tools. The enterprise AI adoption rate was uniformly estimated at 25%, reflecting that many SMEs remain in early adoption stages despite awareness programmes. No disagreements emerged; all predictions and causal reasoning were aligned across the five participants.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that Singapore's global AI readiness rank will improve to 5th, the AI sector's GDP share will reach 3.5%, public trust in AI will be 55%, and AI talent retention will be 70%. All participants attributed these outcomes to the cumulative effects of the 100 Experiments programme, including 80 approved projects, 40 deployed, 12,000 practitioners, 100 generative AI use cases, and 25% enterprise adoption, combined with proactive governance and international engagement. No disagreements were expressed; the estimates were identical across all five participants, reflecting a shared view that tangible outputs and policy support drive these impacts, though tempered by persistent challenges such as SME adoption gaps, data privacy concerns, and global competition for talent.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group unanimously agreed on all input estimates, drawing directly from explicit policy statements for total funding (S$500 million) and co-funding cap (S$330,000 per project). For compute, they converged on 5 million GPU hours as a reasonable allocation to support 100E projects and other experiments, citing typical cloud compute grants. Trainer personnel was consistently estimated at 200, seen as a modest but sufficient team for upskilling initiatives. No disagreements emerged, with all participants treating the funding and co-funding figures as ground truth and independently arriving at the same compute and trainer estimates based on their respective expertise in AI research, safety, and enterprise adoption.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group consistently estimated the same values for all activities: 80 enterprise outreach sessions, 20 researcher matchmaking events, 100 training workshops, and 150 platform onboarding assists. Key themes included leveraging the S$500 million funding, 200 trainers, and 5 million GPU hours to support the 100E programme's goal of generating 100 generative AI use cases in 100 days. There was unanimous consensus on both the numeric estimates and the reasoning, which centered on a realistic pace of 1-2 sessions per week for outreach, bi-weekly matchmaking events, and each trainer conducting about 0.5 workshops per year. No disagreements emerged, as all participants—from policy analysts to AI practitioners and researchers—converged on the same figures, citing the resource logic and typical implementation scales for national AI adoption programmes.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group unanimously agreed that exactly 100 projects would be approved in 2023, directly aligning with the programme's explicit target of 100 generative AI use cases in 100 days. There was strong consensus that not all approved projects would result in fully built prototypes, with estimates ranging from 75 to 80, reflecting a realistic success rate of 75-80% due to technical challenges and validation requirements. All participants estimated that 150 trainees would complete the 9-month AI Apprenticeship Programme, consistent with typical cohort sizes given the available trainers and workshops. For enterprises with at least one AI solution, estimates ranged from 65 to 70, with most agreeing on 65, as some projects may not reach deployment within the year and enterprises are counted once even if they have multiple projects. The central tendency across all variables was 100 approved projects, 75 prototypes, 150 trainees, and 65 enterprises with solutions.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus that exactly 100 projects would be approved in 2023, matching the explicit policy target, and that 100 generative AI use cases would be developed. Disagreement centered on the deployment rate: some participants estimated 60 deployed projects (75% of prototypes) based on technical robustness checks, while others estimated 55 (73% of prototypes) citing additional hurdles like ethical review and integration. The AI practitioner pool was estimated between 11,500 and 12,000, and enterprise AI adoption between 17% and 18.5%, reflecting cautious realism about early-stage adoption and workforce readiness. Key causal mechanisms included the conversion of prototypes to production, the impact of safety testing and compliance on deployment, and the gradual scaling of the practitioner pool and enterprise adoption.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus on all four impact metrics, predicting Singapore's global AI readiness rank would hold at 5th, AI sector GDP share at 3.5%, public trust at 65%, and talent retention at 80%. Key causal mechanisms included the policy's S$500M investment, 55-60 deployed 100E projects, 100 generative AI use cases, and emphasis on Responsible AI and safety testing, which were seen as building international recognition and trust. However, participants tempered optimism with cautious realism, noting that rapid advances by other nations, early-stage enterprise adoption (17-18.5%), potential AI incidents, and global competition for talent would limit further gains. No disagreements emerged; all estimates were identical, reflecting a shared view that strong execution in prior phases would yield moderate but solid impact.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached full consensus on all input estimates, drawing directly from explicit policy context for total funding (S$500 million) and co-funding cap (S$330,000 per project). For compute, they uniformly estimated 500,000 GPU hours based on typical national AI cluster capacity, and for trainers, 50 personnel as a lean team to support the 15,000-practitioner target. No disagreements emerged, as all participants aligned on the same numeric values and reasoning, grounded in stated policy figures and reasonable extrapolations from infrastructure and programme scale.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed that the 100E programme's activities—enterprise outreach, matchmaking, training, and platform onboarding—are driven by the inputs of S$500 million funding, 50 trainers, and 500,000 GPU hours, with the target of 100 projects. There was consensus that outreach should be quality-focused rather than purely high-volume, and that matchmaking events and training workshops must balance the limited trainer resource across multiple duties. Estimates converged around 70-75 outreach sessions, 25 matchmaking events, 35 training workshops, and 180 platform onboarding assists, with the main divergence being the researcher's higher estimates (80 outreach, 30 matchmaking, 40 workshops, 200 assists) versus Nadia's lower ones (60 outreach, 20 matchmaking, 30 workshops, 150 assists). The central tendency across all participants was approximately 71 outreach sessions, 25 matchmaking events, 35 workshops, and 178 onboarding assists.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed that outputs depend on enterprise readiness, researcher bandwidth, and the sufficiency of resources (S$500M funding, 500,000 GPU hours, 50 trainers) and activities (71 outreach sessions, 25 matchmaking events, 35 workshops, 178 onboarding assists). There was consensus that not all 100 targeted projects would be approved due to practical constraints, and that some approved projects would not yield working prototypes or deployable solutions within the year. Disagreement centered on the degree of optimism: some participants estimated higher numbers (85 approved, 65 prototypes, 130 trainees, 75 enterprises) based on strong demand and resource support, while others were more conservative (75 approved, 55 prototypes, 110 trainees, 65 enterprises) citing technical hurdles and slower adoption in less digitally mature sectors. The central tendency of predictions was approximately 78–80 approved projects, 58–60 prototypes, 115–120 trainees, and 68–70 enterprises with a solution, with ranges of 75–85, 55–65, 110–130, and 65–75 respectively.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed the transition from approved projects to production deployment as a key bottleneck, with integration challenges and validation needs limiting conversion rates. There was strong consensus that the AI practitioner pool would reach around 12,000 (range 12,000–12,500), generative AI use cases would be about 80 (range 80–85), and enterprise AI adoption would be around 25% (range 25%–27%). Disagreement centered on the number of approved projects (range 75–85) and deployed projects (range 45–50), with some participants more optimistic due to government backing and resource support, while others emphasized real-world hurdles. Central tendency estimates were 80 approved projects, 48 deployed projects, 12,000 practitioners, 80 generative AI use cases, and 25% adoption rate.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed the impact of the 100E programme on Singapore's AI ecosystem, focusing on how deployed projects, enterprise adoption, and talent development influence global readiness, GDP contribution, public trust, and talent retention. There was consensus that the programme improves Singapore's global AI readiness rank and boosts the AI sector's GDP share, with estimates clustering around a rank of 5-6 and GDP share of 3.8-4.2%. Disagreement centered on the magnitude of improvement: some participants, citing 48-50 deployed projects and 25-27% enterprise adoption, projected a rank of 5 and 4.2% GDP share, while others, emphasizing modest scale and slower adoption in underserved sectors, estimated a rank of 6 and 3.8% GDP share. Public trust estimates ranged from 65% to 68%, with lower values reflecting concerns about job displacement and data privacy among vulnerable groups, and talent retention estimates ranged from 78% to 82%, with lower values considering competition from global tech hubs and career shifts. The central tendency across all variables was a rank of 6, GDP share of 3.8%, trust score of 65%, and retention rate of 78%.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all four input variables, treating the policy's stated S$500 million total funding and S$330,000 co-funding cap as factual anchors. They uniformly estimated 500,000 GPU hours and 50 trainer personnel, reasoning from Singapore's national scale and the need to support enterprise experimentation across multiple programmes. No disagreements emerged; all participants aligned on the logic that these inputs are foundational for enabling SME participation, hands-on AI experimentation, and practical real-world problem-solving. The central tendency for each variable is identical across all posts: total_funding_sgd = 500,000,000, max_co_funding_per_project_sgd = 330,000, available_gpu_hours = 500,000, trainer_personnel_count = 50.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed that the 100E programme's activities should prioritize quality engagement over quantity, given the S$500M funding and 50 trainers. There was strong consensus on enterprise outreach sessions (100), researcher matchmaking events (25), training workshops (35), and platform onboarding assists (150), with most participants converging on these estimates. A single dissenting voice proposed higher numbers (120 outreach, 30 matchmaking, 40 workshops, 200 assists), arguing for more intensive support based on hands-on experience. The central tendency across all predictions was 100-120 outreach sessions, 25-30 matchmaking events, 35-40 workshops, and 150-200 onboarding assists, reflecting a tension between scaling up and ensuring quality.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached strong consensus that the 100E programme would approve about 100 projects in 2023, aligning with the programme's name and the estimated 100 outreach sessions and 25 matchmaking events. They agreed that not all approved projects yield a working prototype, estimating 80 prototypes developed, and that about 150 trainees would complete the AI Apprenticeship Programme given 50 trainers and 35 workshops with typical attrition. The number of enterprises with at least one AI solution was consistently estimated at 90, reflecting that some enterprises may participate in multiple projects or fail to deploy. All participants converged on identical numeric predictions: approved_100e_projects=100, ai_prototypes_developed=80, trainees_completed_programme=150, enterprises_with_solution=90, with no disagreement on estimates or reasoning.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus that the 100E programme would approve 100 projects, build 80 prototypes, and deploy 70 into production, citing a consistent 70% prototype-to-production conversion rate due to integration, data quality, and change management challenges. All participants estimated the AI practitioner pool at 12,000 (below the 15,000 target), generative AI use cases at 80 (below the 100 target), and enterprise AI adoption at 25%, attributing shortfalls to time lags in training, the need for use-case identification, and cost/skills/trust barriers. No disagreements emerged; the shared reasoning emphasized that production deployment and full adoption are the hardest steps, with estimates grounded in practical experience across logistics, finance, retail, and education sectors.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that the NAIS 2.0 policy's Impact phase yields meaningful but not maximal progress, with all participants estimating Singapore's global AI readiness rank at 3rd, AI sector GDP share at 4.5%, public AI trust at 65%, and AI talent retention at 80%. Key causal mechanisms discussed include how 70 deployed projects and 80 generative AI use cases drive economic value and trust, while persistent concerns about data privacy and job displacement cap public trust, and strong ecosystem opportunities retain most talent despite some outflow abroad. No disagreements emerged in estimates or reasoning, with all participants citing the same prior-phase outputs (e.g., 12,000 practitioner pool, 25% adoption rate) as evidence. The central tendency for all four variables is a single value: rank 3, 4.5%, 65%, and 80%.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all four input variables, treating the total funding of S$500 million and the co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project as explicit policy ground truths. For compute capacity, participants uniformly estimated 500,000 GPU hours based on typical national AI programme allocations, and for trainer personnel, all settled on 50 individuals, reasoning that AI Singapore would leverage existing researchers and industry experts rather than hire a large dedicated team. No disagreements emerged, as all participants aligned on the same numeric estimates and causal logic linking these inputs to the programme's ambition. The central tendency and range for each variable are identical: total_funding_sgd = 500,000,000; max_co_funding_per_project_sgd = 330,000; available_gpu_hours = 500,000; trainer_personnel_count = 50.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed translating policy inputs (S$500M funding, 50 trainers, 500,000 GPU hours) into concrete activities to drive enterprise engagement and AI adoption. There was consensus that researcher matchmaking events should be around 10, and training workshops around 15-18, reflecting the complexity and resource intensity of these activities. Disagreement centered on enterprise outreach sessions (range 30-40) and platform onboarding assists (range 40-60), with some participants arguing for higher numbers to match policy ambition and compute resources, while others emphasized slower SME adoption and need for tailored support. Central tendencies were approximately 30-35 outreach sessions, 10 matchmaking events, 15-18 workshops, and 40-50 onboarding assists.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached strong consensus that the policy's explicit target of '100 generative AI use cases in 100 days' anchors the approval of exactly 100 projects in 2023, supported by S$500M funding and a S$330K co-funding cap. They agreed that about 80% of approved projects yield a working prototype within the year due to technical and enterprise readiness challenges, leading to 80 prototypes. For trainees, with 50 trainers and 15-20 workshops, each trainer mentoring 2-3 trainees per cohort yields roughly 120 completions. Enterprises with at least one solution are estimated at 70, reflecting that some enterprises participate in multiple projects. No disagreements emerged; all estimates were identical across participants.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group agreed that the policy target of 100 generative AI use cases in 100 days anchors the number of approved 100E projects at 100 for 2023, supported by S$500M funding and S$330K co-funding. They also concurred that the 100 generative AI use cases target is achievable via the AI Trailblazers programme. However, they disagreed on deployment rates and practitioner pool size: some estimated 50 deployed projects and 12,000 practitioners, while others estimated 40 deployed projects and 11,000 practitioners, citing slower integration and training timelines. Enterprise AI adoption rate estimates ranged from 12% to 15%, with all noting barriers like cost, skills, and trust. The central tendency for deployed projects was 40-50, for practitioner pool 11,000-12,000, and for adoption rate 12-15%.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group consistently estimated that Singapore's global AI readiness rank would drop from 2nd to 5th due to intense global competition, with AI's GDP share rising modestly to 3.5% as early adopters in finance and logistics drive growth while manufacturing SMEs remain cautious. Public trust in AI for government services was pegged at 55%, reflecting wariness about AI in decision-making, and AI talent retention was estimated at 70%, with some talent leaving for global tech hubs despite attractive local programmes. There was strong consensus across all participants on both the numeric estimates and the underlying reasoning, with no substantive disagreements. The central tendency and range for all four variables were identical: global_ai_readiness_rank = 5, ai_sector_gdp_share = 3.5%, public_ai_trust_score = 55%, and ai_talent_retention_rate = 70%.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached full consensus on all input estimates, treating the total funding of S$500 million and the co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project as factual anchors from the policy document. For compute capacity, participants uniformly estimated 500,000 GPU hours, drawing on typical allocations for national AI programs to support dozens of experiments. Trainer personnel count was consistently estimated at 50, based on the common understanding that such programs start with a core team of 30-70 experts to guide enterprises across sectors. No disagreements emerged, as all participants' reasoning aligned with the policy context and each other's estimates.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached strong consensus on all activity estimates, with every participant predicting 40 enterprise outreach sessions, 12 researcher matchmaking events, 30 training workshops, and 200 platform onboarding assists. Their reasoning consistently linked these numbers to the available inputs: S$500 million funding, 50 trainers, 500,000 GPU hours, and a S$330,000 co-funding cap per project. They saw outreach and matchmaking as essential for generating problem statements and quality researcher-enterprise pairings to achieve the 100 Experiments target, while workshops and onboarding assists were viewed as necessary to build enterprise AI capabilities and ensure smooth platform adoption. No disagreements or alternative estimates were presented, indicating a shared understanding of the causal mechanisms linking inputs to activities.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached full consensus on all output estimates, with every participant predicting 100 approved projects, 80 AI prototypes developed, 150 trainees completing the programme, and 70 enterprises with a solution. Key causal mechanisms included the direct link between the programme's name and funding (S$500 million total, S$330,000 co-funding cap) enabling 100 approvals, development timelines limiting prototype completion to 80% of approved projects, the 9-month apprenticeship structure and 50 trainers supporting 150 completions, and the tendency for multiple projects per enterprise reducing the count of enterprises with solutions to 70. No disagreements were expressed; all estimates were identical and supported by consistent reasoning across participants.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed the significant challenge of moving from prototype to production deployment, citing engineering, compliance, and regulatory hurdles, especially in regulated sectors. There was consensus that the AI practitioner pool (11,000–12,000) and generative AI use cases (70–80) would fall short of targets, and enterprise AI adoption would remain low (20–25%). Disagreement centered on deployment rates, with estimates ranging from 30 to 40 deployed projects, reflecting differing sector experiences. The central tendency for deployed projects was 35, with a range of 30–40.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached full consensus on all impact estimates, predicting Singapore's global AI readiness rank would improve to 5th, AI sector GDP share would reach 3.5%, public AI trust would be 55%, and AI talent retention would be 70%. Key causal mechanisms included the 100E programme's visible success and government commitment driving readiness improvements, while deployed projects and enterprise solutions generated economic value but full adoption remained nascent. Public trust was moderated by media coverage of AI risks and limited transparency, and talent retention was constrained by competitive offers from global tech hubs. No disagreements emerged across the five participants.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group unanimously agreed on explicit policy figures: total funding of S$500 million and a co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project. For estimated inputs, all participants converged on 500,000 GPU hours annually, based on typical national supercomputing capacity (e.g., 2 petaflops), and 50 AI trainers, reflecting the scale of upskilling programs like AIAP. No disagreements emerged, as each participant independently arrived at the same estimates using similar reasoning about infrastructure and workforce scale.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group focused on resource constraints (S$500M funding, 50 trainers, 500K GPU hours) and the 100E programme target of 100 projects. They agreed that 80 enterprise outreach sessions (each targeting 5-10 companies) are needed to generate enough viable problem statements, 12 matchmaking events (pairing 8-10 researchers per event) to fill the pipeline, 30 training workshops (20-30 participants each) to begin upskilling toward the 15,000 practitioner target, and 100 platform onboarding assists (one per 100E project). There was full consensus across all participants, with no disagreements, and all numeric predictions were identical: enterprise_outreach_sessions=80, researcher_matchmaking_events=12, training_workshops_held=30, platform_onboarding_assists=100.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group agreed that the 100E programme is designed to fund exactly 100 projects, given its name and the S$500 million budget with a S$330,000 co-funding cap. Most participants estimated that about 80% of these projects (80 prototypes) would result in a working AI prototype within the year, citing technical challenges, integration difficulties, and regulatory delays; one participant estimated a slightly higher 85% success rate. There was consensus that 30 training workshops with 20-30 participants each and a 70-80% completion rate would produce approximately 600 trainees. The number of enterprises with a solution was consistently tied to the number of successful prototypes, leading to a range of 80-85 enterprises. The central tendency across all estimates was 100 approved projects, 80-85 prototypes, 600 trainees, and 80-85 enterprises with a solution.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed the 100E programme's design, with consensus that it would approve exactly 100 projects due to its name and budget. However, deployment rates were debated, with estimates ranging from 55% to 60% (55-60 deployed projects), reflecting differing views on regulatory and integration hurdles in sectors like finance and healthcare. There was agreement on the AI practitioner pool at 12,000 (short of the 15,000 target) and that the AI Trailblazers programme would achieve 100 generative AI use cases. Enterprise AI adoption rates varied slightly, from 22% to 25%, with lower estimates citing barriers like data privacy and integration costs.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that the 100E programme's approval of 100 projects but deployment of only 55-60 into production indicates a solid but not transformative start, limiting Singapore's global AI readiness rank improvement to around 6th due to fierce competition from larger economies. There was consensus that AI sector GDP share would rise modestly to about 3.2%, public trust in AI for public services would be around 60%, and AI talent retention would be approximately 75%, reflecting cautious citizen sentiment, early-stage enterprise adoption, and some talent outflow to global tech hubs. A single dissenting voice estimated a more optimistic rank of 5, GDP share of 3.5%, trust of 65%, and retention of 80%, citing stronger government investment and ecosystem growth, but most participants viewed these as slightly optimistic given real-world deployment and adoption challenges.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group unanimously agreed on the total funding (S$500 million) and co-funding cap (S$330,000 per project), citing explicit policy statements as ground truth. There was strong consensus on compute capacity (500,000 GPU hours) and trainer personnel (50), with participants reasoning from typical cloud allocations, program scale, and hands-on experience. No disagreements emerged; all estimates were identical across the four variables, reflecting a shared reliance on the same policy context and practical constraints.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed the Activities phase as the operational engine of NAIS 2.0, focusing on enterprise outreach, researcher matchmaking, training workshops, and platform onboarding to drive 100E project approvals and deployments. There was strong consensus on conservative estimates for enterprise outreach sessions (60), researcher matchmaking events (10), training workshops (30), and platform onboarding assists (100), with most participants citing the need for quality over quantity due to the program's early stage and limited trainer dedication. One participant (an AI researcher) proposed higher estimates: 80 outreach sessions, 12 matchmaking events, 40 workshops, and 150 onboarding assists, based on a more optimistic view of trainer capacity and a 6-month active period. The central tendency across all participants was 60-80 for outreach, 10-12 for matchmaking, 30-40 for workshops, and 100-150 for onboarding assists.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group agreed that the 100E programme's explicit target of 100 experiments and the '100 generative AI use cases in 100 days' policy anchor the number of approved projects at exactly 100. There was broad consensus on 150 trainees completing the programme, based on three cohorts of 50 each, and on 65 enterprises having at least one AI solution, reflecting that some enterprises run multiple projects. The main disagreement centered on the number of AI prototypes developed, with estimates ranging from 70 to 80; most participants settled on 75 as a realistic middle ground, citing that not all approved projects complete a prototype within the same year due to technical challenges and resource constraints.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group focused on the translation of program outputs into real-world outcomes, emphasizing that transitioning from prototype to production is a major bottleneck due to integration, testing, and regulatory hurdles, typically taking 6-12 months. There was strong consensus on most estimates: 100 approved projects, 100 generative AI use cases, 12,000 AI practitioners, and a 25% enterprise AI adoption rate. The only divergence was on deployed projects, with one participant estimating 50 (based on a 60-70% conversion rate) while the majority estimated 40 (based on a ~40% conversion rate due to deployment delays). Numeric predictions ranged from 40 to 50 for deployed projects, with a central tendency of 40, and all other variables were unanimous.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that the 100E programme and NAIS 2.0 investments would improve Singapore's global AI readiness rank from about 10th to 8th, raise AI's GDP share from ~2.5% to 3.5%, achieve 60% public trust in AI, and retain 75% of AI talent. All participants estimated identical values for each variable, reflecting agreement on the programme's effectiveness but recognition that full impact on trust and retention requires more time. Key causal mechanisms included the S$500M funding, 40 deployed projects, 100 generative AI use cases, and 25% enterprise adoption driving economic value and ecosystem maturity. No disagreements emerged, though participants noted that some talent loss abroad and moderate public trust were realistic constraints.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on the four input variables, all estimating total funding at S$500 million, co-funding cap at S$330,000, GPU hours at 500,000, and trainer count at 50. They anchored on explicit policy statements for funding and co-funding, while inferring compute and trainer numbers from Singapore's size and the target of 15,000 AI practitioners. A minor concern was raised that 50 trainers might be insufficient for scaling, but no alternative estimates were proposed. The central tendency and range for all variables were identical across participants.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group focused on balancing outreach quality with resource constraints, emphasizing targeted engagement to attract enterprises with clear problem statements. There was strong consensus on cautious estimates: 25 enterprise outreach sessions, 12 researcher matchmaking events, 15 training workshops, and 80 platform onboarding assists, reflecting concerns about limited trainer capacity (50 trainers) and the need for careful researcher-enterprise pairings. One participant proposed higher numbers (30 outreach, 15 matchmaking, 20 workshops, 100 onboarding), but the majority aligned on lower figures to avoid overwhelming the system and ensure effective support for non-tech users. The central tendency across all predictions was 25-27 outreach sessions, 12-13 matchmaking events, 15-17 workshops, and 80-84 onboarding assists.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group consistently estimated that with S$500 million in funding, a co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project, 500,000 GPU hours, and 50 trainers, the 100E programme would approve about 30 projects, develop 25 AI prototypes, train 80 completers, and result in 20 enterprises with solutions. Key causal mechanisms included the filtering effect of the co-funding cap and trainer capacity on proposals, typical technical attrition of about 17% from approved projects to prototypes, and the 9-month programme duration limiting completions and deployments. There was full consensus on all numeric estimates and reasoning, with no disagreement observed. The prediction ranges were narrow: approved projects 30, prototypes 25, trainees 80, enterprises 20.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed outcomes as a natural progression from prior inputs and outputs, with causal mechanisms linking approved projects to deployment, training to practitioner pool growth, and separate initiatives to generative AI use cases. There was consensus that 30 projects would be approved and 100 generative AI use cases would be met via the AI Trailblazers programme. Disagreement centered on deployment rates (18 vs. 20) and enterprise AI adoption (11% vs. 12.5%), driven by differing views on integration challenges and SME readiness. The AI practitioner pool estimates ranged from 14,000 to 15,000, with most leaning toward the higher target.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that the 100E programme's outputs (e.g., 18-20 production deployments, ~15,000 AI practitioners, ~12.5% enterprise adoption) would improve Singapore's global AI readiness rank from ~7th to 5th-6th, with most converging on rank 6. Consensus held that AI sector GDP share would be modest at 3.2%-3.5%, public trust at 60%-65%, and talent retention at 70%-75%. Disagreement centered on the rank and trust estimates: the AIPractitioner was more optimistic (rank 5, trust 65%) due to tangible deployment progress, while others were more cautious (rank 6, trust 60%-62%), citing rapid global competition and persistent public concerns about job displacement and data privacy. The central tendency across all participants was rank 6, GDP share 3.2%, trust 60%-62%, and retention 70%-72%.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group consistently anchored total funding at S$500 million and the co-funding cap at S$330,000 per project, based on explicit policy statements. For compute, estimates ranged from 3 to 5 million GPU hours, with a central tendency around 4 million, reflecting a balance between industry experimentation and research needs. Trainer personnel estimates varied from 150 to 200, with a consensus around 180, emphasizing the need to scale up existing programs while maintaining quality and diversity of expertise. The main divergence was in the scale of compute and trainer resources, driven by differing views on infrastructure ramp-up and the importance of responsible AI skills.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed the activities needed to achieve the 100E programme's goal of 100 use cases in 100 days, focusing on enterprise outreach, researcher matchmaking, training workshops, and platform onboarding as key causal mechanisms. There was strong consensus on higher estimates for outreach (80 sessions), matchmaking (30 events), workshops (50), and onboarding (200), driven by the need for broad engagement and leveraging substantial resources (S$500M funding, 4M GPU hours, 180 trainers). A minority advocated for lower estimates (outreach 60-70, matchmaking 20-25, workshops 40-45, onboarding 150-180), prioritizing depth and quality over volume, especially for SMEs and underserved sectors. The overall prediction ranges were: outreach 60-80 (central ~75), matchmaking 20-30 (central ~26), workshops 40-50 (central ~46), and onboarding 150-200 (central ~183).
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed the 100E programme's outputs, focusing on how the novelty filter (no off-the-shelf solutions) and enterprise scoping challenges limit approvals below the 100-use-case goal. There was consensus that strong compute and trainer resources support prototype development and trainee completion, but that technical hurdles and deployment challenges reduce the number of enterprises with a solution. Disagreements centered on the degree of optimism: some stakeholders were more conservative, citing quality assurance and SME readiness, while others were more optimistic, emphasizing resource capacity. Numeric predictions ranged from 45 to 60 approved projects (central tendency ~50), 30 to 45 prototypes (central ~35), 120 to 150 trainees (central ~130), and 35 to 40 enterprises with a solution (central ~38).
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed that the 100E programme's novelty filter and SME integration challenges limit both project approvals and production deployment, with deployment rates around 50% due to data quality, operationalization costs, and regulatory hurdles. There was strong consensus on approved projects (45-60, central ~50), deployed projects (20-30, central ~25), practitioner pool (11,500-13,000, central ~12,000), generative AI use cases (75-85, central ~80), and enterprise adoption rate (16-20%, central ~18%). A minor disagreement emerged on deployment and adoption rates, with one participant being more conservative (20 deployed, 16% adoption) due to responsible AI audit requirements, while another was slightly more optimistic (30 deployed, 20% adoption) citing strong onboarding support. Overall, the group agreed that outcomes fall short of targets due to time lags in scaling practitioners and SME digital maturity.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that NAIS 2.0's substantial investments in compute, training, and co-funding, combined with the 100E programme's practical deployments, are driving tangible outcomes such as 25-30 deployed projects, 80-85 generative AI use cases, and 18-20% enterprise adoption. These outcomes are improving Singapore's global AI readiness rank and increasing the AI sector's GDP share, while responsible AI training and governance are building public trust, though concerns about job displacement and data privacy persist. AI talent retention is supported by attractive career pathways but limited by competition from global tech hubs. There was consensus on a global AI readiness rank of 5, AI sector GDP share of 3.5%, public trust of 65%, and talent retention of 70%, with one dissenting voice estimating more conservative values of rank 6, 3.0%, 60%, and 65% due to slower deployment from responsible AI audits and integration challenges. The central tendency across predictions was rank 5, GDP share 3.5%, trust 65%, and retention 70%, with a range of rank 5-6, GDP share 3.0-3.5%, trust 60-65%, and retention 65-70%.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all input estimates, treating the S$500 million total funding and S$330,000 per-project co-funding cap as directly stated in policy. Compute capacity was uniformly estimated at 500,000 GPU hours, based on typical national programme allocations, and trainer personnel at 50, reflecting a dedicated team for enterprise guidance. No disagreements emerged, though some participants expressed concerns about equitable access for smaller enterprises and the need for domain-specific expertise among trainers. The central tendency for all four variables was identical across participants, with no variation in numeric predictions.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed how activities like enterprise outreach, matchmaking, training, and platform onboarding drive the 100E programme's goals, with a focus on balancing quality and quantity of engagements. There was consensus on moderate estimates for outreach sessions (30-40, central tendency ~35), matchmaking events (8-12, central ~10), training workshops (15-25, central ~20), and onboarding assists (40-60, central ~50), reflecting a shared view that 50 trainers and S$500M funding support a balanced rollout. Disagreements centered on the pace of adoption across sectors: some argued for higher numbers to maximize engagement, while others emphasized lower, more targeted efforts for slower-adopting sectors like healthcare. The lead practitioner's higher estimates (40, 12, 25, 60) contrasted with Siti's lower ones (30, 8, 15, 40), with most participants converging on intermediate values (35, 10, 20, 50) to ensure quality and feasibility.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed the 100E programme's first-year outputs, focusing on funding constraints, compute resources, and enterprise readiness as key causal mechanisms. There was consensus that the S$500 million total funding and S$330,000 co-funding cap per project would limit approvals, with estimates ranging from 30 to 35 projects, centering around 30-35. Disagreement emerged on the pace of adoption, with some participants expecting cautious uptake in regulated sectors like healthcare and finance, leading to lower estimates for prototypes (20-25), trainees (30-40), and enterprises with solutions (25-30). The central tendency for approved projects was 30-35, prototypes 20-25, trainees 30-40, and enterprises with solutions 25-30.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed that moving from prototype to production is a major bottleneck, with deployment estimates ranging from 6 to 10 projects (central tendency ~8) out of 20-35 approved, due to validation, integration, and regulatory hurdles. There was consensus that the AI practitioner pool (11,000-12,000) falls short of the 15,000 target, and generative AI use cases (70-80) are below the 100 target, with enterprise AI adoption (20-25%) still in early experimentation. Disagreement centered on deployment optimism: some participants estimated higher (10) based on broader sector progress, while others were more cautious (6) citing slower adoption in regulated sectors like healthcare and finance.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus on all impact estimates, predicting Singapore's global AI readiness rank at 8th, AI sector GDP share at 4.5%, public AI trust at 62%, and AI talent retention at 78%. Key causal mechanisms included the S$500M investment and 100E programme outputs (30-35 approved projects, 20-25 prototypes) driving ecosystem growth, while cautious adoption in regulated sectors like healthcare and finance limited faster trust and deployment. Participants agreed that talent retention was moderate due to competitive global offers, and that the AI sector's GDP share remained below mature economies. No disagreements emerged, though some noted slightly lower output/outcome estimates (e.g., 6-8 deployed projects, 20-22% enterprise adoption) led to marginally more cautious impact views, but the overall trajectory was consistent.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on the four input variables, with all participants agreeing on the same values: total_funding_sgd of 500,000,000, max_co_funding_per_project_sgd of 330,000, available_gpu_hours of 500,000, and trainer_personnel_count of 50. These estimates were grounded in explicit policy commitments (e.g., 'Over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D' and 'Co-funding up to S$330,000 per project for 100 Experiments') and reasonable assumptions about compute allocation and trainer-to-practitioner ratios based on typical national AI program scales. No disagreements emerged, as participants consistently cited the same evidence and reasoning, emphasizing the importance of compute infrastructure and personalized guidance for enterprise AI adoption and practitioner upskilling.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed the causal chain from outreach and matchmaking to project approvals and deployments, emphasizing that enterprise outreach sessions generate problem statements, matchmaking events pair researchers with challenges, training workshops upskill practitioners, and platform onboarding assists enable technical setup. There was consensus that the 100E programme's target of 100 projects requires a moderate scale of activities, with estimates clustering around 150-200 outreach sessions, 40-50 matchmaking events, 80-100 training workshops, and 240-300 onboarding assists. Disagreements centered on the extent of prior enterprise awareness: some argued that many enterprises already know about AI opportunities, reducing the need for extensive outreach (favoring lower estimates), while others emphasized the need for a robust pipeline to generate sufficient problem statements (favoring higher estimates). The central tendency across participants was approximately 168 outreach sessions, 43 matchmaking events, 86 training workshops, and 262 onboarding assists, reflecting a balanced view of resource allocation.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached strong consensus that the 100E programme would approve exactly 100 projects in 2023, aligning with its explicit policy target. Most participants estimated 85 AI prototypes developed, 120 trainees completing the programme, and 80 enterprises with a solution, citing typical success rates and cohort structures. One participant offered slightly lower estimates (80 prototypes, 100 trainees, 70 enterprises), citing real-world frictions in social impact projects. Overall, the central tendency for prototypes was 80–85, trainees 100–120, and enterprises with solutions 70–80.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached full consensus on all five outcome variables, estimating 100 approved 100E projects, 60 deployed projects, 12,000 AI practitioners, 80 generative AI use cases, and a 25% enterprise AI adoption rate. Key causal mechanisms included real-world frictions (data privacy, integration challenges, organizational inertia) limiting prototype-to-production conversion to about 60%, and the AI practitioner pool growing but remaining below the 15,000 target due to limited trainee throughput. All participants agreed that the 100E programme's explicit target of 100 approvals was fixed, and that the AI Trailblazers programme would achieve about 80 of its 100 use-case goal within the year. No disagreements emerged; estimates and reasoning were identical across all participants.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached full consensus on all four impact variables, predicting Singapore's global AI readiness rank would improve from ~7th to 5th, AI sector GDP share would reach 4.2%, public AI trust would settle at 68%, and AI talent retention would hit 82%. Key causal mechanisms included the 100E programme's 60 deployed projects and 80 enterprises with solutions, a 12,000 AI practitioner pool, and 80 generative AI use cases driving ecosystem maturity. The 25% enterprise AI adoption rate and multiplier effects in e-commerce and logistics were cited for GDP contribution, while trust was seen as cautiously optimistic due to governance efforts tempered by job displacement and ethics concerns. Talent retention was attributed to hands-on training and career progression from AIAP and 100E. No disagreements emerged; all participants aligned on estimates and reasoning.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on the key input variables for the 100E programme, with all participants agreeing on total funding of S$500 million and a co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project, both explicitly stated in policy. Estimates for available GPU hours and trainer personnel count were also unanimous at 1,000,000 hours and 200 trainers, respectively, based on reasoning about typical allocations for national AI initiatives and the scale of upskilling programmes. No disagreements emerged, as participants consistently cited the same policy evidence and aligned on the causal logic that these inputs are necessary to support project approvals and deployments. The central tendency for all four variables is thus a single point estimate, reflecting a shared understanding of the resource inputs required.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group focused on the need for high-quality, targeted engagement over broad outreach, emphasizing that meaningful enterprise problem identification and researcher-enterprise pairings require deeper, more selective sessions. There was strong consensus on estimates of 40 enterprise outreach sessions, 15 researcher matchmaking events, 30 training workshops, and 80 platform onboarding assists, with all but one participant converging on these values. The sole dissenter proposed higher numbers (50, 20, 40, 100), reflecting a more optimistic view of the programme's capacity to conduct broader activities. The central tendency across all participants is 42 outreach sessions, 16 matchmaking events, 32 workshops, and 84 onboarding assists, with ranges of 40-50, 15-20, 30-40, and 80-100 respectively.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed that the 100E programme's outputs are constrained by the limited pipeline from outreach and matchmaking activities, with technical hurdles and data access issues reducing the number of prototypes built and deployed. There was consensus that not all approved projects lead to prototypes, and not all prototypes are adopted by enterprises, with estimates for approved projects ranging from 70 to 80, prototypes from 50 to 60, trainees from 120 to 150, and enterprises with solutions from 60 to 70. Disagreement centered on the selectivity of the matchmaking process and the impact of attrition on trainee numbers, leading to a central tendency around 70-75 approved projects, 50-55 prototypes, 120-130 trainees, and 60-65 enterprises with solutions.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group focused on the gap between project approval and production deployment, citing technical, regulatory, and data integration hurdles as key bottlenecks, with deployment rates estimated at 60-70% of approved projects. There was strong consensus on most estimates: 50 deployed projects, 12,000 AI practitioners, 80 generative AI use cases, and a 12.5% enterprise AI adoption rate, all reflecting cautious optimism tempered by practical challenges. The only minor divergence was in the number of approved projects, ranging from 70 to 80, with a central tendency around 75. Overall, the group agreed that while the 100E programme shows promise, real-world constraints limit outcomes, especially for SMEs needing more support.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that NAIS 2.0 and the 100E programme's 50 deployed projects and 12,000 AI practitioners drive ecosystem maturity, but global rankings are constrained by competition from the US and China. There was consensus that Singapore's AI readiness rank would be around 5-6, AI GDP share 3.5-3.8%, public trust 58-62%, and talent retention 68-72%. Disagreement centered on the exact rank (5 vs. 6) and GDP share (3.5% vs. 3.8%), reflecting differing views on SME adoption and global competition. The central tendency was rank 6, GDP share 3.6%, trust 60%, and retention 70%.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached full consensus on all input variables, grounding their estimates in explicit policy text from NAIS 2.0: total funding at S$500 million and co-funding cap at S$330,000 per project were treated as fixed facts. For compute, all participants estimated 50,000 GPU hours, reasoning that this allocation would support the 100 experiments and additional use cases given typical per-experiment needs. Trainer personnel count was uniformly set at 50, based on the target of boosting the AI practitioner pool to 15,000, with each trainer able to mentor cohorts over time. No disagreements emerged, as all participants referenced the same policy resource logic and aligned on the causal link between these inputs and the programme's capacity to approve and deploy 100E projects.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed the 100E programme's activities as a targeted pipeline to generate quality enterprise problem statements and build AI capabilities, emphasizing that outreach and matchmaking should prioritize quality over quantity due to the eligibility requirement for problems lacking off-the-shelf solutions. Consensus emerged that training workshops and platform onboarding are critical for building the 15,000 AI practitioner pool, with all participants agreeing on the need for hands-on support. Disagreement centered on the scale of activities: estimates for enterprise outreach sessions ranged from 100 to 150, matchmaking events from 12 to 20, training workshops from 60 to 100, and platform onboarding assists from 200 to 300, reflecting different views on the balance between broad engagement and focused curation. The central tendency across predictions was approximately 122 outreach sessions, 16 matchmaking events, 80 workshops, and 246 onboarding assists.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached a strong consensus that exactly 100 projects would be approved in 2023, based on the policy's explicit '100 Experiments' target and co-funding cap. They agreed that not all approved projects would yield a prototype or solution within the same year, with estimates for prototypes developed ranging from 75 to 80 (central tendency 75) and for enterprises with a solution ranging from 65 to 70 (central tendency 65). All participants estimated 200 trainees completing the programme, citing the 9-month duration and trainer capacity. The main divergence was a slightly higher estimate from one participant for prototypes (80) and enterprises (70), reflecting a more optimistic view of project completion rates. Overall, the group's reasoning emphasized resource constraints, development timelines, and the structured nature of the programme.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus that the 100E programme's explicit target of 100 approved projects in 2023 is a fixed policy goal, but deployment to production is constrained by integration and scaling challenges, with all participants estimating 50 deployed projects. The AI practitioner pool was uniformly estimated at 12,000, below the 15,000 target, due to the 9-month apprenticeship program limiting cohort completion. Generative AI use cases were consistently estimated at 80, reflecting a learning curve for enterprises new to the technology, and enterprise AI adoption was pegged at 25%, indicating early-stage adoption among SMEs. No disagreements emerged; all predictions and reasoning aligned across participants.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached full consensus on all estimates, projecting that NAIS 2.0 and the 100E programme will improve Singapore's global AI readiness rank to 5th, contribute 3.5% to GDP, achieve 65% public trust in AI, and retain 80% of AI talent. Key causal mechanisms included the deployment of 50 100E projects and 80 generative AI use cases driving readiness and economic impact, while the government's focus on responsible AI and a trusted environment underpinned trust and retention. No disagreements emerged, with all participants citing consistent evidence from prior phases.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all input variables, treating total funding (S$500 million) and co-funding cap (S$330,000 per project) as explicit policy ground truths. For compute capacity, all participants estimated 2 million GPU hours, reasoning that Singapore's infrastructure investment must support both industry and research experiments. Trainer personnel count was uniformly estimated at 50, based on typical staffing needs for programmes like 100E and AIAP, though this value was not explicitly stated in policy documents. No disagreements emerged across any variable.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed how the 100E programme's activities—outreach, matchmaking, workshops, and onboarding—scale from inputs like S$500 million funding and 50 trainers, with causal links to project approvals and deployments. There was consensus that outreach sessions and matchmaking events are resource-intensive, requiring careful curation, and that trainer capacity limits all activities. Disagreement centered on the pace of outreach (100-120 sessions) and matchmaking (30-40 events), with some favoring higher numbers based on ambition and others lower due to practical constraints. Training workshops were consistently estimated at 50-60, and platform onboarding assists at 150-200, with central tendencies around 100 outreach, 30 matchmaking, 50 workshops, and 150 onboarding assists.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group broadly agreed that the 100E programme would approve 100 projects in 2023, directly matching the policy's stated goal of generating 100 generative AI use cases. There was strong consensus that approximately 75 AI prototypes would be developed, with participants citing technical challenges like data preparation and model tuning as reasons not all approved projects would reach that stage. All participants estimated 200 trainees would complete the programme, based on the 9-month AIAP duration and trainer capacity. For enterprises with at least one AI solution, estimates ranged from 85 to 90, with most settling on 85, reflecting a view that some projects would be abandoned or not yet deployed. The central tendency across all variables was: approved projects 100, prototypes 75, trainees 200, enterprises with solution 85.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed that the 100E programme's approval target aligns directly with the policy goal of 100 generative AI use cases in 100 days, leading to consensus on 100 approved projects. Deployment into production was seen as more challenging, with estimates ranging from 45 to 50 deployed projects, reflecting concerns about integration, testing, and scaling hurdles. The AI practitioner pool was estimated between 11,500 and 12,000, below the 15,000 target, due to training cycle constraints and potential attrition. Enterprise AI adoption rates were estimated at 12% to 15%, with lower estimates citing fairness and reliability concerns as slowing adoption. The central tendency across all variables was 100 approved projects, 47-48 deployed projects, 11,750 practitioners, 100 use cases, and a 13.5% adoption rate.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached full consensus that the 100E programme's 100 approved and 45-50 deployed projects would improve Singapore's global AI readiness rank from 6th to 5th, grow the AI sector's GDP share to 3.2%, raise public trust in AI to 62%, and retain 78% of AI talent. All participants cited the same causal mechanisms: practical use cases from deployments drive readiness and trust, nascent adoption (12-15%) limits immediate GDP impact, and global competition for talent causes some attrition. No disagreements emerged, with all estimates identical across the five participants.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all four input variables, with every participant estimating total funding at S$500 million, co-funding cap at S$330,000, GPU hours at 500,000, and trainer count at 50. The key causal mechanism discussed was that these inputs—funding, compute, and expert trainers—are essential to support the 100E programme's goal of enabling dozens of AI projects and training up to 15,000 practitioners. No disagreements emerged; all participants anchored their estimates on explicit policy statements for funding and co-funding, and independently reasoned that 500,000 GPU hours and 50 trainers are plausible allocations for a national programme of this scale. The central tendency and range for each variable were identical across all posts.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed the causal mechanisms linking program inputs (S$500M funding, 500,000 GPU hours, 50 trainers) to activities, emphasizing that enterprise outreach sessions generate problem statements, matchmaking events pair researchers with challenges, training workshops upskill practitioners, and platform onboarding assists translate compute resources into usage. There was unanimous consensus on all four activity estimates: 40 outreach sessions, 12 matchmaking events, 30 training workshops, and 100 onboarding assists, with reasoning consistently citing the need to cover diverse sectors, ensure high-quality pairings, reach the 15,000 practitioner target, and provide technical support. No disagreements emerged; all participants independently arrived at identical predictions. The central tendency and range for each variable were fixed: enterprise_outreach_sessions=40, researcher_matchmaking_events=12, training_workshops_held=30, platform_onboarding_assists=100.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group consistently reasoned from the S$500 million funding and S$330,000 co-funding cap to estimate 100 approved projects, with an 80% prototype success rate yielding 80 prototypes. They agreed on 5,000 trainees completing the programme, derived from 30 workshops training 15,000 enrollees with a 30-40% completion rate, and 60 enterprises with a solution based on typical pairing ratios. No disagreements emerged; all participants independently converged on identical estimates, reflecting strong consensus on the causal logic linking inputs to outputs.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached full consensus on all outcome estimates, projecting 100 approved 100E projects, 40 deployed projects (a 50% conversion from prototypes), 12,000 AI practitioners (below the 15,000 target), 100 generative AI use cases (treated as a policy target), and a 25% enterprise AI adoption rate among engaged enterprises. Key causal mechanisms included a consistent 50% prototype-to-production conversion rate due to integration and scaling challenges, and the AI practitioner pool being built from 5,000 programme completers plus an existing base. No disagreements emerged, with all participants aligning on the same numeric values and reasoning.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group unanimously agreed that the 100E programme's tangible outputs—40 deployed projects, 100 generative AI use cases, and 25% enterprise adoption—would drive Singapore's global AI readiness rank from ~10th to 8th, boost AI's GDP share to 3.5%, achieve 65% public trust, and retain 70% of AI talent. All participants estimated identical values (8, 3.5%, 65%, 70%), citing the same causal mechanisms: deployed solutions demonstrate capability, economic value from adoption, cautious public optimism, and talent retention balanced by global competition. No disagreements emerged; the consensus reflected shared reasoning that the programme's outcomes directly translate into improved international standing, economic impact, moderate trust, and a stable talent pool.
