phase_summaries.Inputs: The group agreed on two fixed inputs from policy: total funding of S$500 million and a co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project. For compute capacity, estimates ranged from 800,000 to 1,000,000 GPU hours, with most converging around 850,000–900,000 hours, reflecting a balance between industry allocation and reserved capacity. Trainer personnel estimates spanned 40 to 50, centering near 42–45, with consensus that a lean but capable team is needed to support the ambitious target of 15,000 practitioners. Disagreements centered on the exact split of compute resources and the optimal size of the training team, with some viewing higher estimates as overly optimistic for a lean programme.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed that the 100E programme's activities—enterprise outreach, researcher matchmaking, training workshops, and platform onboarding—are causally linked to achieving the targets of 100 projects approved and deployed, with resource constraints (S$500M funding, ~42-50 trainers, ~800K-1M GPU hours) shaping activity levels. There was consensus that outreach sessions should range from 60-80 (central tendency ~70) to attract quality problem statements without overextension, and that matchmaking events (8-12, central ~10) and training workshops (20-30, central ~25) must balance depth with trainer capacity. Disagreement centered on the scale of activities: some argued for leaner numbers (e.g., 60 outreach, 20 workshops) to manage quality and avoid strain, while others favored higher estimates (e.g., 80 outreach, 30 workshops) to ensure sufficient pipeline and practitioner coverage. Platform onboarding assists were estimated between 150-200 (central ~180), reflecting differing assumptions about projects' existing technical capabilities. Overall, the group converged on moderate, quality-focused activity levels, with a slight leaning toward the lower end of the ranges to ensure programme effectiveness.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed how the 100E programme's outputs are shaped by the interplay of inputs (funding, compute, trainers) and activities (outreach, matchmaking, workshops, onboarding). There was consensus that the programme would not meet its full 100-project target in 2023, with estimates for approved projects ranging from 80 to 90 (central tendency ~85). Disagreement centered on the level of activity: some argued for higher outreach and compute utilization to achieve more prototypes and trainees, while others cautioned that early-stage constraints would limit outputs. Predictions for prototypes developed ranged from 60 to 75 (central ~70), trainees completed from 100 to 130 (central ~115), and enterprises with solutions from 55 to 70 (central ~65).
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed that outcomes are driven by inputs, activities, and outputs, with deployment rates constrained by integration challenges and testing delays. There was consensus that the number of 100E projects approved in 2023 would be between 80 and 90, and that deployed projects would be lower, with most participants estimating 50-55 deployed from 80-85 approved. Disagreement centered on the AI practitioner pool and generative AI use cases: one participant estimated reaching the 15,000 target and 100 use cases, while the majority projected around 12,000 practitioners and 80 use cases. Enterprise AI adoption rate estimates clustered around 20%, with one higher estimate of 25%. The central tendency for deployed projects was 55, for practitioners 12,000, for use cases 80, and for adoption rate 20%.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that the impact phase reflects foundational progress driven by policy inputs, infrastructure, and the 100E programme, with causal mechanisms linking moderate activity levels (e.g., 70 outreach sessions, 55 deployed projects) to modest improvements in global readiness, GDP share, trust, and talent retention. There was consensus on a global AI readiness rank of 8, public trust at 55%, and talent retention at 70%, with most agreeing that talent gaps, global competition, and limited SME adoption constrain higher impacts. Disagreement centered on Priya's more optimistic estimates (rank 6, GDP 4.5%, trust 60%, retention 75%), which others argued were unsupported by the lean team and moderate resource allocation. Numeric predictions ranged from rank 6-8, GDP share 4.0-4.5%, trust 55-60%, and retention 70-75%, with central tendencies at rank 8, GDP 4.0%, trust 55%, and retention 70%.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all input variables, treating the policy's stated S$500 million total funding and S$330,000 co-funding cap per project as ground truth. They agreed that 500,000 GPU hours and 50 trainers were realistic for the 2023 ramp-up phase, citing typical cloud compute budgets, the time needed to provision infrastructure, and the sufficiency of a small expert team to guide early-stage practitioners. No disagreements emerged; all estimates were identical, reflecting a shared understanding of the programme's initial scale and resource constraints.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group focused on the 50 trainers as the primary capacity constraint, estimating activities based on a cautious, quality-focused ramp-up in 2023. There was full consensus on all estimates: 80 enterprise outreach sessions (1-2 per trainer per week), 30 researcher matchmaking events (limited by careful pairing), 40 training workshops (one per trainer per month), and 200 platform onboarding assists (4 per trainer). No disagreements emerged, with all participants converging on identical numeric predictions and reasoning centered on personalized support and early-stage momentum-building.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached full consensus on all output estimates, with every participant predicting 80 approved projects, 60 prototypes, 150 trainees, and 70 enterprises with solutions. The causal reasoning centered on the co-funding cap and compute hours limiting approvals, typical project timelines reducing prototypes to 75% of approvals, trainer capacity capping apprenticeships, and early-stage adoption limiting enterprise solutions. No disagreements emerged; all participants independently converged on identical numeric predictions and aligned on the same underlying mechanisms.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus on all outcome estimates, predicting 80 approved 100E projects, 40 deployed projects, 12,000 AI practitioners, 80 generative AI use cases, and a 12.5% enterprise AI adoption rate. Key causal mechanisms included typical AI project lifecycles where only half of prototypes reach production in the same year, the AI practitioner pool growing from 150 trainees plus other upskilling but still short of the 15,000 target, and the AI Trailblazers programme yielding 80 use cases due to ramp-up and enterprise readiness constraints. The enterprise adoption rate of 12.5% was seen as reflecting cautious early-stage uptake, consistent with typical adoption curves. No disagreements emerged, as all participants independently arrived at identical estimates and reasoning.
phase_summaries.Impact: All participants reached a strong consensus on the impact phase, predicting that Singapore's global AI readiness rank would improve to 5th, the AI sector's GDP share would reach 3.5%, public trust in AI would be 65%, and AI talent retention would be 85%. They attributed these outcomes to the 100E programme's early successes—80 approved projects, 40 deployed, 12,000 practitioners, 80 generative AI use cases, and 12.5% enterprise adoption—combined with the government's proactive strategy, governance, transparency, and ecosystem-building efforts. No disagreements were expressed; all estimates and reasoning were identical, reflecting a unified view that the programme's foundation and Singapore's proactive stance drive tangible progress in readiness, economic impact, trust, and talent retention.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group unanimously agreed on all input estimates, drawing directly from stated policy commitments: total funding of S$500 million and a co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project were treated as ground truth. For compute capacity, participants converged on 1 million GPU hours annually, reasoning from typical national AI programme allocations and the scale needed for industry experimentation. phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached full consensus on all input variables, grounding estimates in explicit policy statements from NAIS 2.0. They unanimously set total funding at S$500 million and the co-funding cap at S$330,000 per project, citing the resource logic as authoritative. For compute, all participants estimated 5 million GPU hours annually, reasoning that this aligns with typical national AI infrastructure investments to support both industry experiments and training. The trainer count was uniformly set at 50, based on the scale needed to mentor enterprises and the AI Apprenticeship Programme cohort to achieve the goal of 15,000 AI practitioners. No disagreements emerged, with all participants reinforcing the same evidence and causal logic linking these inputs to the policy's ambitions.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached full consensus on all activity estimates, with every participant predicting 60 enterprise outreach sessions, 20 researcher matchmaking events, 40 training workshops, and 100 platform onboarding assists. The core causal mechanism was that these activities directly convert substantial inputs (S$500 million funding, 5 million GPU hours, 50 trainers, S$330,000 co-funding cap) into a robust pipeline of problem statements and projects to meet the 100E target of 100 projects. Participants consistently reasoned that 60 outreach sessions ensure diverse sector engagement, 20 matchmaking events leverage co-funding to pair researchers with enterprises, 40 workshops effectively utilize trainers (0.8 per trainer) to upskill 15,000 practitioners, and 100 onboarding assists provide necessary technical support for the AI experimentation platform. No disagreements or alternative estimates were presented.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached full consensus that the 100E programme's substantial inputs (S$500M funding, 5M GPU hours, 50 trainers) and activities (outreach, matchmaking, workshops) would enable approval of all 100 targeted projects in 2023. They agreed that about 80% of approved projects (80 prototypes) would yield a working prototype within the first year due to technical challenges, and that 150 trainees would complete the programme given 40 workshops and 100 onboarding assists. All participants estimated 70 enterprises would have at least one AI solution, citing that some enterprises may participate in multiple projects or face deployment delays. No disagreements emerged; all predictions were identical across all four variables, with a central tendency of 100 approved projects, 80 prototypes, 150 trainees, and 70 enterprises with solutions.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached full consensus on all outcome estimates, predicting that all 100 100E projects would be approved in 2023, with 50 deployed into production by year-end, citing technical and integration challenges as the primary reason for the 50% deployment rate. They estimated the AI practitioner pool at 12,000 (approaching but not reaching the 15,000 target), and agreed that the AI Trailblazers programme would achieve its goal of 100 generative AI use cases in 100 days. The enterprise AI adoption rate was uniformly forecast at 25%, reflecting typical early-stage adoption curves. No disagreements emerged, with all participants aligning on the causal mechanisms linking policy inputs (funding, compute, training) to these outcomes.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that the 100 Experiments programme's outputs (100 approved projects, 50 deployed, 12,000 practitioners, 100 generative AI use cases, 25% enterprise adoption) would improve Singapore's global AI readiness rank from 6th to 5th, raise AI's GDP share to 3.5%, achieve 60% public trust, and retain 70% of AI talent. Key causal mechanisms included concrete deployments and talent development driving readiness and GDP, while transparent governance and successful public-sector use cases built trust, though job displacement and ethical concerns limited it. Talent retention was supported by hands-on training and career pathways but constrained by global competition. No disagreements emerged; all estimates were identical, reflecting a shared view of the programme's moderate but positive impact.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached full consensus on all four input variables, with every participant estimating total funding at S$500 million, co-funding cap at S$330,000 per project, 500,000 GPU hours, and 50 trainers. These values were directly anchored to explicit policy statements (S$500 million committed, S$330,000 cap) and reasoned from typical national AI programme scales for compute and trainer staffing. No disagreements emerged, as all participants cited the same evidence and aligned on the plausible resource allocations needed to support the 100 Experiments programme.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed that the 100E programme's activities should prioritize quality over quantity in enterprise outreach and researcher matchmaking, given the specialized nature of the program and the need for deep collaboration. There was consensus that outreach sessions should be moderate (60-80, central tendency ~65-70), matchmaking events limited (15-20, central ~16-18), and training workshops around 25-30 (central ~28) due to trainer capacity constraints. Disagreement centered on platform onboarding assists, with estimates ranging from 80 to 100 (central ~85-90), reflecting differing views on demand from SMEs and the impact of free cloud toolsets. All participants agreed that the S$500 million funding and 50 trainers set the scale, and that activities should be focused on quality engagement to drive project approvals and deployments.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached strong consensus on all output estimates, predicting 80 approved projects, 60 AI prototypes, 200 trainees completing the programme, and 70 enterprises with at least one AI solution. Key causal mechanisms included the rigorous selection process limiting approvals despite ample funding, a 75% prototype success rate from approved projects, and training workshops yielding 7-8 completions each. No disagreements emerged, with all participants aligning on the same numeric values and reasoning.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus on all outcome estimates, with every participant predicting 80 approved 100E projects, 48 deployed projects, 12,000 AI practitioners, 100 generative AI use cases, and a 25% enterprise AI adoption rate. The central causal mechanism was a consistent 60% deployment success rate from approved projects to production, grounded in shared experience with technical integration, regulatory, and enterprise readiness challenges. The AI practitioner pool was seen as below the 15,000 target due to the ramp-up phase of training programs, while the 100 generative AI use cases were treated as a policy-guaranteed outcome from the AI Trailblazers programme. No disagreements emerged; all estimates and reasoning aligned perfectly.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group unanimously agreed that the NAIS 2.0 programme's inputs (S$500 million, 500,000 GPU hours, 50 trainers) and outputs (80 approved 100E projects, 48 deployed, 12,000 practitioners, 100 generative AI use cases, 25% enterprise adoption) would improve Singapore's global AI readiness rank to 8th, raise AI's GDP share to 4.5%, achieve 65% public trust, and retain 80% of AI talent. All participants converged on identical numeric estimates, with no disagreement. The causal mechanisms cited include strong government commitment and infrastructure boosting readiness, deployed solutions driving economic value, successful deployments and governance building trust, and attractive research opportunities retaining talent despite global competition.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached full consensus on all input variables, treating total funding (S$500 million) and co-funding cap (S$330,000 per project) as fixed policy parameters from NAIS 2.0. They estimated 500,000 GPU hours for AI experimentation in 2023, reasoning that Singapore's compute infrastructure must balance 100E with other initiatives, and a trainer count of 50, seen as a core team sufficient to guide enterprises without overstaffing. No disagreements emerged, with all participants aligning on the same numeric predictions and causal logic.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group focused on the constraint of 50 trainers and the 100E programme being one of many under NAIS 2.0, leading to conservative activity estimates. There was consensus that resource limitations would keep numbers moderate, with most participants agreeing on 35 outreach sessions, 10 matchmaking events, 18 training workshops, and 120 onboarding assists. A single participant estimated higher values (40, 12, 20, 150), citing the S$500 million funding and 500,000 GPU hours, while others considered this optimistic. The range for outreach sessions was 30-40, matchmaking events 10-12, training workshops 15-20, and onboarding assists 100-150, with central tendencies around 35, 10, 18, and 120 respectively.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group consistently estimated that the 100E programme would approve about 30 projects in 2023, constrained by a S$500 million total fund and a S$330,000 co-funding cap, with not all funds dedicated to 100E. They agreed that 500,000 GPU hours and 50 trainers would support roughly 20 AI prototypes, and that 18 workshops and 120 onboarding assists would yield around 150 trainees completing the 9-month programme after attrition. Enterprises with a solution were estimated at 25, as a subset of approved projects that achieve a deployable prototype within the year. There was no disagreement; all participants converged on identical numeric predictions and reasoning, citing resource constraints and programme scope.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus that of the 30 approved 100E projects, only about 40% (12) would be deployed into production by end of 2023, citing the 9-month programme duration and typical integration delays. They agreed the AI practitioner pool would be around 12,000, short of the 15,000 target, as the 150 trainees and other upskilling efforts add incrementally. For generative AI use cases, the group estimated 80 out of the 100-day target were created, due to enterprise readiness constraints. Enterprise AI adoption was pegged at 18.5%, reflecting that while 25 enterprises gained solutions from 100E, broader SME adoption remains nascent. No disagreements emerged; all participants aligned on estimates and causal reasoning.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that Singapore's targeted investments and the 100E programme will modestly improve its global AI readiness rank from 6th to 5th, with the AI sector's GDP share rising to 3.5% from 12 deployed projects and broader enterprise adoption. Public trust in AI was uniformly estimated at 65%, reflecting cautious optimism tempered by data privacy and job displacement concerns, while AI talent retention was pegged at 80% due to hands-on experience and local career opportunities, despite some pull from global tech hubs. No disagreements emerged across the four variables, with all participants aligning on the same numeric values and causal mechanisms linking programme outputs to these outcomes.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on the input estimates, with all participants predicting total funding of S$500 million, co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project, 500,000 GPU hours, and 50 trainers, citing explicit policy context as ground truth. Key themes centered on scalability concerns: the compute allocation and trainer count were widely seen as insufficient to meet demand from both research and industry, potentially causing queue times and stretched mentorship capacity. The co-funding cap was considered reasonable for prototyping but tight for hardware-intensive projects. No disagreement emerged on numeric values or reasoning, as all aligned on the same estimates and shared worries about resource bottlenecks.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached full consensus on all activity estimates, projecting 40 enterprise outreach sessions, 12 researcher matchmaking events, 20 training workshops, and 150 platform onboarding assists. They reasoned that the S$500 million funding, 50 trainers, and 500,000 GPU hours constrain activities to a focused but not massive scale, with the 100E programme's target of 100 experiments driving the need for sufficient problem statements. All participants agreed that 40 outreach sessions can generate enough enterprise challenges, 20 workshops are feasible given trainer capacity, 12 matchmaking events balance coordination demands, and high onboarding assists (150) are critical due to SMEs' lack of technical expertise, as evidenced by prior programmes like AIAP. No disagreements emerged, with estimates and reasoning uniformly aligned across all stakeholders.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group consistently estimated that resource constraints—namely the S$330,000 co-funding cap, 500,000 GPU hours, and 50 trainers—would limit approvals to about 80 projects (range 80–80), with only 60 prototypes successfully built due to compute bottlenecks and mentorship shortages. Trainee completion was pegged at 40 (range 40–40), reflecting typical AIAP cohort sizes and trainer bandwidth, while enterprises with at least one solution were estimated at 50 (range 50–50), accounting for some enterprises having multiple projects and others failing. There was full consensus on all estimates and the underlying causal mechanisms, with no disagreement expressed.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group consistently identified a significant gap between prototype development and production deployment, citing integration challenges, limited compute resources (500,000 GPU hours), and insufficient trainer support (50 trainers) as key bottlenecks. There was strong consensus across all participants on the numeric estimates: 80 approved projects, 40 deployed projects, 12,000 AI practitioners, 80 generative AI use cases, and a 25% enterprise AI adoption rate. No disagreements emerged, with all reasoning aligning on the constraints slowing progress toward the 15,000 practitioner and 100 use-case targets.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that 2023 impact is constrained by limited compute (500,000 GPU hours) and trainer capacity (50 trainers), resulting in a global AI readiness rank of 8th, AI sector GDP share of 3.5%, public trust at 60%, and talent retention at 70%. All participants agreed that 80 approved and 40 deployed 100E projects, plus 80 generative AI use cases, demonstrate tangible progress but are insufficient for a major leap in global standing. The 25% enterprise adoption rate and experimental nature of deployments were cited as key limits on economic contribution and public trust. Talent retention is seen as moderate, with the 40 programme trainees likely to stay but the broader 12,000-practitioner pool facing outflow pressure from countries offering more compute and higher salaries. No disagreements emerged; all predictions and reasoning were aligned.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached full consensus on all four input variables, treating total funding (S$500 million) and max co-funding per project (S$330,000) as fixed policy commitments. They estimated 500,000 GPU hours and 50 trainers as plausible allocations based on typical national AI programme scales, with reasoning centered on supporting dozens of teams and the AI Apprenticeship Programme. No disagreements emerged, and all predictions were identical across participants.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed the need to balance the quantity of activities with quality engagement, emphasizing that effective enterprise outreach and researcher matchmaking require careful curation to ensure meaningful partnerships. There was consensus on moderate estimates for outreach sessions (30-40), matchmaking events (10-12), training workshops (15-20), and onboarding assists (100-150), with a central tendency around 35, 10, 18, and 120 respectively. Disagreements centered on whether to prioritize broader reach (higher estimates) or deeper, trust-building interactions (lower estimates), reflecting different views on capacity and the importance of inclusive, responsible AI deployment. The group also highlighted the practical constraints of 50 trainers and S$500 million funding, with estimates grounded in the need for hands-on support and alignment with the 100E programme's co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed the 100E programme's outputs, focusing on how practical constraints like outreach sessions and matchmaking events limit first-year approvals, with a success rate of 60-65% for prototype development and deployment. There was consensus that the S$500 million funding and S$330,000 co-funding cap allow for many projects, but careful curation and ethical review reduce throughput. Disagreements centered on the pace of approvals and the impact of responsible AI practices, with some favoring lower numbers for quality. Numeric predictions ranged from 50-60 approved projects, 30-40 prototypes, 60-80 trainees, and 40-50 enterprises with solutions, with central tendencies around 55, 35, 70, and 45 respectively.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group focused on the translation of 100E project approvals into production deployments, with deployment rates constrained by integration challenges, rigorous testing, and ethical review. Consensus emerged on several estimates: 55 approved projects, 25 deployed projects, 12,000 AI practitioners, 80 generative AI use cases, and a 15% enterprise AI adoption rate. One participant offered lower estimates (20 deployed, 11,000 practitioners, 70 use cases, 12% adoption) due to a stronger emphasis on responsible AI practices slowing deployment. The central tendency across participants was 50-55 approved projects, 20-25 deployed, 11,000-12,000 practitioners, 70-80 use cases, and 12-15% adoption.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that the Impact phase of NAIS 2.0 in 2023 shows tangible progress, with 55 approved 100E projects, 25 deployed solutions, and 12,000 AI practitioners, leading to improved global AI readiness and economic contributions. There was consensus that Singapore's global AI readiness rank would improve to 6th, driven by strong governance and responsible AI practices, though still trailing larger economies. Disagreements centered on the AI sector's GDP share (ranging from 3.2% to 3.5%) and public trust in AI (60% to 65%), with some participants emphasizing that ethical rigor and community skepticism slow commercial deployment and trust-building, while others highlighted demonstrated value in manufacturing and public services. AI talent retention estimates clustered around 75-78%, reflecting a balance between program success and global competition. The central tendency of predictions was a rank of 6, GDP share of 3.3%, trust score of 62%, and retention rate of 76%.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached full consensus on all input estimates, grounding total funding at S$500 million and the co-funding cap at S$330,000 per project as explicit policy commitments. Compute capacity was uniformly estimated at 500,000 GPU hours, and trainer personnel at 50, both based on typical national program scales and the need to support diverse sectors. No disagreements emerged; all participants aligned on the same numeric values and reasoning, emphasizing that these inputs must be sufficient to drive inclusive AI adoption without overstretching resources.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed the need to balance breadth and depth in activities to support the 100E programme, given resources of S$500 million total funding and 50 trainers. Consensus emerged that enterprise outreach should be targeted and sector-specific, with estimates ranging from 25 to 30 sessions, and that researcher matchmaking events should be carefully curated, with a range of 10 to 12. Disagreement centered on whether higher estimates (e.g., 30 outreach, 12 matchmaking, 20 workshops, 40 onboarding) might overstretch resources or lower ones (e.g., 25, 10, 15, 35) might under-serve sectors needing more support. The central tendency across participants was around 28 outreach sessions, 11 matchmaking events, 18 training workshops, and 38 onboarding assists, reflecting a middle ground that ensures both reach and quality.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group focused on resource constraints (S$500M funding, S$330K co-funding cap, 500K GPU hours, 50 trainers) and first-year caution as key drivers of outputs. There was consensus that not all approved projects would yield prototypes or deployed solutions within 2023, with estimates for prototypes (50-60) and enterprises with solutions (55-70) reflecting development timelines and quality focus. Disagreement centered on approval volume: some argued for higher numbers (75-80) based on outreach capacity, while others favored lower estimates (70) due to selective, quality-first approval processes. Training completions were consistently estimated at 100-120, with a central tendency around 100. Overall, numeric ranges were: approved projects 70-80, prototypes 50-60, trainees 100-120, enterprises with solutions 55-70.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group focused on the 100E programme's first-year outcomes, emphasizing that a quality-first approval process and development timelines (6-9 months) plus ethical validation and data governance constraints would limit both approvals and deployments. There was strong consensus on the AI practitioner pool reaching 12,000 (below the 15,000 target), 80 generative AI use cases (below the 100 target), and a 25% enterprise AI adoption rate, reflecting early-stage experimentation. Disagreement centered on the number of approved projects (range 70-80) and deployed projects (range 40-45), with some participants arguing that inclusive outreach could boost approvals, while others stressed that responsible AI compliance would slow deployment. The central tendency for approvals was 73-75, and for deployments, 40, with most estimates converging on the lower end.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached full consensus on all four variables, estimating Singapore's global AI readiness rank at 5th, AI sector GDP share at 3.5%, public AI trust at 55%, and AI talent retention at 70%. Key causal mechanisms included the S$500M investment and 100E programme's quality-first approach driving reputation and ethical governance, with 40-45 deployed projects limiting public exposure and trust, while 100-120 trainees and a 12,000-person practitioner pool supported retention despite global competition. No disagreements emerged, with all participants citing similar reasoning around early-stage economic impact and cautious public optimism.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached full consensus on all input variables, anchoring their estimates on explicit policy statements: total funding at S$500 million and co-funding cap at S$330,000 per project. For compute and training capacity, they uniformly estimated 500,000 GPU hours and 50 trainers, reasoning from the need to support concurrent experiments and the structure of the AI Apprenticeship Programme. No disagreements emerged, as all participants treated the policy figures as ground truth and aligned their qualitative reasoning on infrastructure and personnel needs. The central tendency and range for each variable were identical across all predictions.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed how the 100E programme's activities—outreach, matchmaking, training, and onboarding—are constrained by the available 50 trainers and the need to source 100 problem statements, with causal mechanisms emphasizing that enterprise hesitancy and the need for personalized engagement reduce the effectiveness of large-scale sessions. There was consensus that enterprise outreach sessions should be in the 30-40 range, matchmaking events between 8-12, training workshops 15-20, and platform onboarding assists 40-60, with all participants agreeing that trainer bandwidth and enterprise caution limit activity counts. Disagreement centered on the exact numbers: Dr. (u7u5yd) estimated higher figures (40, 12, 20, 60) based on operational capacity, while others (A (57gc0h), Ravi, I (yh9dgd), A (ezyulw)) argued for lower estimates (30-35, 8-10, 15-18, 40-50) due to real-world adoption friction and the need for one-on-one consultations. The central tendency across predictions is approximately 33 outreach sessions, 9 matchmaking events, 17 training workshops, and 48 onboarding assists.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed that the 100E programme's outputs are shaped by the interplay of funding, outreach activities, and real-world friction such as enterprise hesitancy, technical delays, and attrition. There was consensus that not all targeted 100 problem statements would be approved due to quality checks and due diligence dropouts, and that not all approved projects would yield prototypes or deployed solutions within the year. Disagreement centered on the approval rate and prototype yield: some participants estimated 75-80 approvals and 55-60 prototypes, while one participant estimated 85 approvals and 68 prototypes, reflecting differing views on operational efficiency and industry demand. The numeric predictions ranged from 75 to 85 for approved projects (central tendency ~78), 55 to 68 for prototypes (~60), 35 to 45 for trainees (~38), and 45 to 55 for enterprises with a solution (~48).
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed that real-world friction, such as enterprise hesitancy, due diligence dropouts, and integration delays, constrains outcomes from the 100E programme and AI Trailblazers, despite substantial funding and resources. There was consensus that the AI practitioner pool is approaching but below the 15,000 target (range 12,000–12,500) and that enterprise AI adoption is gradual (range 17.5%–18.5%). Disagreement centered on the number of approved and deployed 100E projects, with estimates ranging from 75 to 85 for approvals and 45 to 55 for deployments, and on generative AI use cases, ranging from 75 to 80. The central tendency for approvals was around 78, for deployments around 48, for generative use cases around 77, and for adoption rate around 17.8%.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that the 100E programme's impact is tempered by real-world friction, with enterprise adoption being gradual and talent mobility high. There was strong consensus on all estimates: global AI readiness rank improving to 6th, AI sector GDP share at 3.8%, public trust at 62%, and AI talent retention at 72%. No disagreements were expressed, with all participants agreeing on cautious realism and rejecting overly optimistic predictions. The numeric predictions were unanimous across all four variables.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all input variables, estimating total funding at S$500M, co-funding cap at S$330,000 per project, 500,000 GPU hours, and 50 trainers. They reasoned that these values align with explicit policy commitments (S$500M floor, S$330,000 cap) and typical national infrastructure (NSCC capacity) and a high-touch mentorship model. No disagreements emerged; all participants converged on the same numeric estimates and causal logic, viewing the inputs as a solid, focused foundation for industry AI experimentation under NAIS 2.0.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed how the high-touch 100E model, with substantial inputs (S$500M, 50 trainers), emphasizes quality over quantity, limiting the scale of activities. There was consensus that enterprise outreach and matchmaking events require careful curation, not mass outreach. Disagreement centered on the precise scale: estimates for outreach ranged from 100 to 150 sessions, matchmaking from 20 to 30 events, workshops from 25 to 40, and onboarding assists from 120 to 200. Central tendencies were around 120 outreach, 24 matchmaking, 30 workshops, and 150 onboarding assists.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group consistently estimated that the high-touch, quality-focused design of the 100E programme—characterized by rigorous selection, limited trainer capacity (50 trainers), and a co-funding cap of S$330K per project—would cap outputs at a moderate scale. There was strong consensus on all four output variables: 80 approved projects, 60 AI prototypes, 120 trainees completing the programme, and 70 enterprises with at least one AI solution. No disagreements emerged, as all participants reasoned that the intensive mentorship and selective pipeline would yield a 75% prototype success rate and that some enterprises would host multiple projects, reducing the unique enterprise count. The central tendency across all predictions was identical, reflecting a shared view that quality over quantity would define the outputs.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached a strong consensus that the high-touch, quality-focused 100E model would lead to 80 approved projects and 60 prototypes, but only about half (40) would be deployed in 2023 due to the 6-12 month validation and integration process required for production deployment. All participants estimated the AI practitioner pool at 12,000, short of the 15,000 target, and the generative AI use cases at 80 out of 100, citing enterprise readiness and problem scoping as limiting factors. Enterprise AI adoption was uniformly estimated at 25%, reflecting cautious SME adoption despite increased awareness from NAIS 2.0 and the 100E programme. No disagreements were expressed; all predictions and reasoning were aligned across the five participants.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached full consensus, estimating that Singapore's global AI readiness rank would improve to 5th, AI sector GDP share would reach 3.8%, public AI trust would be 65%, and AI talent retention would be 78%. All participants attributed these moderate but solid impacts to the quality-focused, conservative trajectory of the NAIS 2.0 programme, citing 40 deployed projects, 25% enterprise AI adoption, and strong infrastructure as key drivers. However, they noted that the small domestic market, limited programme scale, and global competition for talent prevented higher outcomes. No disagreements emerged, with all narratives aligning on the same causal mechanisms and numeric predictions.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached full consensus on all input variables, grounding their estimates in explicit policy statements and shared reasoning about resource allocation for the 100 Experiments programme. They unanimously accepted S$500 million total funding and S$330,000 maximum co-funding per project as factual anchors. For compute, all participants estimated 500,000 GPU hours, reasoning that this allocation supports 100 projects at roughly 5,000 hours each without over-provisioning. Similarly, they agreed on 50 trainers, citing the need for specialized mentorship and the scale of existing AI Singapore programmes. No disagreements emerged, as each participant independently derived the same values from their respective operational, research, or startup perspectives.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed causal mechanisms linking outreach, matchmaking, training, and onboarding to the target of 100 approved and deployed 100E projects, emphasizing that hands-on support is critical for adoption. There was strong consensus on platform onboarding assists (100, one per project) and general agreement on enterprise outreach sessions (20-25, with 20 as the central tendency) and researcher matchmaking events (10-12, with 10 as the central tendency). A minor disagreement emerged on the sufficiency of outreach and matchmaking: some argued that more sessions (25 and 12) are needed to ensure quality problem statements and effective pairings, while others maintained that 20 and 10 are adequate given the focused programme scope. Training workshops estimates ranged from 15 to 20, with 15 as the central tendency, reflecting differing views on how many sessions the 50 trainers can support.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group agreed that the 100E programme's explicit target of 100 approved projects would be met, given ample funding and resources. They converged on 80 AI prototypes developed, citing an 80% success rate due to technical and data challenges, and 300 trainees completing the programme, accounting for workshop capacity and attrition. A minor disagreement emerged on enterprises with solutions: most estimated 75, citing typical adoption lags, while one participant estimated 80 based on faster deployment in logistics. Overall, predictions ranged from 80-85 prototypes, 300 trainees, and 75-80 enterprises with solutions, with a central tendency of 80, 300, and 75 respectively.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group agreed that the 100E programme's explicit target of 100 approved projects would be met, given sufficient funding and co-funding caps. However, deployment to production was seen as significantly constrained by integration challenges, data quality issues, and enterprise change management, leading to a consensus estimate of 70 deployed projects (range 70-75). The AI practitioner pool was estimated at 11,000-12,000, falling short of the 15,000 target due to the time needed for scaling and training. For generative AI use cases, the group estimated 80-85 out of the 100-day target, citing practical constraints like data readiness and enterprise alignment. Enterprise AI adoption rate was estimated at 16-18.5%, reflecting early-stage uptake with many enterprises still in pilot phases. Disagreement centered on the deployment rate (70 vs 75) and adoption rate (16% vs 18.5%), driven by differing sector experiences (e.g., logistics vs healthcare) and the perceived impact of data privacy and compliance requirements.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus on all four impact variables, estimating Singapore's global AI readiness rank at 5, AI sector GDP share at 3.8%, public AI trust at 65%, and AI talent retention at 70%. Key causal mechanisms included the 100E programme's deployment of 70-75 projects, a growing AI practitioner pool of 11,000-12,000, and 80-85 generative AI use cases, which together drive improved global standing and economic contribution. Public trust is seen as cautiously optimistic, tempered by concerns over data privacy and algorithmic bias, especially in healthcare. Talent retention is supported by a growing local ecosystem but challenged by competition from higher salaries abroad. Minor variations in deployment counts (70 vs 75) did not affect the final estimates, reflecting a shared view of the programme's impact.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached full consensus on all input variables, treating the policy's explicit commitments as ground truth: total funding of S$500 million and a co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project. For compute, all participants estimated 500,000 GPU hours, reasoning that this level is typical for national AI programmes of this scale and sufficient to support dozens of experiments, especially for SMEs lacking their own infrastructure. Trainer personnel count was uniformly estimated at 50, based on the need to train 15,000 practitioners with a core team of experts. No disagreements emerged; the alignment was reinforced by shared references to the same policy documents and a common understanding of resource requirements for AI adoption and upskilling.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached full consensus on all activity estimates, with every participant predicting 200 enterprise outreach sessions, 30 researcher matchmaking events, 100 training workshops, and 150 platform onboarding assists. Key causal mechanisms included leveraging S$500 million funding and co-funding caps to attract SMEs, using 500,000 GPU hours for high-impact matchmaking, and scaling workshops to train 15,000 practitioners with 50 trainers. The alignment stemmed from shared assumptions about resource efficiency and the critical need for hands-on support, particularly for SME adoption of the AI platform.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group unanimously agreed that all 100 100E projects would be approved in 2023, citing the explicit policy target and sufficient funding. They also converged on an 80% prototype success rate due to technical challenges, 1,500 trainees completing the programme (10% of 15,000 participants), and 70 unique enterprises with solutions, reflecting some enterprises having multiple projects. No disagreements emerged, with all participants providing identical estimates and similar reasoning grounded in resource constraints and typical success rates. The central tendency for all four variables was fixed at 100, 80, 1500, and 70 respectively.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus that all 100 approved projects would generate the targeted 100 generative AI use cases, but deployment to production was seen as a major bottleneck. Most participants estimated 50 deployed projects, citing integration, data quality, and regulatory hurdles, though one researcher predicted a lower 40 due to additional SME resource constraints. The AI practitioner pool was consistently estimated at 12,000, short of the 15,000 target, as many trainees were still in the 9-month program. Enterprise AI adoption rate was uniformly placed at 25%, reflecting the slow pace of capability building among SMEs. The central tendency across all variables was 100 approved projects, 50 deployed (range 40-50), 12,000 practitioners, 100 use cases, and 25% adoption.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group agreed that NAIS 2.0's prior phases (100 approved projects, 50 deployments, 12,000 practitioners, 100 generative AI use cases) provide a strong foundation, but global competition limits Singapore's AI readiness rank to 5th. There was consensus on AI sector GDP share reaching 3.5%, driven by deployed projects and 25% enterprise adoption. Disagreement emerged on public trust (range 60-65%) and talent retention (range 65-70%), with lower estimates citing persistent SME skepticism about data privacy/bias and strong pull from US/European tech giants. Central tendency: rank 5, GDP share 3.5%, trust ~62%, retention ~67%.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all four input variables, grounding their estimates in explicit policy commitments: total funding at S$500 million and co-funding cap at S$330,000 per project were taken as stated facts. For compute, participants estimated 500,000 GPU hours as a modest initial allocation to balance industry demand and experiment scale, while trainer personnel count was set at 50, reflecting a realistic team size for quality guidance. No disagreements emerged; all participants aligned on the same numeric values and reasoning, citing policy documents and practical experience with AI adoption and skilling programs.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed how enterprise outreach, matchmaking, training, and onboarding activities drive the 100E programme's approval and deployment targets, emphasizing that quality of engagement is critical given limited trainers (50) and compute resources (500,000 GPU hours). There was consensus that personalized, small-group sessions are essential for identifying viable problem statements and ensuring effective researcher-enterprise pairings. Disagreement centered on the feasible scale of activities: estimates ranged from 50 to 80 outreach sessions, 12 to 20 matchmaking events, 20 to 30 training workshops, and 100 to 150 onboarding assists, with a central tendency around 60-70 outreach, 14-18 matchmaking, 22-28 workshops, and 110-140 assists. The divergence reflected differing views on whether to prioritize breadth (higher numbers) or depth (lower numbers) given resource constraints.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed that the 100E programme's rigorous approval process and the need for multiple enterprise touchpoints limit the number of approved projects, with estimates ranging from 22 to 30 (central tendency ~27). They agreed that prototype development is constrained by 6-9 month timelines and technical challenges, yielding 15-20 prototypes, and that trainee completion rates are lower than 90% due to mid-career work demands, with estimates from 280 to 400 (central tendency ~350). Consensus emerged that not all prototypes lead to deployed solutions, with enterprises having at least one solution estimated at 18-25 (central tendency ~21). Disagreement centered on the conversion rate from outreach to approved projects and the exact trainee completion rate, with some assuming higher rates based on mentoring experience and others citing lower rates from hands-on SME work.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group discussed that the 100E programme's rigorous approval process, requiring multiple enterprise touchpoints and a strict no-commercial-solution filter, limits the number of approved projects, while deployment into production is further constrained by integration challenges and data quality issues, resulting in only about half of prototypes being deployed within the same year. There was consensus that the AI practitioner pool is growing but remains below the 15,000 target, and that the AI Trailblazers programme's target of 100 generative AI use cases in 100 days is overly ambitious, with enterprises needing more time. Disagreement centered on the conversion rates from outreach to approved projects and from prototypes to deployed solutions, with some participants estimating higher numbers based on outreach efforts and others citing lower rates from resource-constrained settings. The range of predictions was: approved projects 22-28 (central tendency ~25), deployed projects 10-14 (~12), AI practitioner pool 11,000-12,000 (~11,750), generative AI use cases 70-80 (~75), and enterprise AI adoption rate 15%-18.5% (~17%).
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that NAIS 2.0 inputs (S$500M funding, 500,000 GPU hours, 50 trainers) led to modest early impacts: 22-28 approved 100E projects and 10-14 deployments. Consensus emerged that Singapore's global AI readiness rank improves to 7th-8th, AI's GDP share reaches 3.5%-3.8%, public trust in AI is 62%-68%, and AI talent retention is 68%-72%. Disagreement centered on the pace of trust-building and talent retention, with some participants citing slower progress in SMEs and social sectors, while others saw steady gains from hands-on training. Central tendencies were rank 7, GDP share 3.5%, trust 65%, and retention 70%.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all four input variables, treating the total funding of S$500 million and the co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project as explicit policy ground truths. They estimated 1 million GPU hours for compute capacity and 50 trainer personnel, reasoning that these allocations are typical for national AI programs of this scale and sufficient to support quality over quantity in early deployment. No disagreements emerged, as all participants independently converged on the same numeric values and causal logic linking these inputs to enabling industry and academic AI experimentation.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group consistently estimated 40 enterprise outreach sessions, 12 researcher matchmaking events, 20 training workshops, and 150 platform onboarding assists, all derived from the S$500 million funding and 1 million GPU hours. They reasoned that these activities would generate 200-300 problem statements to support 100 active projects, with each matchmaking event handling 8-10 pairings and each workshop training 20-30 participants. There was full consensus on all estimates and underlying logic, with no disagreements. The central tendency and range for each variable were identical across all participants.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached strong consensus that the 100E programme would approve 100 projects in 2023, aligning with its name and supported by S$500 million funding and outreach activities. They agreed that not all approved projects would yield working prototypes due to technical challenges like data quality and integration, with estimates for prototypes developed ranging from 75 to 80 (central tendency 75). For trainees completing the programme, estimates converged on 140 to 150 (central tendency 140), accounting for a 9-month duration and typical dropout rates. Enterprises with at least one AI solution were estimated at 85 to 90 (central tendency 85), reflecting that some prototypes may not be deployed in the first year. The key causal mechanism was that resource inputs and outreach generate a pipeline of projects, but technical hurdles and deployment delays reduce the number of prototypes and enterprise solutions realized within the year.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus that the 100E programme would approve 100 projects in 2023, aligning with its name and S$500M funding, but only about 60% (60 projects) would deploy into production due to data integration and organizational readiness hurdles. All participants estimated the AI practitioner pool at 12,000 (below the 15,000 target), generative AI use cases at 80 (short of the 100 goal), and enterprise AI adoption at 12.5%, reflecting first-year ramp-up constraints and SME readiness gaps. No disagreements emerged; reasoning consistently cited technical challenges, training scaling delays, and tight timelines as key causal mechanisms limiting outcomes.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that the first year of NAIS 2.0 would lay a foundation without transformative impact, with all participants estimating Singapore's global AI readiness rank at 5, AI sector GDP share at 3.5%, public AI trust at 55%, and AI talent retention at 70%. Key causal mechanisms included the 60 deployed 100E projects and 80 generative AI use cases driving modest economic and trust gains, while persistent concerns about bias and global competition for talent limited higher scores. No disagreements emerged; all narratives emphasized that one year is insufficient for major shifts in rankings or trust, and that the apprenticeship programme and research ecosystem supported retention but could not fully counter global tech hub attraction. The central tendency across all four variables was identical across all five participants, reflecting unanimous alignment on early-stage, moderate progress.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached full consensus on all input variables, anchoring total funding at S$500 million and co-funding at S$330,000 per project based on explicit policy statements. Compute capacity was uniformly estimated at 500,000 GPU hours, justified by typical allocations for 100 experiments requiring roughly 5,000 hours each. Trainer personnel were consistently set at 150, derived from a 1:1.5 trainer-to-experiment ratio to ensure effective hands-on guidance. No disagreements emerged, as all participants aligned their reasoning on the same policy anchors and practical experience. The central tendency and range for each variable were identical: total_funding_sgd = 500,000,000; max_co_funding_per_project_sgd = 330,000; available_gpu_hours = 500,000; trainer_personnel_count = 150.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group focused on how to translate S$500 million funding and 150 trainers into activities that drive 100E project approvals and deployments. There was strong consensus that platform onboarding assists should match the 100-experiment target, with all participants estimating exactly 100. For other activities, estimates converged around moderate numbers: enterprise outreach sessions ranged from 40 to 50 (central tendency ~45), researcher matchmaking events from 15 to 20 (~18), and training workshops from 25 to 30 (~28). Disagreements centered on the pace of engagement, with some favoring more aggressive outreach and matchmaking to maximize volume, while others emphasized quality interactions and careful pairing to ensure viable problem statements and successful matches.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group consistently estimated that rigorous selection criteria for the 100E programme would approve around 75 projects (range 75-80), with technical challenges and iterative development reducing successful prototypes to about 55 (range 55-60). They agreed that the AI Apprenticeship Programme, with 150 trainers and 28 workshops, would produce approximately 130 trainees (range 120-130), and that roughly 65 enterprises (range 65-70) would have a deployable solution due to integration hurdles. Key causal mechanisms included the filtering of proposals by viability and enterprise AI maturity, the inherent uncertainty in prototype development, and the gap between prototype completion and production deployment. Minor divergence centered on whether approvals would be 75 or 80, and whether trainees would be 120 or 130, reflecting different assumptions about pipeline attrition and training capacity.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group broadly agreed that rigorous selection and technical challenges would limit 100E project approvals to 75-80 and deployments to 50-55, with deployment hindered by integration hurdles and responsible AI validation. Consensus emerged on an AI practitioner pool of 14,000-14,500 (below the 15,000 target) and generative AI use cases of 90-95 (slightly below the 100 target), driven by enterprise learning curves and training program outputs. Enterprise AI adoption was estimated at 28-30%, reflecting gradual uptake among small firms. Minor disagreements centered on deployment counts (50 vs. 55) and adoption rates (28% vs. 30%), with one participant citing responsible AI practices as enabling smoother deployment and higher adoption.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that the 100E programme and AI Apprenticeship Programme would yield gradual, steady progress across all four impact metrics. All participants estimated Singapore's global AI readiness rank at 6th, AI sector GDP share at 3.8%, public AI trust at 72%, and AI talent retention at 85%, with no variation in numeric predictions. Key causal mechanisms included strong government infrastructure and talent development driving readiness, deployed 100E projects and generative AI use cases contributing to GDP, responsible AI practices boosting trust, and hands-on training retaining talent. However, participants consistently noted that competition from larger economies, early-stage scaling of solutions, lingering concerns about job displacement and bias, and lures from global tech giants prevented higher scores. No disagreements emerged, as all narratives and evidence aligned closely.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all four input variables, with every participant estimating total_funding_sgd at 500,000,000, max_co_funding_per_project_sgd at 330,000, available_gpu_hours at 500,000, and trainer_personnel_count at 50. No disagreements emerged; all participants anchored their estimates on the same policy figures and reasoning about typical national AI programme allocations and quality-focused training models.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached strong consensus on all activity estimates, with every participant predicting 40 enterprise outreach sessions, 12 researcher matchmaking events, 30 training workshops, and 100 platform onboarding assists. The reasoning consistently cited the S$500M funding, 50 trainers, and 500,000 GPU hours as enabling a weekly outreach cadence over 10 months, monthly matchmaking, and multiple onboarding sessions per enterprise. No disagreements emerged, as all participants aligned on the same resource-driven logic and pacing. The central tendency and range for each variable were identical across all predictions.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group reached strong consensus that the 100E programme's outputs are constrained by upstream activities, with all participants estimating 80 approved projects, 60 prototypes, 120 trainees, and 50 enterprises with solutions. They identified key causal mechanisms: outreach and matchmaking events limit the pipeline of quality problem statements to about 80, data and integration issues cause roughly 25% of approved projects to stall, and the AI Apprenticeship Programme's cohort structure and trainer capacity cap completions at 120. No disagreements emerged, as all estimates and reasoning aligned, reflecting a shared understanding of real-world constraints. The central tendency across all predictions was 80 approved projects, 60 prototypes, 120 trainees, and 50 enterprises, with no variation in numeric values.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus on all outcome estimates, predicting 80 approved 100E projects, 40 deployed projects (half of the 60 prototypes), an AI practitioner pool of 12,000 (short of the 15,000 target), 100 generative AI use cases (treated as a policy commitment), and a 25% enterprise AI adoption rate. The central causal mechanism was that real-world integration, data quality, and change management issues typically cause about half of prototypes to stall before production deployment. The practitioner pool was seen as growing incrementally from the 120 trainees and other upskilling efforts, while enterprise adoption was viewed as still in early experimentation, with larger firms ahead of SMEs. No disagreements emerged; all participants independently arrived at the same estimates based on hands-on experience with AI projects and policy implementation.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached full consensus that the 100E programme's 40 deployed projects and 50 enterprises with solutions provide tangible but modest AI adoption, leading to a global AI readiness rank of 8 (up from ~10-12) and an AI sector GDP share of 4.5%, constrained by fierce global competition and early-stage scaling across sectors. Public trust in AI systems was uniformly estimated at 65%, reflecting persistent skepticism despite governance efforts and insufficient critical mass of deployed projects to shift perceptions. AI talent retention was consistently pegged at 85%, attributed to hands-on experience and career growth from the 100E programme and AIAP. No disagreements emerged; all participants independently converged on identical numeric predictions and causal reasoning, emphasizing the programme's solid yet limited impact relative to economy-wide transformation.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group agreed that the total funding for AI R&D is S$500 million, a multi-year commitment fully allocated for 2023, and the maximum co-funding per 100E project is S$330,000. For compute capacity, estimates ranged from 800,000 to 1,000,000 GPU hours, with a central tendency around 900,000 hours, reflecting moderate initial scaling and operational constraints. Trainer personnel estimates varied from 40 to 50, centering on 45, based on a lean but effective core team for hands-on guidance. Disagreements centered on the realism of initial compute deployment and trainer staffing levels, with some participants assuming more conservative operational realities.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed how the lean team of 40-45 trainers constrains the scale of activities, emphasizing quality over quantity for enterprise outreach and matchmaking due to the 100E programme's requirement for problem statements with no off-the-shelf solution. There was consensus on the need for targeted engagement and that trainer capacity limits all activities, with most participants estimating 90-100 outreach sessions, 18-20 matchmaking events, 45-50 training workshops, and 135-150 onboarding assists. Disagreement centered on whether the team could achieve higher numbers (e.g., 120 outreach sessions, 25 matchmaking events, 60 workshops, 200 assists) as proposed by one participant, with others arguing these were unrealistic given the lean team and other duties. The central tendency across estimates was approximately 90-100 outreach sessions, 18-20 matchmaking events, 45-50 workshops, and 135-150 onboarding assists, reflecting a conservative but practical view of capacity.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group consistently estimated that operational constraints, such as a lean team of 45 trainers and the need for high-quality problem statements, would limit the number of approved 100E projects to about 60 in 2023, despite a large total funding pool. They agreed that approximately 45 AI prototypes would be developed within the year, as each project takes 6-9 months and not all complete a prototype. The consensus was that around 120 trainees would complete the 9-month AI Apprenticeship Programme, based on each trainer mentoring 3-4 trainees. Finally, they estimated that about 40 enterprises would have a deployed AI solution by year-end, citing that roughly two-thirds of prototypes reach production. All participants converged on the same numeric predictions, with no disagreement on estimates or reasoning.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus on all outcome estimates, with unanimous agreement that 60 100E projects were approved and 40 were deployed in 2023, based on a consistent reasoning that about two-thirds of prototypes reach production. All participants estimated the AI practitioner pool at 15,000, generative AI use cases at 100, and enterprise AI adoption rate at 12.5%, citing explicit policy targets and the small fraction of enterprises with solutions relative to Singapore's total base. Key causal mechanisms included the constraint of a lean trainer team on project approvals, the conversion rate from prototypes to deployed projects, and the role of the AI Trailblazers programme in meeting generative AI targets. No disagreements emerged, with all estimates identical across the five participants.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group discussed that the 100E programme's outputs (40 deployed projects, 100 generative AI use cases) are early-stage and have not yet significantly boosted Singapore's global AI readiness rank or GDP share, with most participants estimating a rank of 6 and GDP share of 2.8% due to intense global competition and small market size. Consensus emerged that public trust in AI is moderate at 60%, constrained by skepticism about job displacement and data privacy, and that AI talent retention is around 75%, as top talent is attracted abroad despite strong local programmes. A single dissenting view from an AI researcher estimated a higher rank of 5, GDP share of 3.5%, trust of 65%, and retention of 80%, citing stronger governance and ecosystem effects. The overall numeric ranges were: global_ai_readiness_rank 5-6, ai_sector_gdp_share 2.8-3.5%, public_ai_trust_score 60-65%, and ai_talent_retention_rate 75-80%.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all input estimates, drawing directly from stated policy commitments: total funding of S$500 million, a co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project, 500,000 GPU hours for compute, and 50 trainers. Participants consistently cited the same policy documents and their practical experience with AI Singapore's 100E programme to justify these numbers. No disagreements emerged, as all six stakeholders aligned on both the values and the reasoning. The central tendency is uniform across all variables, with no variation in the estimates.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group reached strong consensus on activity estimates, with all participants predicting 40 enterprise outreach sessions, 12 researcher matchmaking events, 30 training workshops, and 100 platform onboarding assists. Their reasoning centered on leveraging 50 trainers and S$500 million funding to sustain a monthly cadence of outreach and matchmaking, while training workshops and onboarding assists were scaled to match the expected number of projects and teams. No disagreements emerged, as all narratives cited similar experiences with CTO-as-a-Service and the 100E programme to justify the same numeric values.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group consistently estimated that the 100E programme would approve 50 projects in 2023, based on the S$500M funding and S$330K co-funding cap, with typical project costs of S$200K-300K. They projected 40 AI prototypes would be developed (80% success rate from approval to prototype), 30 trainees would complete the AIAP programme (two cohorts of 10-15 each), and 35 enterprises would have at least one AI solution (70% of approved enterprises). All participants agreed on these numbers and the underlying causal mechanisms, such as constraints from GPU hours and trainer capacity, with no disagreement in estimates or reasoning.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group consistently estimated 50 approved 100E projects, 30 deployed projects, 12,000 AI practitioners, 80 generative AI use cases, and a 25% enterprise AI adoption rate. Consensus centered on the gap between prototypes and production due to integration, data pipeline, and operationalization challenges, as well as the ambitious nature of the 15,000 practitioner target and the 100 generative AI use cases goal, with practical constraints like enterprise readiness and compute access limiting outcomes. No disagreements emerged; all participants independently converged on the same numeric estimates and causal reasoning.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus on all four variables, with every participant estimating a global AI readiness rank of 5, AI sector GDP share of 3.5%, public AI trust score of 65%, and AI talent retention rate of 85%. Key themes included Singapore's strong policy foundation and ecosystem (e.g., 50 approved 100E projects, 30 deployed, 12,000 practitioners) being offset by the larger scale of the US and China, leading to a sustained rank of 5. Causal mechanisms highlighted that moderate public trust (65%) stems from tech-savviness balanced by data privacy and bias concerns, while high talent retention (85%) is driven by competitive opportunities and quality of life, though some outflow to larger markets persists. No disagreements were expressed; participants independently arrived at identical estimates, reinforcing confidence in the projections.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group largely agreed on the government's S$500 million funding commitment and the S$330,000 co-funding cap per 100E project, treating these as ground truth. There was consensus on 500,000 GPU hours as a plausible compute allocation, though some noted it could be stretched thin. Disagreement centered on trainer personnel count, with estimates ranging from 40 to 50, reflecting differing views on talent scarcity and practical constraints. The central tendency for trainer count was around 40-50, with a median of 45.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group identified the number of trainers (estimated between 40 and 50) as the primary constraint on activity levels, with enterprise outreach sessions (range 90-120, central tendency ~100) and platform onboarding assists (range 120-200, central tendency ~150) expected to be the highest-volume activities. There was consensus that matchmaking events (range 10-15, central tendency ~12) and training workshops (range 25-40, central tendency ~30) would be fewer due to their resource-intensive nature and need for careful curation. Disagreement centered on the efficiency of trainers, with some participants assuming higher throughput (e.g., 120 outreach sessions, 40 workshops) while others, citing firsthand experience with stretched resources, argued for lower estimates (e.g., 90 outreach sessions, 25 workshops).
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group identified trainer capacity as the primary bottleneck limiting outputs, with a consensus that approximately 75 of the 100E projects would be approved in 2023 due to selective curation and the S$330,000 co-funding cap. They agreed on a prototype success rate of 67-75% from approved projects, yielding 50-60 prototypes, and estimated 100-120 trainees would complete the programme based on a 60-70% completion rate from workshops. The number of enterprises with at least one AI solution was projected at 60-70, reflecting deployment challenges. Key disagreements centered on the exact number of approved projects (75 vs. 80) and the prototype success rate (67% vs. 75%), with most participants converging on 75 approvals, 55 prototypes, 110 completions, and 65 enterprises.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group focused on the gap between approved projects and production deployment, citing integration challenges, data quality, and ethical compliance as key bottlenecks. There was strong consensus that the AI practitioner pool would reach about 11,500, generative AI use cases around 70, and enterprise adoption near 10%, with most participants estimating 75 approved projects and 45 deployed. A single outlier predicted 80 approvals, 50 deployments, a 12,000 practitioner pool, 80 use cases, and 12.5% adoption, reflecting a more optimistic view on deployment rates and practitioner counting. Overall, predictions ranged from 75–80 approved projects, 45–50 deployed, 11,500–12,000 practitioners, 70–80 use cases, and 10%–12.5% adoption.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that the 100E programme's outcomes—45-50 deployed projects, 11,500-12,000 practitioners, 70-80 generative AI use cases, and 10-12.5% enterprise adoption—represent solid but early-stage progress, not transformative change in one year. All participants estimated identical values: global AI readiness rank improving from ~10th to 8th, AI sector GDP share rising to 3.2%, public AI trust reaching 62%, and AI talent retention at 78%. Key causal mechanisms included the coordinated NAIS 2.0 push and visible public-sector deployments boosting readiness and trust, while the SME-heavy economy and competitive global salaries for talent limited faster scaling and retention. No disagreements emerged; all narratives cited the same evidence and reasoning, with later participants explicitly aligning with the initial consensus.

phase_summaries.Inputs: The group reached strong consensus on all input estimates, with every participant predicting S$500 million total funding, S$330,000 max co-funding per project, 500,000 GPU hours, and 50 trainers, citing explicit policy statements and typical national programme scales. Key themes included the importance of these inputs for de-risking enterprise AI adoption, upskilling the workforce, and supporting 50-100 projects with moderate compute needs. However, several participants expressed skepticism about execution efficiency, noting potential bureaucratic delays, insufficient compute under peak demand, and trainers being stretched thin, which could reduce effective capacity. No numeric disagreements emerged, but the group highlighted that these inputs are only a starting point and that bottlenecks in implementation may limit actual project approvals and deployments.
phase_summaries.Activities: The group discussed that the S$500 million funding and 50 trainers are strong enablers for the 100E programme, but execution bottlenecks such as preparation time, bureaucratic delays, and trainer fatigue will limit activity levels. There was consensus that enterprise outreach sessions and matchmaking events should be roughly paired at a 2:1 ratio, and that training workshops are constrained by the need for 2-3 trainers per workshop. Disagreement centered on the degree of optimism: some participants estimated higher numbers (e.g., 30 outreach sessions, 20 workshops) while others were more conservative (e.g., 25 outreach sessions, 15 workshops), citing practical constraints like trainer stretch and prior enterprise cloud experience. The central tendency across estimates was approximately 28 outreach sessions, 14 matchmaking events, 18 workshops, and 90 platform onboarding assists, with ranges of 25-30, 12-15, 15-20, and 80-100 respectively.
phase_summaries.Outputs: The group discussed that strong inputs (S$500M funding, 50 trainers, 500K GPU hours) are enabling, but execution bottlenecks—bureaucratic delays, careful curation, technical challenges, and high attrition in the 9-month training programme—will reduce outputs. There was consensus on estimates for approved projects (55), prototypes developed (40), and enterprises with a solution (45), with most participants converging on these values. A single participant estimated higher numbers (60 approved, 45 prototypes, 120 trainees, 50 enterprises), but others considered this optimistic. The range for approved projects was 55-60, prototypes 40-45, trainees 100-120, and enterprises 45-50, with central tendencies of 55, 40, 100, and 45 respectively.
phase_summaries.Outcomes: The group reached strong consensus that execution bottlenecks—such as integration challenges, data pipeline issues, and lack of sustained enterprise commitment—significantly limit the translation of approved projects into production deployments, with all participants estimating only 30 of 55-60 approved projects would be deployed in 2023. There was also broad agreement on the AI practitioner pool being around 12,000 (short of the 15,000 target), generative AI use cases reaching about 80 of the 100 target, and enterprise AI adoption rate at 25%. The only minor divergence was on the number of approved projects: most estimated 55, while one participant estimated 60, citing sufficient outreach and matchmaking activities. Overall, the group's pragmatic view highlighted that while policy inputs are strong, real-world friction and enterprise readiness constraints temper near-term outcomes.
phase_summaries.Impact: The group reached strong consensus that Singapore's global AI readiness rank would improve from 6th to 5th, the AI sector's GDP share would grow from 3.5% to 4.5%, public trust in AI would reach 65%, and AI talent retention would be 75%. All participants cited the same causal mechanisms: strong policy foundations (NAIS 2.0), 30 deployed projects, 80 generative AI use cases, and 25% enterprise adoption as drivers, tempered by global competition, execution bottlenecks (bureaucratic delays, integration challenges), and persistent concerns about job displacement and data privacy. No disagreements were expressed; estimates were identical across all five participants, reflecting a shared pragmatic view that real-world friction and global talent competition limit the impact of Singapore's investments.
